name: HMS Umbra (P 35)
events: The history of HMS P 35 / HMS Umbra as compiled on this page is extracted from this submarine's patrol reports and logbooks and was created in February 2014. Corrections and details regarding information from the enemy's side (for instance the composition of convoys attacked) is kindly provided by Mr. Platon Alexiades, a naval researcher from Canada.
This page was last updated in March 2026.
1 Sep 1941 P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed her builders yard at Barrow for Holy Loch. She was escorted by HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR). (1)
2 Sep 1941 At 1420 A/2, P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) arrived at Holy Loch to begin a period of trials and training. [No log is available so no further details can be given.]
En-route from Barrow acceptance trials had been carried out and the submarine was taken over from the builder and commissioned in full on arrival at Holy Loch. (1)

24 Sep 1941 At 2000 A/24, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN), which had completed her work-up period, departed Holy Loch for Dundee. She was escorted by HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR). (1)
27 Sep 1941 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) arrived at Dundee from Holy Loch to join the 9th Submarine Flotilla. (1)

30 Sep 1941 Around 1400 A/30, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Dundee for Scapa Flow. She made the passage together with HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) that was to proceed to Malta via Gibraltar. They were escorted by HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN). (1)
1 Oct 1941 Around 1145 A/1, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) arrived at Scapa Flow to participate in A/S exercises.
[As no logs are available for HMS P 35 before June 1942 no details can be given for these exercises.]

[According to the logbook of HMS Tyne exercises may have been carried out on the following days during the month of October 1941; 2, 3, 6, 8 and 9 (possibly including night exercises), 11, 13, 14, 15, 17, 18 to 21 (possibly including night exercises), 22, 23, 25, 26 and 27 (possibly including night exercises).] (2)
15 Oct 1941 In the morning HMS London (Capt. R.M. Servaes, CBE, RN) conducted A/S exercises at Scapa Flow with HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN).
In the late afternoon, HMS London conducted a bombardment exercises with the 8" main battery. (3)
28 Oct 1941 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Scapa Flow for Dundee. She was escorted by HMS Leicester City (Lt. F.A. Everett, RNR). (1)
29 Oct 1941 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) arrived at Dundee from Scapa Flow. (1)
30 Oct 1941 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Dundee for Scapa Flow. She was escorted by HMS Equerry (T/Lt. H.C. Hutchinson, RNVR). (1)
31 Oct 1941 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) arrived at Scapa Flow from Dundee. (1)
2 Nov 1941 At 0735 A/2, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Scapa Flow for her 1st war patrol. She was ordered to patrol off the Norwegian coast near Bergen.
She was escorted until 2359 A/2, by HMS Leicester City (Lt. F.A. Everett, RNR) (4)
2 Nov 1941 For the daily positions of HMS P 35 during her 1st war patrol see the map below.

3 Nov 1941 At 1946 B/3, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) received the Admiral Submarines signal timed 1836 A/3, in which she was ordered to patrol in position 59°18'N, 04°23'E (west of Utsira) following information that the pocket battleship Admiral Scheer and perhaps a raider from the Baltic may have sailed. Course was set to comply with these orders. (5)
5 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1241 A/5, the Admiral Submarines ordered HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) to patrol in the vicinity of a line joining positions 58°30'N, 05°16'E and 57°56'N, 06°21'E. (6)
6 Nov 1941 At 0332 A/6, to the south-west of Utsira, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) sighted two vessels, possibly trawlers. These were almost immediately lost from sight in the rain squalls.
[We have been unable to identify these vessels, which may have been small fishing vessels.]
At 0340 A/6, HMS P 35 dived in position 59°06'N, 04°23'E to listed for HE but nothing was heard.
At 0425 A/6, course was altered to proceeded to the end of the patrol line ordered in the Admiral Submarines signal timed 1241 A/5.
At 1900 A/6, HMS P 35 surfaced north-west of Obrestad in position 58°46'N, 05°08'E.
At 1956 A/6, a white light was sighted bearing 060°. HMS P 35 set off in pursuit.
At 2052 A/6, the chase was abandoned as the light appeared to be coming from a very small vessel, most likely a fishing vessel.
At 2359 A/6, HMS P 35 arrived on the ordered patrol line. (4)

7 Nov 1941 At 0608 A/7, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) dived to the west-south-west of Egersund, in position 58°21'N, 05°32'E. Weather conditions made it difficult to keep an effective periscope patrol.

At 1627 A/7, the Admiral Submarines signal timed 1416 A/7 was received which stated the following; Merchant ship of about 3000 tons was sighted by aircraft in position 58°00'N, 06°38'E [south-south-east of Lister], course 300°, speed 8 knots at 1240 A/7.
Course was altered to close the likely track of this ship.
At 1855 A/7, HMS P 35 surfaced in position 58°08'N, 06°01'E (west of Lister). Weather conditions were still bad.
The reported ship was not sighted. (5)
9 Nov 1941 At 1858 A/9, after a day of submerged patrol between Lister and Egersund, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) surfaced in position 58°15'N, 05°58'E.

At 2111 A/9, the C-in-C Rosyth's signal timed 1538 A/9 was received which contained the following information; Aircraft reports twelve merchant ships 270° - Listergle, steering 270° at 5 knots at 1520 A/9.
This may have referred to two westbound convoys which had departed Kristiansand (South) in the morning.

At 0735 B/9, a convoy made up of the transports Halse (Norwegian (under German control), 2136 GRT, built 1910), Hans Bornhofen (German, 2130 GRT, built 1905) and Hinrich Peters (German, 1052 GRT, built 1908) had departed Kristiansand (South) for Stavanger where they arrived around 1510 B/10. They were escorted by the auxiliary minesweepers M 1104 and M 1106.

At 0900 B/9, a convoy made up of the transports Ditmar Koel (German (former Russian Tallin, 4480 GRT, built 1941), Gouwe (Dutch (under German control) 2119 GRT, built 1927), Harvestehude (German, former French Ville de Reims, 4683 GRT, built 1918) and Karl Leonhardt (German, 6042 GRT, built 1913) had departed Kristiansand (South) for Stavanger where they arrived around 1555 B/10. They were escorted by the auxiliary minesweepers M 1107 and M 1109.

Course was altered to 330° and increased speed so HMS P 35 would be in a good position to intercept at midnight when the moon would be favourable. It was estimated the convoy could be intercepted near Obrestad.
Nothing was however sighted despite good visibility. (5)

12 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1713 A/12, the Capt. S.9 sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Leave patrol after dark 14th for Dundee diving by day. [Routing was also included in the signal but we have been unable to find the exact text of the signal.]

In a signal timed 1652 A/12, the Admiral Submarines sent the following orders to HMS P 35; In addition to cruisers and above and northbound merchant ships with them you may now attack any large merchant ship whether escorted or not and irrespective of its course. You must however reserve at least 4 torpedoes for attacks on cruisers or above. (6)

14 Nov 1941 At 2132 A/14, after a day of submerged patrol between Lister and Obrestad HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) surfaced in position 58°35'N, 05°29'E and left patrol in accordance with the Capt. S.9's signal timed 1713 A/12. (4)
19 Nov 1941 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) ended her 1st war patrol at Dundee. (4)

7 Dec 1941 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Dundee for Gibraltar. She was escorted until west of Bishops Rock by HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR). They were expected to part company around 0100 A/12.

Thence HMS P 35 was to dive by day to position 48°00'N, 08°00'W and position 46°00'N, 12°40'W. Thence on the surface at the Commanding Officers discretion to position 43°00'N, 12°40'W. ETA in this last position at 1600 A/15. Thence as ordered by F.O.C.N.A. (Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic).
[As no log of HMS P 35 is available for this period and as there is also no passage report it is not possible to display a map of the passage to Gibraltar or to give details of this passage.] (4)

14 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1618 A/14, the Admiral Submarines sent the following to HMS Regent (Lt. W.N.R. Knox, DSC, RN), HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Keep a good look out for any heavy enemy warships which may be taking advantage of weather to break out from Brest to the south-westward.

In a follow up signal timed 1949 A/14, the Admiral Submarines sent the following orders to HMS Regent; My 1618 A/14. Proceed to patrol in the vicinity of position 46°20'N, 09°00'W for 24 hours continuing your passage to Portsmouth after dark tomorrow. (7)
14 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1106 A/14, F.O.C.N.A. (Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic) sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) and HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN).
(1) Report position, course and speed of advance on crossing 43°00'N.

(2) HMS P 35 onward route through the following positions; Position L = 43°00'N, 12°40'W Position M = 36°30'N, 10°00'W Thence to R/V with escort in position 350° - Cape Spartel - 5 miles Dive by day on the 17th and 18th

(3) HMS Una onward route through the following positions; Position N = 43°00'N, 10°50'W Position O = 40°00'N, 11°00'W Position P = 37°30'N, 11°00'W Position Q = 36°30'N, 10°00'W Thence to R/V with escort in position 350° - Cape Spartel - 5 miles Dive by day on the 17th (7)

16 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 0131 A/16, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) sent the following to the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.); My position 43°00'N, 12°41'W, course 163°, speed on surface 8 knots, submerged 3 knots.

In a signal timed 1108 A/16, F.O.C.N.A. sent the following orders to HMS P 35; Amend onward route from present position passing through position 'R, 36°45'N, 11°45'W at 0700 A/18 thence direct to arrive in position 350° - Cape Spartel - 3 miles at 1200 A/20. Diving by day on the 18th and 19th. Bombing restrictions are arranged. (7)

18 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 2336 A/18, F.O.C.N.A. (Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic) informed HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) that HMS Blankney (Lt.Cdr. P.F. Powlett, DSC, RN) and HMS Exmoor (Lt.Cdr. L.StG. Rich, RN) would pass through position 36°14'N, 14°40'W at 1430 A/18 to Gibraltar at 18 knots. They were also informed that the destroyer were aware of the submarines presence. (7)

19 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1806 A/19, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); My 1108 A/16. Your escort is HMS Heliotrope (Lt.Cdr. J. Jackson, RNR). Report if unable to comply within one hour. Several U-Boats are operating in the western approaches to the Straits of Gibraltar. (7)
20 Dec 1941 Around 1625 A/20, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) arrived at Gibraltar from Dundee. (8)
27 Dec 1941 Around 0815 A/27, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Gibraltar for Malta.
She was to pass through position 38°20'N, 09°00'E about 0300 A/29, thence as ordered by the Vice-Admiral Malta.
[As no log is available for this period it is not possible to display a map of this passage. Also no passage report is available.] (9)

29 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1131 B/29 and later amended in a signal timed 2045 B/29, the Vice-Admiral Malta ordered HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) to proceed through the following positions; B = 37°54'N, 11°53'E C = 37°20'N, 12°50'E D = 37°10'N, 13°12'E E = 36°10'N, 14°00'E Una was also to adjust speed to proceed submerged between positions B and C during daylight of 2 January and thence by the south-east searched channels to arrive Marsamxett harbour, Malta before 1800 B/3 making an E.T.A. signal when east of 14°E.
In the same signal HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) was ordered to follow the same route as HMS Una but 24 hours later. (7)

1 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 1450 B/1, Capt. S.10 referring the the Vice-Admiral Malta's signal timed 1131 B/29 informed HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) of the following; Owing to enemy fighter activity during daylight HMS P 35 was now to arrive off Marsamxett harbour at 0800 B/5. She was also informed that submarines encountered on opposite courses during nights of 2nd/3rd and 3rd/4th were not to be attacked as HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) might be encountered. (10)

2 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 1655 B/2, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) of the following; The sailing of HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) has been postponed by 24 hours. (10)

3 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 1049 B/3, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) of the following; HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) and HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) leave the end of the south east searched channel, about 2200 B/3 and 2200 B/4 respectively.
HMS Una and HMS P 35 were to keep to the westward of 14°10'E until 0001 B/4 for Una and 0001 B/5 for HMS P 35. Submarines encountered south of 36°30'N must not be attacked. (10)
5 Jan 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) arrived at Malta. (11)

11 Jan 1942 At 1515 B/11, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Malta for her 2nd war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the vicinity of position 34°50'N, 12°25'E (well to the south of Lampedusa).
On departure exercises were carried out with HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) which departed around the same time. The submarines parted company around 1735 B/11. (12)
11 Jan 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 35 during her 2nd war patrol see the map below.
12 Jan 1942 At 1600 B/12, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) arrived in position 34°50'N, 12°25'E and commenced patrolling near this position to intercept the expected northbound enemy convoy.

At 2155 B/12, HMS P 35 received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1801 B/12 ordering her to patrol in the vicinity of position 34°48'N, 11°54'E (east of Kerkennah). Course was set to comply with these orders.

This positioning of HMS P 35 was to intercept the Italian transport Monginevro (5324 GRT, built 1940) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Castore which were known by an ULTRA intercept (DEFE3/748/1/frame 280) of 11 January 1942 to be sailing from Tripoli at 1430 B/12 for Naples through position 318° - Tripoli - 20 miles then through positions 35°20'N, 12°20'E at 0230 B/13 and 36°11'N, 11°50'E, then a point south-west of Pantelleria at 0930 B/13, off Marittimo at 1345 B/13, off Cape San Vito and then direct to Naples, ETA 0530 B/14. The Italian transport Monviso (5322 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Procione would sail one hour later at same speed and would follow the same route. An intercept of 1845 GMT/11 (DEFE3/748/1/frame 303) corrected that Monviso was to be diverted to Palermo and then to continue to Taranto. A third intercept from 0215 GMT/12 (DEFE3/748/1/frame 308) stated that the departure of Monginevro and Monviso was delayed by 24 hours. However, a new intercept of 0305 GMT/13 indicated that Monviso and escort would again be going to Naples instead of Taranto (DEFE3/748/1/frame 326).

The departure of Monginevro and Monviso was confirmed by another intercept at 0015 GMT/14 (DEFE3/748/1/ frame 347). Monginevro escorted by Castore sailed from Tripoli at 1519 B/13. The destroyer Maestrale sailed from Palermo at 1052 B/14 and at, 1635 B/14, off Cape San Vito, joined the convoy. She relieved Castore which was directed to Messina where she arrived at 0126 B/15. Monginevro and Maestrale arrived at Naples at 0540 B/15. Monviso escorted by Procione sailed from Tripoli at 1630 B/13 and arrived at Naples at 0630 B/15. (12)
13 Jan 1942 At 0200 B/13, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) arrived in position 34°48'N, 11°54'E and commenced to patrol in the area.
At 2132 B/13, HMS P 35 received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1705 B/13, ordering her to leave her present patrol area at 0300 B/14 to patrol off Kuriat.

13 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 1137 B/13, the Vice-Admiral Malta informed HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) and HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) that an aircraft had reported six medium sized merchant vessels escorted by three destroyers to the south-south-east of Kelibia, Tunisia in position 36°40'N, 11°12'E on course 180° at 9 knots.

[We have been unable to place an Italian convoy in this position at this time. Also no Italian destroyers or torpedo boats were this area on that day. Vichy French phosphate ships usually travelled singly and unescorted. The only Vichy French warship in the area was the torpedo boat L'Iphigénie leaving Sfax on 12 January and arriving at Bizerte on 13 January. (10)
14 Jan 1942 At 1601 B/14, east-south-east of Mahdia, Tunisia, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) sighted a vessel to the south-west. HMS P 35 closed at speed and commenced an attack.

At 1702 B/14, the attack was abandoned as the range was still over 6000 yards and was now opening. The vessel was northbound and about 2000 to 3000 tons. It was escorted by a small escort vessel of which only the masts and the tops of the bridge and funnel were visible.
At 1908 B/14, HMS P 35 surfaced in position 35°20'N, 11°46'E.

At 2345 B/14, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1845 B/14 [or 1835 B/14, sources vary on this time of origin] was received which contained the following orders; Establish patrol within 30 miles of Sousse proceeding through positions 35°45'N, 11°30'E and 35°55'N, 11°20'E. Spend daylight hours near port retiring to charge. Indications are that supplies to Libya are being sent in (?) single ships which arrive from north at dawn anchor 4 miles out and leave for southward at dusk. You may sink any ship outside territorial waters day or night (?) except Vichy warships but particular care must be taken that no ships are sunk by day inside territorial waters unless definitely identified as enemy. By night ships may be sunk if under way inside territorial waters south of 35°46'N. Suspect route for Sousse 36°18'N, 10°55'E, 36°01'N, 10°55'E and 35°49'N, 10°46'E and from Sousse close round Kuriat to southward. Port light believed visibility reduced to 2 miles. Make full use of M.D.U. (Mine Detection Unit) but minefield not suspected except possibly within 5 miles of position 34°48'N, 11°18'E. (13)
15 Jan 1942 At 0410 B/15, in position 36°01'N, 10°55'E, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) set course 210 down the suspected swept channel to Sousse. Commenced using the M.D.U. (Mine Detection Unit).
At 0652 B/15, HMS P 35 dived in position 35°52'N, 10°50'E and investigated Sousse Bay and the Gulf of Monastir without result.

At 1512 B/15, a grounded vessel was sighted near Kuriat Island. P 35 closed to investigate. The position of the grounded vessel was 082° - Kuriat Light - 1.2 miles. The mast and bridge of a charted wreck in position 067° - Kuriat Light - 3 miles were clearly visible awash.
At 1948 B/15, HMS P 35 surfaced in position 360° - Kuriat Light - 5 miles and patrolled on the surface in the Gulf of Hammamet. (12)

16 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 0135 B/16, the Vice-Admiral Malta informed HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) of the following; Position of one merchant ship one destroyer is 180° - 35°00'N, 12°00'E - 8 miles. Enemy course 360°, speed 12 knots. Swordfish have attacked the enemy with torpedoes, estimated number of hits unknown.

This signal referred to the Italian submarine depot ship Antonio Pacinotti (former Città di Sassari, 3057 tons, built 1925) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Calliope which reported being illuminated by flares and attacked by torpedo bombers at 0130 B/16 in position 34°45'N, 12°25'E but were undamaged. Antonio Pacinotti manoeuvred successfully to avoid two torpedoes. They had sailed from Tripoli at 1530 B/15.

At 1137 B/16, Calliope left the depot ship and turned back to reinforce a southbound convoy (the Calliope report erroneously report that it was a northbound convoy), these were the Italian transport San Giovanni Battista (5628 GRT, built 1913) escorted by the Italian destroyer Freccia which had sailed from Trapani at 1740 B/15-1800 B/15 and arrived at Tripoli at 1418 B/17-1540 B/17.
At 0653 B/16, HMS P 35 dived off Sousse and patrolled to the eastward.
At 1114 B/16, a small steamer was seen approaching from Sousse. It was eastbound. On investigation it was seen to be actually very small. It flew the Tunisian flag.
At 1937 B/16, HMS P 35 surfaced in position 008° - Kuriat Light - 12.5 miles and commenced to patrol on the surface to the north and west in the Gulf of Hammamet. (13)
17 Jan 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian salvage vessel Rampino (301 GRT, built 1909) north-north-east of Sousse, Tunisia in position 35°59'N, 10°46'E.
HMS P 35 picks up the Rampino's sole survivor. According to this survivor Rampino had a crew of fifteen on board and was en-route to Pantelleria.
0311 B/17 - A low dark shape was sighted ahead. It was thought to be a u-boat. Started attack.
0320 B/17 - In position 35°59'N, 10°46'E fired three torpedoes from 1500 yards. Dived on firing. One hit was obtained.
0330 B/17 - Heard HE of a small patrol vessel stopping and starting. This soon turned away and retired bearing 080° and HE soon faded.
0636 B/17 - Surfaced near the position of the attack.
0800 B/17 - Sighted a raft with one survivor. Picked him up. Another raft was seen but it was empty.
0839 B/17 - Dived in position 35°59'N, 10°48'E.
0905 B/17 - Proceeded into Sousse Bay to see if there were any other ships but nothing was sighted.
1222 B/17 - Proceeded to investigate the Gulf of Monsastir but he also nothing was sighted. (12)
20 Jan 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) attacked two merchant ships in a convoy about 5 nautical miles north of Kuriat with four torpedoes. No hits were obtained.
[The targets have not yet been identified and may well have been Vichy-French.]
In a signal timed 1039 B/20 the Capt. S.10 informed HMS P 35 of the following; Escorted southbound Vichy-Frech convoy of about eight ships may be encountered during daylight today 20th.
2014 B/20 - Surfaced in position 035° - Kuriat Light - 18 miles and proceeded on course 200° to close Kuriat.
2100 B/20 - Sighted many lights in the direction of Monastir. Closed.
2126 B/20 - The lights were seen to be ships.

2146 B/20 - Reduced to slow speed. Six ships fully illuminated were counted. Their course was 090°. It was estimated that this convoy must have been outside French territorial waters while crossing the Gulf of Monastir and would pass 2 to 3 miles North of Kuriat. They were proceeding to the Eastward and could only leave French territorial waters if they were not already outside them. If they were to turn to the South after rounding Kuriat they would cross latitude 35°46'N south of which they were open to attack irrespective of whether they were in French waters or not. It was therefore decided that if they were French they were proceeding or about to proceed illegitimately and that it was possible they were Italians masquerading as French. It was decided to attack.
2149 B/20 - In position 35°55'N, 11°02'E fired two torpedoes at the leading ship, the second largest. Range 4000 yards.
2150 B/20 - Fired two torpedoes at the second ship in line, the largest. Range 4000 yards.
2151 B/20 - Dived and altered course to starboard. All torpedoes missed as the range was most likely underestimated. Set course to the north-east.
2210 B/20 - Commenced reloading the last remaining torpedo.
2233 B/20 - Set course to 170° on completion of the reload.
2234 B/20 - Fired the last torpedo at the last ship. It too missed. Set course to the north-east to return to Malta.
2314 B/20 - Surfaced and proceeded on course 070°. (12)

21 Jan 1942 At 0106 B/21, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) passed her signal timed 2149 B/20 which contained the following; Six vessels left Kuriat southbound at slow speed. All torpedoes expended, am returning to base. (10)
22 Jan 1942 At 0735 B/22, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) ended her 2nd war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (12)

1 Feb 1942 At 1920 B/1, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Malta for 3rd war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Kuriat between 35°15'N / 36°01'N and west of 11°56'E in Area 'H' (35°15'N, 10°30'E - 000° - 45 -70).
[As no log is available for this period it is not possible to display a map of this patrol.] (13)

2 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1945 B/2, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) of the following; P.M. air reconnaissance sighted a medium motor vessel anchored off Sousse with steam up.

In a signal timed 2157 B/2, the Capt. S.10 sent the following to HMS P 35; 5000 tons north hound motor vessel anchored in position 35°29'N, 11° 06'E P.M./2 awaiting escort from north. Aircraft will probably attack tonight. Endeavour to intercept before ship passes Kuriat. (14)
3 Feb 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) torpedoed and damaged the Italian transport Napoli (6142 GRT, built 1941) about 30 nautical miles east of Sousse, Tunisia.

Napoli had sailed from Tripoli for Italy but on 30 January had broken her propeller’s axle and had to drop anchor at Mahedia. Because of the heavy seas, attempts by her escort, the Italian torpedo boat Orsa to tow her failed. On 2 February, Orsa was relieved by the Italian torpedo boat Procione and the naval auxiliary (cable layer) Giasone (630 tons, built 1941).

Napoli was hit astern and damaged, five of the crew were wounded by the explosion. Giasone took her in tow towards Sousse but after a few miles, she had to be beached the following day in 35°27'N, 11°03'E. Napoli was destroyed by aircraft on the 11th.
1206 B/3 - Sighted a torpedo boat of the Spica-class. The enemy turned out of range and disappeared on a course of 290°.
1340 B/3 - Sighted Mehedia and proceeded into the Bay to the southward.
1526 B/3 - Sighted a transport of about 6000 tons anchored in approximate position 35°30'N, 11°06'E.
1533 B/3 - Sighted an Italian torpedo boat of the Spica-class patrolling close inshore. This was the same vessel as seen at 1206 B/3. Started attack on the transport. [This was Procione.]
1637 B/3 - Fired one torpedo at the transport from 2600 yards. It missed.
1702 B/3 - Fired another torpedo at the transport from 2400 yards. It hit. The vessel was seen to be down by the stern and listing to starboard afterwards.
1725 B/3 - The torpedo boat [Procione] counter attacked. Eight depth charges were dropped but these did no damage.
1830 B/3 - Commenced reloading torpedo tubes numbers 3 and 4.
2145 B/3 - Surfaced in position 35°33'N, 11°15'E. Patrolled during the night to the north-eastward of Mahedia outside the suspected minefield to the east of Kuriat Island. (12)

5 Feb 1942 At 0055 B/5, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) sighted a medium sized merchant vessel followed by a small vessel leaving Sousse Roads on course 040°. The vessels were burning navigation lights. They were not molested as these were most likely Vichy-French.
At 1449 B/5, near Kuriat Island, HMS P 35 sighted smoke close inshore of Ras Dimass. P 35 closed at full speed.
At 1535 B/5, the chase was abandoned as the vessel was still very distant and was small northbound steamer inside territorial water. It was thus very likely this vessel was also Vichy-French. (15)

6 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1059 B/6, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) of the following; Air reconnaissance had sighted two destroyers and one merchant ship in position 34°50'N, 12°00'E proceeding on course 020° at 8 knots at 1000 B/6.

This referred to the Italian transport Monviso (5322 GRT, built 1941), escorted by the Italian destroyer Antonio da Noli and the Italian torpedo boat Castore which had sailed from Tripoli at 1710 B/5-1717 B/5. They had stopped at Marsa Dila from 2000 B/5 to 0230 B/6 before sailing for Trapani. They were attacked by RAF torpedo bombers at 0042 B/7 without result, during which Monviso's steering gear broke down. They arrived at Trapani 0145 B/7-0200 B/7 where the torpedo boat Castore entered harbour at 0206 B/7 and was replaced by the torpedo boat Aretusa. After a very brief stop just off Trapani, they went on to Messina, arriving at 1430 B/8-1435 B/8. (14)
7 Feb 1942 At 2325 B/7, east of Kuriat Island, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) sighted a searchlight on bearing 090°. Course was altered and P 35 closed at full speed to investigate.

At 2350 B/7, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1635 B/7, which had been received at 2320 B/7, was decoded. It contained the following information; HMS Zulu (Cdr. H.R. Graham, DSO, DSC, RN) and HMS Lively (Lt.Cdr. W.F.E. Hussey, DSC, RN) will operate on a line Lampedusa - Pantellaria tonight, returning by the south-east channel to arrive in Grand Harbour at 0730 B/8.
On this signal being decoded HMS P 35 immediately withdrew to the south-west at full speed. (16)

9 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1253 B/9. the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) to arrive at Marsamxett (Malta) by the south-east channel at 0730 B/13 by proceeding through positions 35°40'N, 12°00'E and 35°40'N, 14°08'E. (14)
11 Feb 1942 At 2012 B/11, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) surfaced in position 35°39'N, 11°40'E and left patrol to return to Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1253 B/9. (12)
13 Feb 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) ended her 3rd war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (12)

21 Feb 1942 Around 2020 B/21, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Malta for her 4th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the east of Tripoli, Libya, in position 'T' (32°38'N, 14°42'E), to intercept an important enemy convoy which was expected to proceed from Italy to Tripoli soon.
P 35 was to proceed to position 'T' through position 32°48'N, 14°45'E.

This was to intercept the enemy convoy operation K 7. On 13 February, ULTRA had revealed the imminent departure of this convoy [see DEFE3/750 frames 77, 78, 153]. The transport Monviso (Italian, 5322 GRT, built 1941), escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Lince was to leave from Brindisi. The tanker Giulio Giordani (Italian, 10534 GRT, built 1939) and the transport Lerici (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Scirocco and the torpedo boat Pallade were to leave from Taranto. They were to be screened by a task group formed around the battleship Caio Duilio. This operation was postponed, perhaps due to the presence of a Malta convoy (convoy MW 9A/B).

Eventually, the convoys were reorganised with three more ships from Messina and sailed on 20-21 February: 1) At 1935 B/20-2105 B/20, Giulio Giordani and Lerici sailed from Taranto, escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta (leader), Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Scirocco and the torpedo boat Circe. 2) At 2205 B/20-2237 B/20, Monviso sailed from Brindisi escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Angelo Bassini. 3) At 0345 B/21, the transport Unione (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1942) sailed from Naples at 0345 B/21 escorted by the torpedo boats Orsa and Enrico Cosenz and transited the Straits of Messina at 1730 B/21. 4) At 1730 B/21-1915 B/21, the transports Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941) and Monginevro (Italian, 5324 GRT, built 1940) sailed from Messina, escorted by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello, Premuda, Strale, Nicolò Zeno and the torpedo boats Giuseppe Dezza and Pallade and merged with the Unione convoy.

To cover the operation the following forces were deployed: 1) At 1445 B21-1514 B/21, the battleship Caio Duilio, screened by the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Ascari and Geniere sailed from Taranto. 2) At 1705 B/21-1845 B/21, the heavy cruisers Gorizia, Trento and the light cruiser Giovanni Delle Bande Nere screened by the destroyers Alpino, Alfredo Oriani and Antonio da Noli sailed from Messina. During the night, they caught up with the Ravello/Monginevro/Unione convoys.

At 0726 B/21-0754 B/21, the Giordani and Monviso convoys merged and proceeded to Corfu where they arrived at 1100 B/21-1126 B/21. At 1130 B/21-1400 B/21, they sailed from Corfu (without the torpedo boat Angelo Bassini, which later sailed for Valona).

At 0710 B/22, the Caio Duilio squadron sighted the Ravello/Monginevro/Unione convoy covered by the Gorizia squadron. Scirocco left the convoy as it crossed the 34th parallel and arrived at Messina at 1424 B/23. The Caio Duilio squadron turned back and arrived at Taranto at 0202 B/24-0230 B/24. The Gorizia squadron also left the convoy and returned to Messina, arriving at 1159 B/24-1340 B/24. The torpedo boats Calliope and Perseo sailed from Tripoli to reinforce the convoy and joined it at 1150 B/23. The convoy arrived at Tripoli at 1525 B/23-1735 B/23. (16) 22 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1018 B/22, the Capt. S.10 informed all submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla which were at sea that at 0925 B/22 three merchant ships, two cruisers and ten destroyers had been sighted in position 35°18'N, 18°40'E proceeding on course 190° at speeds of 8 to 12 knots In a signal timed 1111 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that eight merchant merchant ships, two battleships, five cruisers and fifteen destroyers had been sighted in position 35°12'N, 18°22'E on course 190° at a speed of 8 knots. In a signal timed 1336 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that an enemy battleship of the Littorio-class, two cruisers of the Trento / Condottieri-class, four destroyers, three merchant ships and three enemy aircraft had been reported in position 34°48'N, 18°30'E proceeding on course 190° at a speed of 10 knots. The following submarines were the ones that were on patrol at that time and thus must have received these signals; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN), HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN). Presumably HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) also received these signals as she was also on patrol but she was to the north of Sicily at that time so given the position of the convoy sighted the signals were of no use to her. (14) 23 Feb 1942 At 0815 B/23, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) was in position 32°57'N, 14°45'E and course was altered to 180° for the ordered patrol position which was position 'T' (32°48'N, 14°45'E). Between 1000 B/23 and 1450 B/23, numerous and heavy explosions were heard fairly close. Nothing could be seen through the periscope though or detected on Asdic. P 35 therefore continued on proceeding southwards towards position 'T'. At 1430 B/23, HMS P 35 was in estimated position 32°38'N, 14°43'E. She continued on to the southwards to fix her position by land bearings. At 1803 B/23, P 35 was in estimated position 32°32'N, 14.45'E. Soundings gave a depth of 44 fathoms. Course was altered to 080°. At 2205 B/23, course was altered to 010° and HMS P 35 proceeded in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1203 B/23 in which she was ordered to patrol east of Sicily / South of the Straits of Messina. (12) 23 Feb 1942 In signals timed 1152 B/23 and 1203 B/23, Capt. S.10 sent the following orders; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) leave their patrol positions at 2000 B/23 through positions 35°00'N, 14°12'E, and 35°00'N, 14°18'E respectively to arrive off Marsamxett harbour at 0715 B/25. HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'K', 34°20'N, 11°10'E - 000° - 40 - 60 through position 33°25'N, 13°45'E. HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'G', 35°05'N, 12°05'E - 000° - 50 - 40 through positions 33°05'N, 14°11'E and 35°00'N, 13°05'E. HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'L', south of 34°15'N and west of 11°40'E. She was to proceed through positions 33°00'N, 14°11'E and 33°50'N, 13°20'E. HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'H', 35°15'N, 11°00'E - 000° - 40 - 50 proceeding through positions 33°00'N, 14°32'E and 35°00'N, 12°30'E. HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'N', south of 33°45'N and between 11° 50'E and 12°55'E proceeding through positions 32°50'N, 14°45'E and 33.20'N, 13.45'E. HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'J', 36°54'N, 15°24'E - 330° - 40 - 70 proceeding through positions 32°38'N, 15°00'E and 35°00'N, 15°30'E. They were not to attack submarines encountered during the night of 23/24 February to prevent friendly fire incidents from happening. (14) 24 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 2314 B/24, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Suspected for 20 miles morth and 10 miles west of position 36°00'N, 15°40'E. (14) 27 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1707 B/27, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Air reconnaissance Messina on the 27th showed, two 8-inch, one 6-inch cruiser, eleven destroyers and six submarines. (14) 28 Feb 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) attacked a medium seized merchant vessel with two torpedoes about 10 nautical miles South-East of Catania, Sicily in position 37°22'N, 15°16'E. No hits were obtained. [The target has not yet been identified.] 0917 B/28 - Sighted smoke bearing 190°. Set course to intercept. 1012 B/28 - In position 37°22'N, 15°16'E fired two torpedoes at a 2000 - 3000 tons merchant vessel. Range was 3800 yards. A loud explosion was heard and the torpedoes were not heard to run anymore. The target made a dense cloud of black funnel smoke and made for Catania at increased speed. Proceeded to the northward to patrol along the Calabrian shore. (12) 1 Mar 1942 At 0849 B/1, was in position Capo Del Armi - 325° - 5.2 miles, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN). She then patrolled eastwards along the Calabrian coast. Large railway and road bridges at the Amendolea river estuary appeared inviting gun targets. (12) 1 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 1203 B/1, the Capt. S.10 sent the following instructions on leaving patrol to the submarines mentioned; HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) was to arrive at Malta on 4 March and was to proceed through positions 35°40'N, 12°00'E and 35°40'N, 14°20'E on the way back. HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) was to arrive at Malta on 5 March and was to proceed through positions 34°00'N, 12°50'E and 35°32'N, 14°14'E on the way back. HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) was to arrive at Malta on 5 March and was to proceed through positions 37°02'N, 15°40'E and 35°51'N, 15°15'E on the way back. (17) 2 Mar 1942 Lt. Maydon decided to bombard the bridges seen the day before so HMS P 35 returned during the forenoon to within 3/4 of a miles of the river mouth for final reconnaissance and then withdrew to seaward to attack late in the afternoon. At 1040 B/2, two small vessels, prossible MA/SB's (A/S launches) close inshore proceeding slowly eastwards. In view of this, Lt. Maydon abandoned the projected attack on the bridges and set course for the area to the south-east of Catania. (12) 3 Mar 1942 At 1122 B/3, near Catania, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) sighted two small vessels which could not be identified before they disappeared in the mist. (12) 4 Mar 1942 At 0755 B/4, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) was to the east of Syracuse, in position 37°03'N, 15°27'E. She then left patrol to return to Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1203 B/1. (12) 5 Mar 1942 At 0635 B/5, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) ended her 4th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (12) 8 Mar 1942 While dived during daylight at Malta with half the crew on board, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) had her pressure hull pierced forward by accident. The tube space flooded. At 0940 B/8 she tried to surface but failed to do so. She was then raised at 1220 B/8 and was docked for repairs. During repairs the fore-end compartment, which had been flooded was re-wired. Repairs were completed by March 27th. (18) 27 Mar 1942 At 2000 B/27, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Malta for her 5th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol between Kerkennah and Lampedusa, between 35°00'N / 35°50'N and 11°10'E and 12°96'E. [As no log is available for this period it is not possible to display a map of this patrol.] (12) 28 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 1630 B/28, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) of the following; Escorted southbound medium motor vessel of 11.5 knots may be encountered in the vicinity of position 35°20'N, 11°50'E around 1700 B/29. Aircraft may attack after dark. This was most probably the Italian steamer Argentea (3302 GRT, built 1908) escorted by the Italian destroyer Antoniotto Usodimare which known from ULTRA intercepts (DEFE3/751/frames 226, 229, 265, 272, 350) to leave Naples at 0400 B/28, at a speed of 12 knots for Tripoli. Argentea escorted by the Antoniotto Usodimare and the Italian torpedo boat Giuseppe Sirtori actually sailed from Naples at 0500 B/28-0550 B/28 in heavy fog and rain. At 2302 B/28, when off Cape San Vito, they turned back and stopped at Palermo (0410 B/29-1630 B/29). Shortly after leaving Palermo, defects forced Giuseppe Sirtori to leave them and return to Palermo, only Antoniotto Usodimare escorted the merchant ship. At 0448 B/31, they met the torpedo boat Castore which had sailed from Tripoli at 0445 B/31 to pilot them in, and they reached Tripoli at 0943 B/31-0950 B/31. Note: it was also known that the Italian tanker Saturno (former French Massis, 5022 GRT, built 1914) (DEFE3/751, frames 217, 219, 228, 256, 326) was to sail from Naples at 1700 B/27, at a speed of 9 knots for Tripoli. She actually sailed at 1950 B/27 escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Lince. The Italian torpedo boat Generale Carlo Montanari had sailed from Trapani at 1835 B/27 to reinforce the escort, but bad weather forced them to anchor at Favignana which they reached at 0030 B/28. Generale Carlo Montanari briefly returned to Trapani to refuel before returning to Favignana. The three ships sailed from there at 1730 B/28-1900 B/28. The torpedo boat Calliope sailed from Tripoli at 0430 B/30 and met the convoy at 0800 B/30 to pilot them in. They reached Tripoli at 1120 B/30-1150 B/30. (17) 30 Mar 1942 In the afternoon, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) investigated the wreck of the Italian transport Napoli (6142 GRT, built 1941) she had torpedoed on 3 February. The vessel beached near Mahedia appeared abandoned. Also her guns had been removed and no further action seemed necessary. (4) 30 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 2207 B/30, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Patrol in the vicinity of position 35°23'N, 12°21'E from 1400 B/31 where reconnaissance suggests that a large southbound convoy may be encountered before dark. On 26 March, ULTRA had intercepted a signal that six merchant ships were expected to arrive at Tripoli on 1 April (see DEFE3/091/1/frames 202, 214, 236). No details of this convoy were known but it was suspected that the battleship Littorio and the light cruiser Eugenio di Savoia might be involved in covering it. This must have referred to the convoys of operation LUPO (three convoys which merged into one) which were delayed when it was learned that an enemy squadron of two cruisers and four destroyers screening a convoy of four merchant ships had been sighted at 1315 B/1 in position 040° - Ras Haleima – 35 miles, course 285°. This probably referred to convoy AT 36 which had sailed from Alexandria on 31 March for Tobruk, escorted by the corvettes HMS Delphinium (Cdr.(Retd.) R.L. Spalding, RN), HMS Primula (T/Lt. J. Paterson, RNR), HMS Snapdragon (T/Lt. P.H. Potter, RNR), the escort destroyers HMS Eridge (Lt.Cdr. W.F.N. Gregory-Smith, DSC, RN), HMS Beaufort (Lt.Cdr. S.O’G Roche, RN), minesweepers HMS Fareham (Lt.Cdr. N.H.B. Bloye, RNR) and the auxiliary M/S whaler HMS Firmament (?). The three Italian convoys finally sailed on 2-3 April: Convoy 1: Transports Unione (Italian 6071 GRT, built 1942) and Lerici (Italian 6070 GRT, built 1941), escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta (leader), Antonio da Noli, Euro and the torpedo boat Cigno, sailed from Taranto at 1200 B/2-1250 B/2. During the evening, Euro was detached and reached Bari at 0625 B/3. Convoy 2: Transports Nino Bixio (Italian, 7137 GRT, built 1940) and Monviso (Italian 5322 GRT, built 1941), escorted by the destroyers Emanuele Pessagno (leader), Folgore and the torpedo boat Centauro, sailed from Messina at 2350 B/2-0030 B/3. Convoy 3: Transports Gino Allegri (Italian, 6836 GRT, built 1941) and Monreale (6989 GRT, built 1941), escorted by the destroyers Nicolò Zeno (leader), Freccia and the torpedo boat Pallade, sailed from Augusta at 0145 B/3-0200 B/3. Covering force: The light cruisers Eugenio di Savoia and Raimondo Monteccucoli (7th Cruiser Division) screened by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello and Mitragliere sailed from Taranto at 1759 B/2-1900 B/2. Mitragliere left the escort at about 0800 B/3 and returned to Taranto at 2230 B/3. The battleship Littorio did not take part in the operation as she was docked for repairs at Taranto on 2 April. The convoys were united at 0747 B/3 in position 36°59'N, 16°47'E. At this point, Cigno received the order to return to Messina where she arrived at 1412 B/3. The covering force joined at 0830 B/3 and proceeded together until 2020 B/3 when the covering force left and at 2110 B/3, when, in position 34°03'N, 16°04'E, the three convoys separated again. Monreale and Gino Allegri escorted by Nicolò Zeno (leader), Folgore and Centauro. Monviso and Lerici escorted by Emanuele Pessagno (leader), Freccia and Pallade. Unione and Nino Bixio escorted by Antonio Pigafetta (leader), Antonio da Noli. At 0035 B/4, Antonio da Noli was attacked by an enemy bomber but was missed. The torpedo boat Castore sailed from Buerat El Hsun at 1630 B/3 to carry out an A/S sweep off Tripoli ahead of the arrival of the convoys. At 0540 B/4, she sighted the Monviso/Lerici convoy which was following the coastal route to Tripoli and at 0606 B/4, she sighted the Unione/Nino Bixio convoy arriving from the north and took position ahead to pilot it to Tripoli where they arrived at 0928 B/4. In the meantime, Emanuele Pessagno escorting the Monviso/Lerici convoy, ordered Pallade to pilot it to Tripoli where the trio arrived at 0858 B/4. The torpedo Calliope sailed from Tripoli at 0445 B/4 and at 0627 B/4, joined the Gino Allegri/Monreale convoy to pilot it in. The destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Antonio da Noli, Nicolò Zeno, Emanuele Pessagno and Freccia stopped short of entering harbour and returned to Italy. Antonio da Noli and Emanuele Pessagno arrived at Messina at 0730 B/5-0740 B/5, Antonio Pigafetta, Nicolò Zeno and Freccia arrived at Taranto at 1510 B/5-1600 B/5. The covering force returned to Taranto at 1842 B/4-1930 B/4. (17) 31 Mar 1942 In a singal timed 1415 B/31, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) of the following; My 2207 B/30. Convoy now unlikely. Patrol eastern route near position 35°40'N, 12°15'E for a northbound merchant vessel expected in the forenoon of April 1st. speed about 13.5 knots. (17) 1 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1019 B/1 the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Remain in the vicinity as ordered in my signal timed 1415 B/31. (19) 3 Apr 1942 At 0600 B/3, south-west of Lampedusa, in position 35°23'N, 12°15'E, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) sighted three vessels bearing 045° at a range of 10000 yards. This were thought to be probably two merchant vessels with one small escort. They were zigzagging on a northerly course to pass between Lampion and Lampedusa. Estimated speed of the enemy convoy was 10 about 10 knots. HMS P 35 proceeded at full speed but was unable to close. At 0700 B/3, the convoy was lost from sight. HMS P 35 set course to patrol to the north of Lampion Island. At 1900 B/3, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1215 B/3 was received which contained the following orders; Cancel my signal timed 1019 B/1. Resume normal patrol. Lt. Maydon decided to remain in the vicinity of Lampion though. (12) 4 Apr 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) attacked an escorted merchant vessel with one torpedo about 10 nautical miles north-west of Lampion Island in position 35°37'N, 12°09'E. The torpedo missed. This must have been the Italian transport Una (1397 GRT, built 1904) escorted by the German motor minesweepers R 6, R 10, R 11 and R 12. They had sailed from Trapani around 1730 B/3 bound for Tripoli but stopped on the way at Lampedusa at 1715 B/4. Una continued her passage to Tripoli at 2000 B/5 but now escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Calliope and the German motor minesweepers R 10, R 11, R 15 and R 16. The convoy arrived at Tripoli around 0500 B/7. 1138 B/4 - Sighted smoke bearing 299°. Altered course to intercept, went deep and closed at full speed. 1155 B/4 - The target appeared to be a 3500 tons merchant vessel with an escort and accompanying aircraft. Altered course to close more quickly. 1228 B/4 - The target was now seen to be painted black and white. She was escorted by 4 E-boats or R-boats and aircraft. Enemy course was estimated as 160°, speed 8,5 knots, range 7500 yards. 1238 B/4 - The target was seen to have altered course to 130°. P 35 was now almost right ahead of her at a range of 3500 yards. Maneuvered to obtain a favourable attack position. 1250 B/4 - It appeared P 35's periscope had been spotted so Lt. Maydon went to 180 feet and altered course. No counter attack followed however. 1317 B/4 - Returned to periscope depth. Saw the target about 4500 yards away steering a course of 140°. The escorts were in station on each side of the target. P 35 was nearly right astern of the target, not a favourable attack position. Given the strong escort of the target it was decided to attack with 1 torpedo as it must have been a valuable ship and cargo for the enemy. 1319 B/4 - In position 35°37'N, 12°09'E fired no.1 torpedo tube. Range was 5000 yards. The torpedo missed. P 35 then retired from the area to the west. (20) 5 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1808 B/5, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) were informed that HMS Havock (Lt.Cdr. G.R.G. Watkins, DSC, RN) would be sailing at 2000 B/5 by the south east swept channel thence direct to position 36°42.5'N, 11°04'E. Speed of advance would be 28 knots. The submarines were instructed to keep clear. (19) 6 Apr 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) attacked an escorted merchant vessel with two torpedoes about 20 nautical miles west-north-west of Lampedusa in position 35°37'N, 12°12'E. No hits were obtained. This must have been the German transport Atlas (2297 GRT, built 1927) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Perseo and the German motor minesweepers R 9, R 12 and R 14 bound from Trapani to Tripoli. At 1205 B/6, the submarine had observed three E-boats or R-boats moving to the northwest, this were R 9, R 12 and R 14 who had sailed from Lampedusa at 1000 B/6 to reinforce Atlas her escort. Curiously enough Lt. Maydon did not mention the presence of the R-boats during the attack. Perhaps they were not seen due to their low silhouettes and the probability that the target was only very briefly observed with the periscope because of the presence of aircraft. The R-boats had joined the convoy at 1255 B/6. The attack appeared not to have been observed. The convoy arrived at Tripoli around 1130 B/7. 1205 B/6 - In position 35°43'N, 12°08'E, sighted three E-boats or R-boats bearing 100° at a range of 4 miles. No other ships were in company. Enemy course was approximately 300° at about 8 knots. Due to the presence of a number of aircraft HMS P 35 altered course to the westward and went to 80 feet. 1340 B/6 - Heard HE bearing 250°. Went to periscope depth and sighted masts on that bearing. Altered course to 200° to intercept and closed at full speed. 1414 B/6 - The target was seen to be a merchant ship escorted by a Spica-class torpedo boat which was ahead of her. Turned to a 120 degree track and closed at full speed. 1430 B/6 - In position 35°37'N, 12°12'E fired two torpedoes from 3200 yards. Both missed. Went to 80 feet as aircraft were sighted and retired to the west. (20) 6 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1335 B/6, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Leave patrol through position 35°20'N, 12°10'E and the south-east swept channel to arrive at Marsamxett P.M. 9/4. (17) 9 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1215 B/9, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Adjust speed to enter Marsamxett at 2100 B and berth alongside HMS Talbot. HMS P 31 and HMS Urge are submerged within two miles of 033° - 8 miles and 079° - 9 miles respectively from St. Elmo Light. Former to enter harbour at 1945 B/9, ,latter at 2030 B/9 wile passing through position 067° - St. Elmo - 4.7. miles. Around 2100 B/9, HMS P 35 ended her 5th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (12) 14 Apr 1942 At 0510 B/14, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Malta for her 6th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol between Kerkenah and Lampedusa, between 35°00'N / 35°30'N and 11°10'E / 13°01'E. [As no log is available for this period it is not possible to display a map of this patrol.] (12) 15 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1347 B/15, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) to keep west of 12°05'E in area 'K' for 24 hours from 2359 B/16. (19) 17 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 2217 B/17, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) to patrol north of Kerkennah No.4 buoy until 0800 B/18 to intercept a 15 knot northbound ship. (19) 18 Apr 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) attacked the Italian transport Nino Bixio (7137 GRT, built 1941) about 40 nautical miles south-west of Lampedusa in position 35°05'N, 11°49'E. All torpedoes fired missed their target. Nino Bixio sailed from Tripoli at 1915 B/17-1920 B/17 making 15 knots. She was escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta and Nicolò Zeno. At 0658 B/18, Nicolò Zeno first sighted a bubble discharge from a distance of 1500 metres then a torpedo wake which passed astern Nino Bixio and apparently exploded on Kerkenah Bank. Nicolò Zeno dropped five 50-kg and two 100-kg depth charges. They arrived at Naples at 1017 B/19-1035 B/19. 0015 B/18 - In position 35°14'N, 11°32'E, sighted a steady white light bearing 185°. Gave chase at full speed. After thirty minutes speed was reduced to listen for HE but nothing was heard. Soundings gave a depth of 8.5 fathoms. The light was considered to be most likely a fishing boat among the banks. The chase was abandoned. 0315 B/18 - Arrived in position 15 miles bearing 020° from Kerkennah No.4 buoy and commenced to patrol on a 6-mile line on courses 020° / 200°. 0453 B/18 - Dived in position 35°03'N, 11°50'E. Soundings gave a depth of 15.5 fathoms. 0630 B/18 - Sighted a 6000-tons merchant vessel escorted by two destroyers. Started attack. 0655 B/18 - In position 35°05'N, 11°49'E fired three torpedoes from 5800 yards. It was thought one probable hit was obtained but this was not the case. 0704 B/18 - The first pattern of four depth charges was dropped. After this attack only two more depth charges were dropped. None caused any damage. 0734 B/18 - Returned to periscope depth. Sighted the target bearing 338°. 2333 B/18 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2205 B/18 which contained the following orders; Patrol Kuriat - Kerkennah No.4 buoy route until 2030 B/19 for a southbound 10 knots tanker and escort. Aircraft are searching and will attack tonight in the Marettimo - Cape Bon area. Course was set to comply with these orders. This last signal referred to the tanker Panuco (Italian 7751 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the destroyer Folgore and the torpedo Centauro sailed from Naples at 2220 B/18-2256 B/18 (convoy ALFA). The torpedo boat Pallade sailed from Tripoli at 0125 B/20 to reinforce the escort. At 2312 B/20, the convoy was attacked by a Wellington bomber, bombs were dropped between Pallade and Centauro in position 176° Lampedusa – 40 miles. They arrived at Tripoli at 1432 B/21-1520 B/21. ULTRA intercepts had revealed their imminent departure since 14 April, but it was delayed (see DEFE3/840/1/pages 359, 367, 368, 312, 498, 583-584). (12) 19 Apr 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian transport Assunta de Gregori (4219 GRT, built 1914) about 20 nautical miles east-south-east of Mahdia, Tunisia in position 35°23'N, 11°23'E. The Italian transport was on passage from Sfax to Naples escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Castore. Despite being equipped with sonar, the Castore, who was leading ahead, heard nothing and did not observe any torpedo track. Only the sound of a double explosion followed by the sight of Assunta di Gregori broken in two with her stern sinking vertically, triggered a counterattack. The torpedo boat turned back and picked up 35 survivors. She also dropped two depth charges for intimidation purposes. At 1632 B/18, the torpedo boat Castore had sailed from Tripoli and at 0446 B/19 was in view of the Sfax lighthouse. At 0545 B/19, she sighted Assunta di Gregori coming out of Sfax and at 0605 B/19, she signalled the merchant ship to stop and sent a boat with an officer to bring the operation orders from Marina Tripoli. At 0636 B/19, they proceeded in company. At 0735 B/19, aircraft appeared to provide air cover. At 1645 B/19, a double explosion occurred in position 35°22'N, 11°32'E and the merchant ship sank vertically. Castore did not sight any torpedo wake and did not know from where the attack had come. At 0702 B/19, she could only drop two depth charges for intimidation purposes. She recovered 35 survivors and proceeded to Palermo. She was then instructed by Supermarina to remain in the area and hunt the submarine but her portside engine broke down. She returned to the area of the attack, but the search was fruitless and, at 0800 B/20, she resumed her passage to Palermo where she arrived at 0757 B/21. 1550 B/19 - Sighted a merchant ship approaching from the southward. Altered course to intercept. 1557 B/19 - Sighted a Spica-class torpedo boat escorting the merchant vessel of about 4000 to 5000 tons. Also two aircraft were patrolling the area. Started attack. 1643 B/19 - In position 35°23'N, 11°23'E fired two torpedoes at the merchant vessel from 1100 yards. Both torpedoes hit the target. The torpedo boat dropped only 2 depth charges which caused no damage to P 35. 1730 B/19 - Returned to periscope depth. Saw the torpedo boat laying stopped to pick up the survivors of the merchant ship. P 35 continued to patrol to the south-east of Mahedia in the hope of intercepting the reported southbound tanker. (12) 20 Apr 1942 At 0638 B/20, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) fixed her position off Mahedia as being 35°29'N, 11°11'E, Course was set to 090° to return to the vicinity of Lampion Island. At 1156 B/20, in approximate position 35°29'N, 11°28'E a Spica-class torpedo boat was seen proceeding on a zigzag course of approximately 250°/310° at 15 knots. The enemy vessel was transmitting on Asdic every six seconds. She passed at a range of 4000 yards. No attack was made due to the shallow draft of the enemy vessel and the fact that P 35 had only three torpedoes remaining. It was appreciated that this torpedo boat might be going to reinforce the escort of some southbound traffic and accordingly P 35 set course to return to the coastal route. This was most probably Castore on her way to Palermo after searching for the submarine which had sunk the Assunta di Gregori (see 19 April). (12) 20 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1654 B/20, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) were informed by Capt. S.10 that their position was probably compromised by sighting. (19) 21 Apr 1942 At 0031 B/21, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1047 B/20, which ordered her to concentate on the central routes through her patrol area. Course was set to patrol near Lampion Island to comply with these orders. (21) 22 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1031 B/22, referring to his signal timed 1025 B/21, the Capt. S. 10 informed HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) that two 14-knot escorted merchant ships may be encountered proceeding north on meridian 11°49'E crossing 34°30'N about 1400 B/23. At 2030 B/23, HMS Una was extend her patrol area east to 12°50'E and north to 36°10'N. This must have referred to the convoy made up of the transports Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941) and Vettor Pisani (Italian, 6339 GRT, built 1939) en-route from Tripoli (departed 1935 B/22-2040 B/22) at 14.5 knots for Naples escorted by the Italian destroyer Folgore and the Italian torpedo boats Centauro and Pallade. At about 1630 B/23-1747 B/23, about 30 miles north of Pantelleria, there was a change of escort with another convoy proceeding to Tripoli: This convoy was made up of the transports Gino Allegri (Italian, 6836 GRT, built 1941) and Agostino Bertani (Italian, 8329 GRT, built 1941) and had sailed from Naples at 2245 B/22-2330 B/22, proceeding at 15.5 knots, escorted by the Italian destroyers Antonio Pigafetta Pigafetta, Nicolò Zeno and the Italian torpedo boat Cantore. The information was once again based on an ULTRA source (see DEFE3/753/486, frame 490 and DEFE3/754, frames 8, 78). (19) 23 Apr 1942 At 2056 B/23, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) surfaced in approximate position 35°15'N, 11°47'E and proceeded on passage back to Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.10's orders contained in his signal timed 1033 B/22. This signal contained the following orders; Leave patrol P.M./23 through positions 35°24'N, 12°48'E and 36°11'N, 14°11'E. Keep more than 7 miles from the coast until 070° from Stelmo on which bearing motor launches and drifters may be on patrol after dark. Enter Marsamxett (Malta) at 2101 B/25. (21) 25 Apr 1942 At 2100 B/25, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) ended her 6th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (12) 3 May 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Malta for her 7th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to perform a special operation on the north-west coast of Sicily. We do not know the exact details of her mission. She was to proceeded through position 'A' (38°00'N, 11° 44'E) about 1300 B/5. In a signal timed 2214 B/3, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS P 35 that in case the object is not achieved she should spend 24 hours on the Palermo - St. Vito convoy route before returning to Malta. [As no log is available for this period it is not possible to display a map of this patrol.] (4) 6 May 1942 In a signal timed 0050 B/6, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) informed the Capt. S.10 of the following; Starboard engine broken down, returning to Malta, E.T.A. 2030 B/6. Later that day, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) ended her 7th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (4) 10 May 1942 Around 0400 B/10, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Malta for passage to Alexandria. She was to proceed through position 35°51'N, 15°15'E and 34°50'N, 20°00'E. At 0417 hours, shortly after leaving harbour, five darkened vessels were observed and did not answer the challenge. P 35 opened fire with its Lewis gun. The Port Wireless Station ordered the submarine to cease firing. These were HMS Beryl, HMS Trusty Star and three others who were waiting for the arrival of HMS Welshman. HMS P 35 had not been informed of their presence. [As no log is available for this period it is not possible to display a map of this patrol.] (22) 11 May 1942 In a signal timed 1118 C/11, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Proceed direct to position 34°22'N, 26°35'E thence to arrive at the end of the Alexandria searched channel at 0530 C/19 reporting when safe to do so if you cannot comply within three hours. U-boats are operating on the Cyrenaican coast and may be encountered proceeding to and from Aegean. Submarines are not to be attacked unless definitely identified as hostile. (23) 19 May 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) arrived at Alexandria from Malta. (22) 6 Jun 1942 Around 1850 C/6, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Alexandria for her 8th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to give cover during convoy operations to Malta and took position 'D' (35°57'N, 19°13'E) in a line with three other submarines in the Ionian Sea. (HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) in position 'A' (35°37'N, 18°44'E), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) in position 'B' (35°43'N, 18°55'E) and HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) in position 'C' (35°50'N, 19°02'E). This was to provide cover for operation VIGOROUS. For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 35 during this patrol see the map below. (22) 13 Jun 1942 At 0145 B/13, in position (D.R.) 35°32'N, 20°09'E, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) sighted a submarine bearing 275° at a range of only 1000 yards. Course of the other submarine was approximately 020°. Avoiding action was taken as this submarine was most likely friendly. It was thought to be most probably HMS Proteus (Lt.Cdr. P.S. Francis, RN). This submarine however reported noting at this time nor did any other British submarine in the area. At 0217 C/13, HMS P 35 resumed it's original course of 285°. At 0512 C/13, HMS P 35 dived in position 35°37'N, 19°42'E and proceeded on course 310°. At 1413 C/13, HMS P 35 surfaced for a sunsight whih gave position 35°51'N, 19°16'E. She immediately dived again and proceeded to patrol in the vicinity of position 'D' (35°57'N, 19°13'E). At 2140 C/13, HMS P 35 fixed her position by starsights as being 35°55'N, 19°05'E. (22) 15 Jun 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) made a attack on the Italian battleship Vittorio Veneto. The torpedoes however missed their target. Position of the attack was 36°00'N, 19°06'E. The Italian squadron was led by the battleship Littorio with the Vittorio Veneto next in line. They were in company with the heavy cruiser Gorizia, the light cruisers Giuseppe Garibaldi and Emanuele Filiberto Duca D’Aosta and three squadrons of destroyers. The heavy cruiser Trento had just been disabled by an aircraft torpedo and lagged behind with two destroyers as escort. Later the same morning, HMS P 35 torpedoed and sank the already damaged Italian heavy cruiser Trento in position 35°55'N, 18°59'E. Trento was already damaged by a torpedo from a British Beaufort aircraft (217. Sqn. RAF based at Malta). Trento was screened by the destroyers Saetta and Antonio Pigafetta which subsequently started picking up survivors. The hospital ships Aquileia (9448 GRT, built 1914) and Città di Trapani (2467 GRT, built 1929) were directed to the scene to help with the search. In all 602 survivors were picked up from a crew of 1152 but many were wounded and some died later. 0001 C/15 - Altered course to 234° to proceed to position 'D 1' (35°30'N, 18°18'E) this in accordance with the operation orders. 0115 C/15 - Course was altered to 072° to regain position 'D' (35°57'N, 19°13'E) in accordance with the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2356 C/14 in which the submarines were ordered to re-occupy their original patrol positions. 0239 C/15 - Sighted a white light bearing 282°. Altered course towards to close at full speed. 0256 C/15 - The light appeared to be a calcium flare buring in the water with nothing attached to it. Hailed twice in case there was an aircraft dinghy in the neighbourhood but there came no reply. 0400 C/15 - Estimated to be back in position 'D'. Course was altered to 360° and speed was reduced. 0402 C/15 - Two very distant aircraft flares were seen on bearing 355°. 0413 C/15 - Further aircraft flares were seen. Course was altered to 340° and speed was increased. 0420 C/15 - A large number of aircraft flares were being dropped to illuminate the enemy that had been reported in the Vice-Admiral Malta's signal timed 0235 C/15. Course was altered to 300° and speed was further increased to try to intercept the enemy at dawn. 0427 C/15 - Twelve aircraft flares were seen being dropped in line on bearing 345°. 0524 C/15 - Took starsights and established position to be 36°02'N, 19°07'E. 0529 C/15 - Dived. Set course to 300°. 0540 C/15 - Heard HE bearing 320°. 0545 C/15 - Sighted two large vessels bearing 310°. Altered course to intercept and closed at full speed. 0555 C/15 - Started attack on two Littorio class battleships and two cruisers in line ahead. Enemy course was 175°, speed 26 knots. 0611 C/15 - Aircraft started a bombing attack. P 35 was about to fire torpedoes at a battleship. The Italians went all over the place and it was not possible to attack now. 0618 C/15 - The pandemonium had died down and two cruisers were seen approaching. Started attack on these. 0622 C/15 - The cruisers turned away. 0628 C/15 - The battleships were sighted again escorted by four destroyers. 0630 C/15 - It was thought that the cruisers were laying a smoke screen but it was now sighted that one of them was on fire. Started attack on the battleships. 0646 C/15 - In position 36°00'N, 19°06'E fired four torpedoes from 5000 - 6000 yards. After firing P 35 went deep. It was thought one hit was obtained but this was not the case. 0719 C/15 - Returned to periscope depth to find the battleships disappearing to the South in a haze of smoke. Sighted the burning cruiser bearing 263° with a destroyer on either side. Range was 5500 yards. The enemy was proceeding to the West at slow speed. Commenced reloading the torpedo tubes. 0816 C/15 - The destroyers came nearer forcing p 35 to go deep and to proceed to the North. This delayed reloading. 0915 C/15 - Returned to periscope depth to find the cruiser still on fire bearing 159°. The two destroyers were laying a smoke screen. The cruiser was a Trento class ship. Started attack. 1006 C/15 - In position 35°55'N, 18°59'E fired two torpedoes. Both hit. P 35 went deep. Seven depth charges were dropped close to P 35 but they caused no damage. The counter attack ended at 1040 hours. HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) was approaching from the South saw the cruiser break in two and sink in three minutes. 1058 C/15 - Returned to periscope depth. Only the two destroyers were in sight picking up the survivors. Range was 4 nautical miles. P 35 retired to the south. 1141 C/15 - The destroyers got underway proceeding to the northward. P 35 continued to the south on course 180°. 1257 C/15 - Altered course to 360° to regain position. 1510 C/15 - Surfaced to pass P 35's signal timed 1152 C/15 reporting the morning occurrences to the C-in-C Mediterranean. The message was passed at 1543 C/15 and HMS P 35 dived. 1647 C/15 - Surfaced and proceeded on course 300° for position 'D 2' (36°46'N, 16°07'E) in view of the Capt. S 1's signal timed 0825 C/15 which cancelled his 2356 C/14. As HMS P 35 had missed this signal due to the fact that she had been 'otherwise occupied' and so missed this signal until too late, Lt. Maydon decided to make for the final position 'D 2' ordered for operation VIGOROUS. 1840 C/15 - Altered course to 108° for the new patrol position as ordered in the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1315 C/15. 1902 C/15 - Sighted a Spica-class torpedo boat bearing 098° at a range of 8 miles. The enemy appeared to be searching. 1905 C/15 - Dived in position 35°57'N, 18°42'E and altered course to the southwest to evade this enemy vessel. 1935 C/15 - Altered course to 088° in accordance with the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1625 C/15 which ordered the patrol line to be moved 20 miles in direction 050°. 2100 C/15 - Surfaced and proceeded to the newly ordered patrol position. 2142 C/15 - Established position by starsights as 35°58'N, 18°48'E. (22) 16 Jun 1942 Between 0025 C/16 and 0200 C/16, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) sighted numerous distant aircraft flares between bearings 076° and 067°. At 0443 C/16, P 35 altered course to 276° to return to position 'D' (35°57'N, 19°13'E) in accordance with the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2321 C/15. At 0515 C/16, P 35 took starsights and established her position to be 36°04'N, 19°47'E. Course was set to continue to position 'D' to patrol in that vicinity submerged throughout the day. At 2136 C/16, P 35 surfaced in position 35°50'N, 19°07'E. (22) 17 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 1833 C/17, the Capt. S.1 ordered HMS Porpoise (Lt. L.W.A. Bennington, DSC, RN), HMS Thorn (Lt.Cdr. R.G. Norfolk, DSO, RN), HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) to proceeded towards Taranto with despatch and that additional orders would follow. In a follow up signal timed 2015 C/17 the following orders were signalled; My 1835 C/17 adjust speed and time leaving present patrol area so as to arrive by dawn 20th. In yet another signal timed 2101 C/17 the following orders were signalled; Establish patrol by dawn 20th as follows; HMS Thorn in the vicinity of position 40°04'N, 17°13'E HMS Thrasher in the vicinity of position 40°07'N, 17°26'E. HMS Porpoise in the vicinity of position 35°01'N, 16°29'E. 10th Submarine Flotilla was to patrol line; 4 - 39°47'N 17°17'E - 7 miles - 055°. HMS Una in position 'A', 39°47'N, 17°17'E. Remainder in present relative positions. [thus HMS P 31, HMS P 34 and HMS P 35 in positions 'B', 'C' and 'D' respectively.] Proceed direct to new position diving at dawn 20th on line 247° from Cape Maria di Leuca. (24) 19 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 1109 C/19, the Capt. S.1, ordered HMS Porpoise (Lt. L.W.A. Bennington, DSC, RN), HMS Thorn (Lt.Cdr. R.G. Norfolk, DSO, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) in position 'C' and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) to return to Alexandria forthwith through following positions to arrive as stated; HMS Porpoise through positions 35°22'N, 18°11'E and 32°21'N, 28°01'E to arrive at dawn on the 22nd. HMS Thorn through positions 37°21'N, 20°30'E, 35°42'N, 21°57(?)'E and 32°08'N, 28°57'E to arrive at dawn on the 25th. The four boats of the 10th submarine flotilla were to cross Latitude 34.59'N in the following Longitudes; HMS Una in 21°28'E, HMS P 31 in 22°04'E, HMS P 34 in 22°40'E and HMS P 35 in 23°18'E. [More positions to proceed through are given in the signal but it contains several errors]. The four boats were to arrive off the boom before 2030 C/27 or after 0730 C/28. (24) 19 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 2059 C/19, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) and HMS Thorn (Lt.Cdr. R.G. Norfolk, DSO, RN); Establish patrol line forthwith 5 - 38°16'N, 19°47'E - 8 - 150° in the order HMS Una, HMS P 31, HMS P 34, HMS P 35, HMS Thrasher. HMS Una in position 38°16', 19°47'E. HMS Thorn proceeding as ordered in my signal timed 1109 C/19 is not to cross 37°N before 2200 C/20. In a signal timed 2205 C/19, the above listed submarines were informed of the following by Capt. S.1; On reaching position in my 2059 C/19 proceed submerged at 3 knots on course 145° until 2130 hours. HMS Thrasher then return to patrol area keeping at least 10 miles to north east above course, and the four boats of the 10th S/M Flotilla proceed in accordance with my 1109 C/19. It is important that no submarine will be seen on the surface on the 20th. In a signal timed 2259 C/19, the Capt. S.1 informed the submarines listed above of the following; My 2059 C/19. It is important that error in longitude should be reduced to a minimum. HMS Thrasher is to obtain accurate land fix at dawn, dive to position ordered to replace by 1200 hours or earlier if possible and then establish communication by SS/T to enable the other submarines to adjust their position. (24) 20 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 0401 C/20, the Capt. S.1, sent the following orders to HMS Thorn (Lt.Cdr. R.G. Norfolk, DSO, RN), HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); My 2059 C/19 shift patrol line 22 miles from 267°. My 2205 C/19 for 145° read 134°. HMS Thorn conform. In a follow up signal timed 0455 C/20 sent by the Capt. S.1 to HMS Thorn, HMS Una and HMS P 31 and repeated to the other submarines the following orders were given; My 0401 C/20, HMS Thorn patrol along a line between positions 37°22'N, 20°15'E and 36°50'N, 20°59'E. HMS Una and HMS P 31 return to position 37°55'N, 19° 35'E and then steer 134°. In a signal timed 1211 C/20, the following orders were sent by Capt. S.1; HMS Thorn, HMS Una, HMS P 31, HMS P 34 and HMS P 35 return to Alexandria. Expected in accordance with previous orders. HMS Thrasher return to patrol area through 37°56'N, 20°03'E. (25) 21 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 2135 C/21, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to the four submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla; Area 33°00'N, 47°00'E, 23°14'E - 310° - 65 miles is not to be crossed during night 22nd/23rd. HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) pass to southward of area. HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) passing to the south and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) to the north of Gavdo Island. (25) 23 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 1117 C/23, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to the submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla; Proceed to following positions from whence you are expected end of searched channel at the approx. time given but submarines are not to arrive off the boom after 2030 hours or before 0730 hours. HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) through position 32°19'N, 29°08'E to arrive at (the end of the searched channel) 1401 C/27. HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) through position 31°39'N, 29°02'E to arrive at 0459 C/28. HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) through position 31°53'N, 28°55'E to arrive at 0801 C/28. HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) through position 32°01'N, 28°59'E to arrive at 0501 C/29. (25) 27 Jun 1942 Around 1800 A/27, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) ended her 8th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean) at Alexandria. (22) 29 Jun 1942 Around 2130 C/29, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Alexandria for Port Said. (26) 30 Jun 1942 Around 1900 C/30, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) arrived at Port Said from Alexandria. (27) 5 Jul 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) is docked at Port Said. (27) 12 Jul 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) is undocked. (27) 14 Jul 1942 Around 1815 C/14, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Port Said for Haifa.
