(27) 16 Jul 1942 Around 1110 C/16, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed arrived at Haifa from Port Said. (27) 1 Aug 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) departed Haifa for her 9th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to proceed to Malta passing to the north of Crete en-route. She was to proceed through positions 35°18'N, 26°40'E, south of Avgo Island, 35°34'N, 23°12'E and 35°21'N, 16°19'E to arrive in the latter position at 0801 C/11, thence to Malta as will be ordered by the Capt. S.10. For the daily positions of HMS P 35 during this patrol see the map below. (28) 4 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 1059 B/4, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN); You are not to arrive Malta A.M./13 or A.M./14. At your discretion you may proceed on the surface during daylight between 20°00'E and 17°00'E if considered possible arriving searched channel before 1800 B/12, otherwise adjust speed to arrive at 1001 B/15. Report position during the night after crossing 19°00'E and make E.T.A. Malta. (29) 6 Aug 1942 At 2026 C/6, near the north-east tip of Crete, in position 35°21'N, 26°29'E, HMS P 35 (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) sighted two Navigatori-class destroyers in line abreast steering a course of 240° at 20 knots. At 2046 C/6, when 6000 yards on the port beam of the destroyers both turned 090° to port putting HMS P 35 right ahead which quickly dived to 50 feet. The destroyers passed close astern. This were most probably the Italian destroyers Antonio Pigafetta and Giovamni da Verazzano which had sailed from Portolago (Leros) at 1340 B/6-1353 B/6 to lay a defensive minefield off Mersa Matrujh (Egypt). Antonio Pigafetta’s report indicates that they changed course at 1935 B/6 without stating what it was. It is also possible that the P 35 report made an error. The two destroyers laid minefield 'MM' at 0701 B/7-0723 B/7. 172 EMC mines [each destroyer carried 86 mines] from position 31°27'10"N, 27°13'30"E to position 31°24'40"N, 27°17'10"E, and arrived at Suda Bay at 0806 B/8-0815 B/8. (4) 7 Aug 1942 At 0221 C/7, in position 000° - Cape St. John (Agios Ioannis) - 10 miles, sighted a small destroyer or torpedo boat proceeding into the Gulf of Mirabello at about 15 knots. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.] (4) 12 Aug 1942 At 1605 B/12, HMS P 35 (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) ended her 9th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. She was swept in by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) which had joined around 1345 B/12. (4) 20 Aug 1942 At 1715 B/20, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Malta for her 10th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the west coast of Greece between 36°15'N / 40°00'N, east of a line 160° from 40°00'N, 19°00'E and west of 22°30'E. For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 35 during this patrol see the map below. (22) 21 Aug 1942 At 0200 B/21, P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) increased speed in anticipation of the Capt. S.10's reply to the Capt. S.1's signal timed 0020 C/21 which had contained the following; If you concur request P 35 may proceed on surface daylight 21st patrolling in position 10 miles south of Strovathi Island by daylight/22. At 0356 B/21, HMS P 35 stopped zigzagging and proceeded at full speed on course 071° in accordance with the instuction received in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0220 B/21. In a follow up signal timed 0330 B/21, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders; Make every endeavour to be in position 37°06'N, 21°02'E at dawn/22. Full power with no restriction should be used. Daylight air reconnaissance report will he passed. At 0515 B/21, HMS P 35 was in estimated position 35°23'N, 15°07'E. This was 304 miles from the position given in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0330 B/21. It was impossible to reach that position in time. In a signal timed 1756 C/21, the Capt. S.1 sent the following information / orders to HMS P 35; At 1634 C/21, aircraft reported one large merchant, one medium merchant ship and three or four destroyers in position 39°19'N, 20°16'E, course 170°, speed 9 knots. P 35 was to move 7 miles 090° from the position ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0330 B/21. In a signal timed 2205 B/21, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information / orders to HMS P 35; My 0330 B/21. Your target is a large tanker escorted by five destroyers which was stopped by an aircraft attack at 1618 B/21 in position 39°21'N, 20°14'E and escort well shot up by Beaufighters. Maximum speed was 10 knots and cannot pass your patrol position before 1400 B/22. Advance destroyer A/S sweep must be expected, therefore, necessary to fully charge batteries tonight. Further air reconnaissance hoped about dawn which will be passed by me but you are now under operational control of Capt. S.1. Lt. Maydon now considered it just possible to be in position in time. The ships reported had been the convoy consisting of Italian tanker Poza Rica (7799 GRT, built 1940) and the small German transport Dora (795 GRT, built 1898) which had departed Messina at 2250 B/19 for Libya escorted by the Italian destroyers Aviere (escort leader), Camicia Nera and the Italian torpedo boats Climene and Ciclone. Between 1054 B/20 and 1102 B/20, the convoy came under heavy air attack in position 38°29'N, 16°38'E (near Punta Stilo). This was from twelve Beauforts (39 Squadron) and ten Beaufighters (227 Squadron). The torpedoes and bombs were avoided but the convoy were also machine-gunned. Camicia Nera had one killed and thirty wounded and Dora one killed and one wounded. At 1420 B/21, Camicia Nera left the convoy to proceed to Taranto and land her wounded. During the night the destroyer Geniere from Messina joined the convoy to replace her. At 0525 B/21, the torpedo boat Pegaso arrived from Piraeus to reinforce the escort. At 1613 B/21, the convoy came under air attack in position 39°23'N, 20°13'E (entrance of Corfu Channel) from nine Beauforts (39 Squadron), four Beaufighters bombers (227 Squadron) and escorted by eight Beaufighters (248 Squadron). Poza Rica was torpedoed twice and 50 of her crew abandoned ship in two lifeboats. Climene avoided bombs but was hit by MG fire, however suffered no casualties and little damage. Geniere had three killed and sixteen wounded, Aviere had three killed and a number of wounded. At 1728 B/21, Climene took in tow the two lifeboats and half an hour later took the 50 men on board. She was ordered to the Bay of Saiada to await for Poza Rica (which was eventually beached there) to transfer the crew back. At 2020 B/21: Dora arrived at Corfu escorted by Aviere and Pegaso. (30) 22 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 1600 C/22, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); You have freedom of action in areas 'A' and 'S'. The south end of the Zante channel may be good focal area. (29) 23 Aug 1942 At 0503 B/23, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) dived in position 37°28'N, 21°11'E (D.R.). At 0604 B/23, HMS P 35 fixed her position by bearings as being 37°34'N, 21°11'E. During the day she patrolled off the southern entrance to the Zante Channel. At 1740 B/23, HMS P 35 investigated Katakolo harbour. Only one caique was seen alongside the inner end of the southern breakwater. At 2034 B/23, HMS P 35 surfaced in position 37°35'N, 21°11'E. (22) 24 Aug 1942 At 0252 B/24, in position 37°30'N, 21°09'E, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) sighted a destroyer / torpedo boat bearing 160° at a range of about 5 miles. P 35 was well placed down moon and against the dark background of Zante Island, so she remained on the surface keeping stern on so as to be in position to attack should there be and other vessels in company. The enemy passed between 3 and 4 miles astern and across the moon. It was thought to be a Spica-class torpedo boat. Asdic reported 240 revolutions. She disappeared to the westward. Nothing further happened so HMS P 35 resumed patrol on the surface. This was the Italian torpedo boat Orione which sailed from Navarino at 0000 B/24 and arrived at Corfu at 1330 B/24. At 0459 B/24, HMS P 35 dived and patrolled off the southern entrance to the Zante Channel. At 1726 B/24, HMS P 35 altered course and proceeded southwards in accordance with the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1305 C/24. At 2016 B/24, HMS P 35 surfaced in position 37°27'N, 21°17'E and proceeded towards the northern section of the south-west approaches to the Anti-Kithera Channel. (22) 24 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 1305 C/24, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Proceed forthwith diving by day to patrol within 50 miles of the southern approach to the Antikithera Channel. (29) 24 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 1750 C/24, sent by Capt. 1, HMS Turbulent (Cdr. J.W. Linton, DSC, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) are ordered to patrol within 60 miles from position 35°45'N, 23°24'E. HMS Turbulent in sector 160° to 210°. HMS P 35 in sector 210° to 260°. (29) 25 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 0955 C/25, the Capt. S.1 sent the following information / orders to HMS Turbulent (Cdr. J.W. Linton, DSC, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); My 1550 C/24. Heavy A/S patrols expected. Until further orders keep to seaward in your areas. (29) 26 Aug 1942 At 2240 B/26, in position 35°38'N, 23°01'E, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) sighted a small merchant ship with two destroyers bearing 060° at a range of about 3 miles. Enemy course was approximately 200°. HMS P 35 turned stern on to evade the enemy as this must have been the ships she was ordered not to attack as in the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1603 C/16. [See the other 'event' listed for 26 August 1942 for the text of this signal.] At 2251 B/26, HMS P 35 dived as the enemy was still closing and the bright full moon made the visibility excellent. Enemy speed was estimated to be 10 knots. At 2300 B/26, the enemy altered course to 150°. At 2315 B/26, the enemy was steering now a course of about 250°. One destroyer was heard to transmit on Asdic. At 2342 B/26, only one destroyer was visible now on bearing 267° steering westwards. At 2352 B/26, HMS P 35 surfaced and continued patrol. This was most probably the Dielpi (Italian, 1527 GRT, built 1912) carrying petrol and escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Generale Antonino Cascino which sailed from Suda at 1115 B/26. At 1840 B/2, they were attacked by a large number of low-flying aircraft and Dielpi was machine-gunned and hit by two torpedoes and broken in two, she quickly sank. The torpedo boat was also machine-gunned which put her radio out of use and proceeded to pick up the 60 survivors, 7 were missing. She arrived at Benghazi at 0815 B/26. Note: only one torpedo boat escorted Dielpi not two. The Cascino report does not mention another escort. (22) 26 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 1506 C/26, sent by Capt. 1, it was stated that an enemy convoy had left the Gulf of Athens. HMS Turbulent (Cdr. J.W. Linton, DSC, RN) was ordered to patrol in position 35°07'N, 23°27'E and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) was ordered to patrol in position 35°39'N, 23°07'E. They were to arrive in their positions by 0300 C/27. If nothing had been sighted by 0630 C/27, HMS Turbulent was to proceed towards the Anti-Kithera Channel. In a signal timed 1603 C/26, sent by Capt. 1, it was stated that an enemy convoy made up of one merchant vessel and a destroyer had left Suda Bay. This convoy was expected to pass HMS Turbulent and HMS P 35 between 2300 C/26 and 0200 C/27. This convoy was not to be attacked as bigger ships had been reported coming south. The convoy in question was most likely the one made up of the Italian merchant vessel Dielpi (1520 GRT, built 1912) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Generale Antonio Cascino. They had departed Suda Bay around 1205 B/26. In a signal timed 1915 C/26, sent by Capt. 1, the submarines were informed that at 1130 C/26, a convoy made up of a 7000 tons tanker and two 5000 tons merchant vessels had been seen 10 miles south of Piraeus. They had been escorted by two destroyers. The tanker was most likely the Italian tanker Giorgio (4887 GRT, built 1907) which had departed Piraeus around 0900 B/26 for Tobruk. She was escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Partenope. No merchant vessels should have been with the tanker at this moment though. In a signal timed 2052 C/26, the Capt. S.1 sent the follwing orders to the submarines; If you get an attack or sight and fail to attack act as follows: P 35 retire to the south-east to the edge of your area. Turbulent retire to the west at 3 knots but do not approach within 10 miles of the edge of your area. Further southbound convoys have been sighted in the Aegean and another chance of attack may develop P.M./27. (29) 27 Aug 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) torpedoed and heavily damaged the Italian transport Manfredo Campiero (5463 GRT, built 1925) west of the Anti-Kithera Channel in position 35°41'N, 23°01'E. This vessel was part of a convoy from Piraeus bound for Benghazi and also included the Italian transport Tergestea (5890 GRT, built 1926). The convoy was escorted by the Italian destroyer Nicoloso da Recco and the Italian torpedo boats Climene and Polluce. Manfredo Campiero was hit by a torpedo aft and a second torpedo was observed missing her stern by about 10 metres. She began to burn furiously. The torpedo boat Polluce sent a boarding party of seven with fire extinguishers but she appeared beyond salvage. The torpedo boat finished her off by gunfire. Four had been killed or missing, 255 survivors were picked up including 37 wounded, one of them dying later. In the meantime Nicoloso da Recco and Climene hunted the submarine and dropped depth charges, the destroyer claiming a sinking. The Manfredo Camperio/Tergestea convoy and it's escort sailed from Piraeus at 1655 B/26-1704 B/26. After the sinking of Manfredo Camperio, Polluce landed her survivors at Piraeus at 2132 B/27. Tergestea escorted by Nicoloso da Recco and Climene arrived at Benghazi at 1100 B/28-1155 B/28. Later the same day three torpedoes were fired against a large escorted merchant vessel near the same position but no hits were obtained. This was the Italian transport Unione (6070 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the destroyers Folgore and Saetta. They had sailed from Piraeus for Benghazi. Two underwater explosions were felt which were thought to be torpedoes exploding at the end of their run but no torpedo tracks were seen though before these explosions. Unione, escorted by the destroyer Folgore sailed from Piraeus at 0030 B/27. At 1300 B/27, they were reinforced by the destroyer Saetta, which had sailed from Navarinon at 0606 B/27. They arrived at Benghazi at 1500 B/28-1527 B/28. 0003 B/27 - Sighted a single destroyer bearing 080°. Asdic reported 230 revolutions. Enemy course was 240°. P 35 closed on course 120° to see if there were any ships in company. 0009 B/27 - Dived. The destroyer was apparently alone. The enemy passed ahead on course 200°. 0020 B/27 - The destroyer was now nearly out of sight on bearing 170°. Enemy course was now 170°. 0040 B/27 - Surfaced in position 35°39'N, 23°07'E. 0345 B/27 - Sighted two vessels bearing 080°. Altered course to close to attack. 0400 B/27 - Altered course to 070° and closed at full speed. The vessels had the appearance of a small 2000 tons tanker and one destroyer. Enemy speed was about 15 knots. Their course varied between 025° and 320° which seemed odd. 0408 B/27 - The targets were now identified as a large destroyer heavily dazzle painted and a smaller destroyer or torpedo boat. P 35 altered course to 260° and withdrew at slow speed. One of the enemy vessels was transmitting on Asdic. The enemy appeared to be carrying out an A/S patrol. 0535 B/27 - P 35 fixed her position by bearings as 35°37'N, 23°11'E. 0700 B/27 - Sighted a destroyer bearing 035°. Altered course to 090. 0712 B/27 - Sighted two motor launches painted very pale grey bearing 280°. They were proceeding to the north-east at a range of about 4 miles. 0720 B/27 - Sighted two merchant ships escorted by a Navigatori class destroyer and a Spica class torpedo boat bearing 025°. Enemy course was 245°, speed 10 knots. Started attack. 0737 B/27 - Sighted two more destroyers / torpedo boats hull down to the North-East. 0742 B/27 - Sighted two aircraft over the convoy. 0748 B/27 - In position 35°39'N, 23°05'E fired four torpedoes at the nearer of the two merchant vessels. Range was 3000 yards. One, possibly two hit(s) was / were obtained. The second torpedo fired did not run. 0757 B/27 - Depth charging started. 0821 B/27 - The last depth charges were dropped, 29 in all were counted but no damage was done to P 35 which was at 180 feet. 0850 B/27 - Returned to periscope depth. Sighted one of the Merchants down by the stern and burning fiercely. She was circled by a Spica class torpedo boat. 0900 B/27 - Commenced reloading. No.2 tube still contained its torpedo although it had been fired. No.2 tube was now out of action. 1235 B/27 - The merchant ship blew up and sank. The torpedo boat was seen to proceed to the North-East. Between 1310 B/27 and 1330 B/27 - P 35 passed through considerable wreckage including two lifeboats and six rafts. No names could be seen on these lifeboats. 1410 B/27 - Sighted a large 10000 tons merchant vessel, with a single funnel and of modern design escorted by two destroyers / torpedo boats of the Navigatori / Partenope classes. Enemy course was 270°, speed 12 to 14 knots. Started attack. 1420 B/27 - The enemy altered course away. 1424 B/27 - Enemy course was now seen to be 225°. 1429 B/27 - In position 35°35'N, 23°03'E started firing a salvo of three torpedoes (Tube 2 was out of action). Just as the second torpedo was fired the target was seen to alter course. The third torpedo was fired regardless in the hope it was just a zig. It was not a zig and the target steadied on a course of 170°. No hits were obtained. 1445 B/27 - Altered course to 270° and proceeded. As there was no only one effective torpedo remaining it was decided to return to Malta. 2010 B/27 - Surfaced in position 35°36'N, 22°50'E and set course to proceed to Malta. P 35 also passed her signal timed 1606 B/27 which contained the following; Six torpedoes expended. Have one defective torpedo. One 5000 ton southbound ship sunk in position 35°39'N, 23°05'E. Sighted one 10000 ton ship escorted by two destroyers in position 35°36'N, 23°03'E at 1430 B/27 proceeding on course 210° at 12 knots. Am returning to base through position 35°36'N, 23°00'E direct to position 35°22'N, 15°13'E. (22) 27 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 1630 C/27, the Capt. S.1 signalled the following information to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Aircraft report a merchant vessel on fire at 1105 B/27 in position 35°48'N, 23°15'E. This information came not as a surprise for P 35 as this referred to the Italian transport Manfredo Campiero which had been torpedoed and damaged earlier that morning by HMS P 35. In a signal timed 1806 B/27, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 35; Unless otherwise ordered by Capt. S.1, leave patrol as requisited P.M./28 and proceed through positions 35°22'N, 19°59'E, 35°22'N, 14°12'E and 35°32'N, 14°12'E to make rendezvous with escort (sweeper) at the end of the searched channel at 0929 B/2. (29) 28 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 1046 B/28, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); My 1806 B/27 and your 1606 B/27. Proceed direct to position 35°06'N, 15°01'E thence as previously ordered to arrive at the end of the searched channel at 1001 B/1. Only if unable to comply make E.T.A. during night 29/30 August. You are not to arrive end of the searched channel after 1730 hours. (29) 1 Sep 1942 At 1145 B/1, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) ended her 10th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. She was escorted in by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) which had joined around 0930 B/1. (31) 11 Sep 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) conducted compass adjustment trials at Malta. At 1500 B/11, she then departed Malta for her 11th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the west coast of Greece. She was escorted out until 1740 B/11 by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR). Before they parted company, HMS P 35 made a practice attack on the minesweeper. (4) 11 Sep 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 35 during her 11th war patrol see the map below. 12 Sep 1942 Late in the evening, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) developed a serious engine defect forcing her to abandon her patrol. Course was set to return to Malta. In a signal timed 2236 B/12, HMS P 35 sent the following to the Capt. S.10; Circulated water cross connection port side fractured. Returning to base by reverse of route in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1640 B/11. E.T.A. Calimore Point 1600 B/13. Proceeding on the surface by day. (4) 13 Sep 1942 Around 1500 B/13, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) arrived back at Malta where she was taken in hand for repairs. (4) 14 Sep 1942 After repairs, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Malta around 0845 B/14 to resume her 11th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Ionian Sea near the Greek west coast. She was routed as follows; through position 35°35'N, 15°57'E at about 2015 B/14 direct to arrive in position 36° 40'N, 20°35'E, about 0201 B/18 to operate in Ionian area 'S'. She was swept out until 1120 B/14 by HMS Rye (A/Lt.Cdr. J.A. Pearson, DSC, RNR). Before they parted company HMS P 35 made a practice attack on the minesweeper. (4) 16 Sep 1942 Shortly after midnight, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) once again developed engine problems. These could not be repaired on board and once again course was set to return to Malta. In a signal timed 0036 B/16, P 35 informed the Capt. S.10 of the following; No. 5 starboard bottom end bearing running. Returning by reverse of outward route, expect me at end of searched channel at 0955 B/18. The Capt. S.10 replied as follows in his signal timed 0326 B/18; Your 0036 B/16 acknowledged. HMS Talisman (Lt.Cdr. M. Willmott, DSO, RN) due same time. Further instructions will follow. In a signal timed 1115 B/16, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 35 and HMS Talisman; My 0326 B/16 to P 35. On entering Sanctuary approach rendezvous with the minesweeper about 1 miles south-west of position 5 in Q.B.B. 273. Talisman keep north west and P 35 south-east line 250° from that position and maintain S.S./T. communication from first contact until ordered to surface about 1101 B/18. HMS Utmost (Lt. A.W. Langridge, RN) will leave western corner of sanctuary about 2159 B/17 thence direct arrive in position 'D' on Q.B.B.225 route about 2001 B/18. Submarines encountered during the night of 17/18 are not to be attacked. (4) 18 Sep 1942 Around 1400 B/18, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) arrived back at Malta where she was, once more, taken in hand for repairs. She was swept in for part of the way, from 1145 B/18 to around 1230 B/18 by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR). The minesweeper then turned back to the rendezvous position to await HMS Talisman (Lt.Cdr. M. Willmott, DSO, RN) which had not shown up at the rendezvous at the expected time. HMS Talisman was however lost during the passage from Gibraltar to Malta, cause unknown but probably mined. (4) 23 Sep 1942 After repairs, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Malta, around 1425 B/23, to resume her 11th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean). She was escorted out until around 1750 B/23 by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) on which practice attacks were made before they parted company. (4) 23 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 2301 B/26, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Patrol about 10 miles south-west of Strovathi Island until further orders. (32) 27 Sep 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) torpedoed and damaged the Italian transport Francesco Barbaro (6343 GRT, built 1940) about 35 nautical miles south of Cape Marathia, Zakynthos Island, Greece in position 37°04'N, 20°36'E. The enemy vessel later sank some 50 nautical miles south-west of Cape Marathia, Zakynthos Island, Greece in position 37°15'N, 19°55'E after a second attack by HMS P 35. [Position as per Italian sources]. The convoy attacked by P 35 was made up of the above mentioned Francesco Barbaro (coming from Brindisi) as well as the Italian transport Unione (6070 GRT, built 1942) (coming from Taranto). They were escorted by the Italian destroyers Giovanni da Verazzano and Lampo as well as the Italian torpedo boats Partenope, Clio, Lince and Aretusa. When Francesco Barbaro was torpedoed Unione made off escorted by da Verazzano, Lince and Aretusa. Lampo, Partenope and Clio remained with Barbaro. Barbaro had been hit in the bow in the first attack, she was taken in tow but sank at dawn the following day. Of a complement of 278 (223 Italians and 55 Germans), 248 survivors were picked up (125 by Clio and 123 by Lampo), 30 were killed or missing. Unione escorted by the destroyer Giovanni da Verazzano and the torpedo boats Lince and Aretusa sailed from Taranto at 1937 B/26-2000 B/26. Francesco Barbaro escorted by the destroyer Lampo and the torpedo boat Partenope sailed from Brindisi at 2100 B/26-2116 B/26. The torpedo boat Clio, delayed by defects to her condenser, sailed at 0130 B/27 and joined the convoy at 0610 B/27. At 0751 B/27, the two convoys merged. At 1642 B/27, Francesco Barbaro was torpedoed. Giovanni da Verazzano ordered Lampo to take her in tow and Clio and Partenope to hunt the submarine. Clio dropped two 100-kg and six 50-kg depth charges. After Barbaro sank, Lampo, Partenope and Clio proceeded to Navarinon where they arrived at 1047 B/28-1056 B/28. Unione proceeded escorted by Giovanni da Verazzano, Lince and Aretusa. At 2245 B/27, they were attacked by torpedo bombers. Unione was narrowly missed. At 0105 B/28, a new attack by torpedo bombers scored one hit on the starboard of the merchant ship in position 35°20'N, 21°20'E. She was abandoned but Giovanni da Verazzano took her in tow. The tow line broke at 0855 B/28 after several attempts, towing was finally resumed at 0800 B/28. At 1723 B/28-1735 B/28, Clio and Partenope sailed from Navarinon to reinforce the convoy at 2050 B/28. At 0553 B/29-0645 B/29, they arrived at Navarinon. Eventually, Lampo, Partenope, Aretusa and Clio sailed from Navarinon at 1505-1600 B/29. Partenope and Aretusa arrived at Benghazi at 1131-1205 B/30, Lampo and Clio arrived at 2400 B/30. 27 September 1942 1540 B/27 - Sighted aircraft circling bearing 280°, range about 8 nautical miles. Assumed this to be part of the escort of the expected convoy. Increased to full speed to get into that area. 1602 B/27 - Sighted two large merchant vessels escorted by five destroyers / torpedo boats and aircraft bearing 285°. Started attack. 1630 B/27 - The nearest merchant vessel was chosen as the target. She was 8000 to 10000 tons in size. Range was estimated as being 6000 yards. It was thought that six destroyers / torpedo boats were escorting this convoy. Enemy course was 150°, speed 14 knots. 1633 B/27 - In position 37°04'N, 20°36'E fired four torpedoes from 8000 - 9000 yards. One hit was obtained. After firing P 35 went deep, changed course to 300° and made a burst at full speed for 5 minutes. 1643 B/27 - A bomb exploded fairly close. Altered course to 270°. More aircraft bombs, about 10 in total, followed up to 1757 hours. 1706 B/27 - Returned to periscope depth. The target was seen bearing 176°. She was making much black smoke and slightly down by the stern. She appeared to be stopped with a destroyer nearby. As aircraft were all around went to 50 feet and proceeded on course 200°. 1713 B/27 - Started reloading the torpedo tubes. 1733 B/27 - The target was no longer smoking. Altered course to 240°. 1734 B/27 - A serious counter attack was started by the enemy. P 35 went to 200 feet and took evasive action. 1752 B/27 - Target was now bearing 155°. 1757 B/27 - Three destroyers / torpedo boats were now within 4000 yards. 1825 B/27 - Returned to periscope depth. One destroyer / torpedo boat was 1500 yards astern and coming towards. Went to 200 feet again. Eleven depth charges were dropped quite close causing some minor damage. 1903 B/27 - Six depth charges were dropped very close. 1936 B/27 - Altered course to 250° to get to the westward in order to have advantage of the rising moon. Completed the reload of two torpedo tubes. 2006 B/27 - Surfaced in position 37°06'N, 20°28'E. The target was still afloat laying stopped bearing 070°, range about 4 nautical miles. Three destroyers / torpedo boats were circling her about 2 miles off. P 35 kept within visual range to attack during the night if possible. 2225 B/27 - After having closed on the surface, dived to attack again. 2230 B/27 - In position 37°09'N, 20°27'E fired two torpedoes from 6000 to 8000 yards at the stationary target. Upon firing P 35 went to 120 feet and altered course hard to starboard. One explosion was heard after almost 10 minutes giving a much greater range. 2252 B/27 - The three destroyers / torpedo boats were seen to the north-east about 5 miles away. 2310 B/27 - Ships were sighted bearing 079°, 073°, 067° and 061°. The last was the target. Reloaded the other two torpedo tubes. 28 September 1942 0034 B/28 - Altered course to 360° to get down moon again. 0045 B/28 - Sighted two destroyers / torpedo boats bearing 044° and 058°. 0050 B/28 - Surfaced in position 37°07'N, 20°21'E. The target was still afloat but was enveloped in a veil of smoke. The escorts were still circling her. P 35 remained in the area diving at 0437 B/28 in position 37°03'N, 20°35'E. Shortly before 0500 B/28 a ship was heard sinking. When P 35 returned to periscope depth shortly after 0600 B/28 nothing was in sight. 0645 B/28 - P 35 was now in position 37°01'N, 20°19'E. Course was set to 245° to return to Malta. 1940 B/28 - P 35 surfaced in position 36°51'N, 19°30'E and passed her signal timed 0644 B/28 in which she reported her results and the expenditure of six torpedoes and her intention the return to Malta. (4) 27 Sep 1942 In a signal 1120 B/27, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); My 2301 C/26. Estimate you should he about 15 miles west of position given in order to intercept. At 1000 B/27, an aircraft from Malta, had reported a convoy made up of two medium motor vessels (not exceeding 10000 tons) escorted by six destroyers in position 38°30'N, 20°10'E proceeding on course 170° at 15 knots. [For the composition of this convoy see the other event for 27 September 1942.] In a signal timed 2030 B/27, the Capt. S.10 cancelled his signal timed 2301 B/26. (32) 1 Oct 1942 At 1415 B/1, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) ended her 11th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. She was escorted in by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) which had joined around 1155 B/1. (4) 7 Oct 1942 At 1335 A/7, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Malta for her 12th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the southern Adriatic. P 35 first patrolled in the Cape Dukato - Corfu area and only went north through the Otranto Straits on the 12th after HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN) left the Adriatic. She was escorted out until 1705 A/7 by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN). Before they parted company HMS P 35 made a practice attack on HMS Beryl (?) which was also in the area. (4) 7 Oct 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 35 during her 12th war patrol see the map below. 10 Oct 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) attacked an Italian destroyer with three torpedoes in the Ionian Sea. No hits were obtained. This was most probably Nicolò Zeno who had sailed from Patras at 0010 B/10 on the 10th and arrived at Messina at 1930 B/10. 0739 A/10 - Sighted a vessel thought to be a cruiser bearing 085°, estimated enemy course was 285°. Asdic reported enemy revolutions as 170. Altered course to intercept and increased speed to 8 knots. 0748 A/10 - Enemy bearing 081°, range 7800 yards. Enemy course was now seen to be 260°. Altered course to port to attack from the enemy's starboard bow. 0755 A/10 - The enemy was identified as a Navigatori-class destroyer still doing 170 revolutions. She was making no bow wave and speed was thought to be 12 knots. 0802 A/10 - The target appeared to be increasing speed as a bow wave was now sighted. In position 37°53'N, 18°54'E fired three torpedoes. All missed astern as it was later calculated that the destroyer was doing around 20 knots. The target was out of sight 25 minutes later. 1932 B/10 - Proceeded northwards towards the Otranto Straits. (4) 10 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 2019 A/10, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Patrol line Cape Dukato - Cape Maria di Leuca until necessary to comply with my 1037 A/8 in which signal P 35 had been ordered to leave Ionian Sea area 'C' after 2001 A/11 through position 39°40'N, 19° 00'E to make the submerged northward passage Otranto Straits during daylight/12. (33) 11 Oct 1942 At 0230 B/11, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) arrived on the line between Cape Dukata and Cape Santa Maria di Leuca and commenced patrolling on this line in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2019 B/10. At 1818 B/11, HMS P 35 surfaced in position 39°19'N, 19°42'E and proceeded on course 312° at 5 knots to pass between minefields Q.B.B. 100 and Q.B.B. 109. (4) 12 Oct 1942 At 0430 B/12, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) dived to proceeded north through the Straits of Otranto. At 1833 B/12, HMS P 35 surfaced in position 40°31'N, 18°44'E. At 2050 B/12, HE was picked up thought to be of a fast motor boat proceeding at 240 revolutions. Bearing was 290° moving aft. P 35 turned away. Between 2102 B/12 and 2111 B/12, HE appeared to be approaching on bearings from 280° to 270°, still doing 240 revolutions. At 2115 B/12, HMS P 35 dived. HE was now bearing 263°. P 35 was gradually turned back to course 360°. Ten minutes later the HE stopped. At 2249 B/12, HMS P 35 surfaced and proceeded to the north-west. (4) 12 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 1013 A/12, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) of the following; HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN) reports look out port on eastern shore very alert, cleverly camouflaged motor gun boat and Yugoslav torpedo boat carried out A/S patrol but shipping inefficiently escorted and depth charge set shallow. Two northbound merchant ships in position 40°29'N, 18°41'E were seen at dawn/11. All other shipping was coast crawling. (33) 13 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 0959 A/13, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Interested to know night patrol situation off Brindisi between 5 and 10 miles from ports. At your discretion remain on surface between those limits for three hours on one suitable night. (33) 14 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 0048 A/13, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Move immediately to close eastward of port mentioned in my 0959 A/13 [Brindisi] and remain in vicinity until 1200 A/14 unless attack occurs. (33) 14 Oct 1942 At 1040 A/14, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) sighted masts and funnels of two merchant ships bearing 168°. These were thought to be leaving Brindisi harbour. P 35 altered course to 200° and closed the coast submerged at 6.5 knots to gain a suitable attack position. At 1101 A/14, it was established that these two merchant vessels were still in the harbour although they were making considerable smoke. P 35 resumed course 180°. At 1215 A/14, an Italian destroyer, thought to be of the Aviere-class was sighted on bearing 285° approaching on course 120° down the searched channel. P 35 turned to 080° to keep clear. At 1224 A/14, the merchant vessels in the harbour were seen to make more smoke then before. It was considered they would be underway soon and that the destroyer was to escort them. HMS P 35 turned to 200° to gain a suitable attack position. At 1240 A/14, the attack was abandoned as the ships were still in harbour and the destroyer was now turning to enter the gate. P 35 altered course to 090°. At 1310 A/14, HMS P 35 fixed her position as being 40°44'N, 17°57'E. At 1500 B/15-1530 B/15, the transport Etiopia (Italian, 2153 GRT, built 1918) sailed from Brindisi, escorted by the torpedo boat Pallade. They arrived at Taranto at 1150 B/16-1314 B/16. At 1645 B/15, the transport Chisone (6168 GRT, built 1922) sailed from Brindisi and arrived at Taranto at 0910 B/16. Perhaps this had been the merchant vessels sighted inside the harbour making smoke. The 'destroyer' sighted was most likely the torpedo boat Aretusa which had sailed from Taranto at 0635 B/14, escorting the transport Lucrino (4372 GRT, built 1917) to Bari. The torpedo boat left her at the entrance of the harbour at 1050 B/14 and sailed from there at 1055 B/14. She arrived at Brindisi at 1428 B/14. [Note the one hour difference in time zone !] (4) 15 Oct 1942 During daylight hours, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) made the submerged southbound passage of the Straits of Otranto by taking the Italian coastal convoy route. (4) 17 Oct 1942 At 1849 A/17, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) sighted a lighted bearing 120°. Course was altered towards and speed was increased to full speed. At 1858 A/17, the light was now seen to be white with another red light and a blur of a green light. P 35 slowed down to 3.5 knots to listen for hydrophone effect (HE). At 1901 A/17, P 35 again went to full speed to close. At 1918 A/17, while in position 36°49'N, 16°49'E, the contact was identified as a hospital ship with two funnels. The vessel was fully lit in accordance with the Hague Convention. Range was about 6 to 8 miles bearing 115°. Course was 320° which was in the direction of Messina. Estimated speed of the hospital ship was 12 knots. HMS P 35 altered course to 230° to keep clear. The only movement of a hospital ship in this area was the Italian Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1928) which sailed from Naples at 1100 B/16 and arrived at Tripoli at 0845 B/18 but she was sailing in the opposite direction. So perhaps there was an error in the patrol report or perhaps the hospital ship had made a temporary change in course. (4) 19 Oct 1942 At 1245 A/19, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) ended her 12th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. She, and HMS P 247 (Lt. M.G.R. Lumby, DSC, RN), were escorted in by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC, RNR) which had joined around 1000 A/19. (4) 21 Oct 1942 At 1645 A/21, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Malta for her 13th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to destroy a damaged and grounded enemy ship off Khoms, Libya. She, and HMS P 247 (Lt. M.G.R. Lumby, DSC, RN) were escorted out by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) until 1845 A/21. (4) 21 Oct 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 35 during her 13th war patrol see the map below. 23 Oct 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) torpedoed and destroys the grounded Italian transport Amsterdam (10873 GRT, built 1921) off Khoms, near Tripoli, Libya. She had been hit by an aerial torpedo on 15 October. She also sank the Italian tug Pronta (182 GRT, built 1913) that was trying to salvage the Amsterdam. The Amsterdam was grounded after being hit by a torpedo during an air attack. During the attack by P 35 30 men lost their lives on board the two ships. 0651 A/23 - Fixed our position as 32°44'N, 14°16'E. Closed Khoms roads. 0720 A/23 - Observed the situation in Khoms roads as follows. The 8500 tons merchant ships was seen anchored and aground in position 093°, Khoms Cemetery Lighthouse, 7.1 cables. A small tug was nearby. An armed trawler of about 500 tons was anchored about 4 cables to the Eastward. The merchant ships was unloading her cargo in lighters. 0832 A/23 - P 35 now retired to the North. It was intended to return just before dark to make a final reconnaissance. Then to withdraw on the surface to charge. Then to return after midnight making use of the 'sinking' moon. 1406 A/23 - As the weather was getting worse it was decided to close submerged and attack with torpedoes at once. 1433 A/23 - In position 32°39'5"N, 14°18'5"E fired no. 2 tube at a salvage tug at anchor. The torpedo had either missed or ran under. 1439 A/23 - Fired tubes 3 and 4 at the merchant ship. Both hit. The ship settled further but did not sink or catch fire. The small craft alongside were demolished. 1440 A/23 - Proceeded out of Khoms Bay at periscope depth. An aircraft then started machine gunning P 35 so went to 40 feet and changed course. 1505 A/23 - Some small bombs were dropped during the next twelve minutes. Went to 80 feet and proceeded on course 360° at 5 knots. 1714 A/23 - Returned to periscope depth. Reduced speed to 3 knots. Position was fixed by bearings as being 32°56'N, 14°22'E. Set course to return to Malta. (4) 25 Oct 1942 At 1200 A/25, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) ended her 13th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. She was escorted in by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) which had joined around 1000 A/25. (4) 2 Nov 1942 Around 1000 A/2, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Malta for her 14th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the North of Sicily to provide cover during the Allied landings in North Africa (Operation Torch). She departed Malta together with HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) and HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN). They were escorted out until around 1730 A/2 by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN) when the escort parted company in position 36°05'N, 14°01'E. P 35 was to patrol in position 38°27'N, 15°31'E. HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) was to patrol in position 38°42'N, 15°15'E. The submarines were to patrol in these positions from dawn/5 and were to exchange billets every 48 hours with the first change being effected by 0300 A/7. The dividing line between these areas being a line drawn 020° from Cape Milazzo light. (4) 2 Nov 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 35 during her 14th war patrol see the map below. 3 Nov 1942 At 0528 A/3, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) dived and proceeded on course 295°, speed 4.5 knots so as to pass submerged through Q.B.B. 255 (mined aread) At 0537 A/3, HMS P 35 fixed her position by bearings as being 37°23'N, 12°45'E. HMS P 35 then went to 120 feet. At 0601 A/3, course was altered to 309°. At 0736 A/3, HMS P 35 came to periscope depth and fixed her position as being 37°29'N, 12.34'E. Smoke was sighted on bearing 335°. HMS P 35 did not investigate but went back to 120 feet. At 1100 A/3, HE was heard. This were two fast engined craft on bearings 190° and 150°. Speed was reduced to 3 knots and HMS P 35 shut off for depth charge attack. At 1130 A/3, the HE was fading on the starboard quarter on bearing 110°. It was considered to be two motor torpedo boats crossing astern. This were probably the Italian motor torpedo boats MAS 530 and MAS 531 which had departed Trapani at 0310 A/3, to give protection for the tug Nereo (340 GRT, built 1936) and the naval auxiliary Lago Tana (783 GRT, built 1940), and to do an A/S search. The above mentioned tug and naval auxiliary were to move the buoy "Avventura" to another position. They had departed Trapini at 0230 A/3-0250 A/3. At 0020 A/3, the minesweeper RD 20 had sailed from Trapani to determine the exact position of the buoy "Avventura". At 0315 A/3, Marina Messina informed Marina Trapani that an enemy unit had been detected by radiogoniometry after she signalled Malta in position 251° - Marettimo - 31 miles (approximately 37°50'N, 11°31'E). This was the signal timed 0215 A/3 from HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) reporting her safe passage through Q.B.B. 255 to Malta. When the enemy report was received, the two MAS boats, MAS 530 and MAS 531 were diverted to search for the submarine and Lago Tana, Nereo and RD 20 to return to port. They arrived at Trapani at 0625 A/3-0745 A/3. At 0420 A/3, MS 22 and MS 24 also sailed to join the A/S search. In the meantime the naval auxiliary Lago Zuai (783 GRT, built 1940) had sailed from Trapani for Pantelleria but returned at 0650 A/3 and MAS 560 sailed at 0845 A/3 and arrived at Trapani at 0945 A/3. At 1040 A/3, MS 22 and MS 24 arrived at Trapani, followed at 1205 A/3 by MAS 530 and MAS 531, nothing had been sighted. At 1550 A/3, MS 35 and MS 36 sailed from Mazara to sweep the area west of the island of Zembra, in anticipation of the minelaying operation by the three destroyers and torpedo boat (see below). At 1141 A/3, speed was increased to 4,5 knots. Course was now 305°. At 1328 A/3, HMS P 35 came to periscope depth and fixed her position as being 37°43'N, 12°01'E (south of Marettimo Island). The masts and funnel of a southbound merchant ship were sighted bearing 103°. It was estimated the size of the merchant ship was about 2000 to 4000 tons. Range was about 7 miles. This ship was not attacked as HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) and HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) were following HMS P 35 at 10-mile intervals. A tripod mast, probably of a destroyer was sighted on bearing 261° on an approximate course of 120°. A seaplane was sighted on bearing 139° which was patrolling ahead of the merchant vessel. This was probably the refrigeration vessel Emilio (Italian, 191 GRT, built 1912) which sailed from Trapani unescorted at 1200 A/3 and arrived at Tripoli at 2330 A/6. [There is a big difference in the estimated size of the vessel sighted and the actual size of this vessel. However the ship was sighted through the periscope at long range.] No escort vessel operated in this area at this time as far as we could determine. The destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Nicolo Zeno and Antonio da Noli and the torpedo boat Castelfidardo sailed from Trapani at 1822 A/3-1825 A/3 to lay a minefield. The torpedo boat Cigno was at Trapani on this day but sailed only the next day to proceed to Palermo. At 1447 A/3, HMS P 35 returned to periscope depth and fixed her position as being 37°45'N, 11°57'E. At 1455 A/3, course was altered to 350°. Speed was reduced to 3 knots. At 1654 A/3, HMS P 35 fixed her position as being 37°50'N, 11°56'E. At 2005 A/3, HMS P 35 surfaced in position 37°59'N, 11°54'E (west of Marettimo Island). At 2022 A/3, HE was heard on bearing 075°. 220 Revolutions. P 35 stopped both generators to listen. At 2029 A/3, in position 38°00'N, 11°54'E, a small patrol trawler was seen approaching on bearing 085°. P 35 dived to 80 feet and proceeded on course 270°. HE of two vessels was heard on bearings 010° and 315° doing 220 revolutions. P 35 was turned slowly to 360° at slow speed, proceeding dived until the HE faded out astern. At 2130 A/3, HMS P 35 surfaced in position 38°03'N, 11°55'E. At 2203 A/3, HE was heard on bearing 120°. This was from a slow reciprocating engine doing 160 revolutions. Stopped both generators to listen. At 2205 A/3, both generators were started up again and HMS P 35 proceeded at full speed for a quarter of an hour on course 360° to get clear. At 2220 A/3, HMS P 35 reduced speed to 6 knots. Very faint HE was still audible bearing 150°. HMS P 35 resumed her mean course of 360° but now she was zigzagging. At 2300 A/3, course was altered to 072° and speed as requisite for charging batteries. (4) 4 Nov 1942 At 0500 A/4, in position 38°25'N, 12°40'E (north-north-west of Cape St. Vito), HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) sighted a small darkened vessel bearing 090°. Faint HE was picked up on bearing 130° doing 140 revolutions. This may have been transport Mariannina (Italian, 1262 GRT, built 1885) which sailed from Palermo at 2230 A/3 and arrived at Trapani at 0900 A/4, she passed Cape San Vito around 0500 A/4. At 1024 A/4, HMS P 35 fixed her position as being 38°32'N, 13°00'E (south-west of Ustica Island). At 1415 A/4, in position 38°34'N. 13°10'E, a small steamer of about 500 to 1000 ton was sighted bearing 350°. Enemy course was 170°. The enemy was doing 200 revolutions. It is considered that this was the Ustica ferry. She was not worth molesting. At 1811 A/4, HMS P 35 surfaced in position 38°34'N, 13°23'E and proceeded on a mean course of 080°, zigzagging, making good 5.2 knots while charging. At 2000 A/4, speed was increased to 9.5 knots. At 2337 A/4, the position was fixed as being 38°44'N, 14°17'E. (4) 5 Nov 1942 At 0020 A/5, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) dived for a suspected patrol craft seen west of Filucudi Island. No HE was picked up and it was considered this was not a patrol craft but Canno Rock. At 0054 A/5, HMS P 35 surfaced in position 38°46'N, 14°29'E and proceeded on course 095° to make the passage between Panarea and Salina Islands. At 0517 A/5, dived for the day having just completed the passage between the two earlier mentioned islands. At 0554 A/5, HMS P 35 fixed her position as being 38°34'N, 15°06'E. At 1239 A/5, HMS P 35 was estimated to be in position 38°27'N, 15°31'E which was her assigned patrol position. At 1453 A/5, in position 38°25'N, 15°34'E, two hospital ships were sighted. They were proceeding northwards up the Calabrian coast. The leading ship shortly afterwards changed course to 270° and at 1530 A/5, she passed at 11 knots (100 revolutions) at a range of 1000 yards from P 35. The vessel was identified as being the Italian hospital ship Virgilio (11718 GRT, 1928). The identification was correct, Virgilio had sailed from Tobruk at 1655 A/3 and arrived at Naples at 0712 A/6. At 1540 A/5, the second ship, thought to be the Italian hospital ship Gradisca (13868 GRT, built 1913), altered to approximate course 310° and an hour later she was disappearing on bearing 324°. This was indeed the Gradisca which was carrying the wounded from the battle of El Alamaein. She had sailed from Mersa Matruh at 1630 A/2, and at some time joined Virgilio, and arrived at Naples at 0730 A/6. At 1640 A/5, in position 38°25'N, 15°28'E, the masts and smoke of a small northbound coaster were sighted bearing 276°. It was thought this vessel may be proceeding to Lipari Island. At 1657 A/5, an object was sighted which was thought to be a submarine. HMS P 35 closed to attack. At 1704 A/5, the target was identified as an MA/SB (Stefano Turr-type). Evasive action was taken. Position was 38°25'N, 15°36'E. At 1711 A/5, HE was heard which was thought to be coming from two craft doing 240 revolutions. They passed down the port side after which the HE was gradually fading. At 1723 A/5, an MA/SB was sighted disappearing to the southward on course 185°. At 1845 A/5, HMS P 35 surfaced in position 38°33'N, 15°33'E. She then withdrew to the north-west while charging the batteries. At 2340 A/5, HE was picked up on bearing 120° doing 240 revolutions. It sounded similar to the HE picked up at 1711 A/5 and was considered to be another MA/SB. At 2349 A/5, HMS P 35 dived after it had been considered the enemy was conducting a night A/S patrol. Position was 38°28'N, 15°27'E. (4) 6 Nov 1942 At 0022 A/6, the HE first picked up at 2340 A/5, faded out on bearing 140° (toward the north end of the Messina Strait) after HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) had dived. However it now re-appeared on bearing 150° apparently circling. It was suspected there was also very faint HE at 150 revolutions, almost drowned by the former. At 0109 A/6, the HE closed and crossed astern bearing 150°, finally fading altogether on bearing 250°. HMS P 35 withdrew to the northwards and remained dived until 0300 A/6 in view of the possibility of further patrols and as batteries were fully charged. At 0310 A/6, HMS P 35 surfaced and the boat was ventilated and the battery charge was completed. At 0506 A/6, HMS P 35 dived in position 38°31'N, 15°30'E. At 0634 A/6, in position 38°31'N, 15°47'E, a small southbound merchant vessel was sighted close to the Calabrian shore on bearing 090°. This vessel looked like the Lorenzo Marcello (1413 GRT, built 1928). [We have been unable to identify this vessel.] At 0642 A/6, HMS P 35 fixed her position as being 38°31'N, 15°41'E. At 0913 A/6, in position 38°26'N, 15°36'E, masts and smoke of a small eastbound vessel was seen to the north of Cape Peloro on bearing 180° at a range of about 8 miles. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.] At 1655 A/6, HMS P 35 fixed her position as being 38°29'N, 15°27'E. At 1845 A/6, HMS P 35 surfaced in position 38°33'N, 15°28'E. Course was set for the north at slow speed until latitude 38°42'N was reached, thence west to position 38°42'N, 15°15'E so as to exchange positions with HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) in accordance with the patrol orders. At 2343 A/6, HMS P 35 dived for a dark object which was sighted to the north-west. This turned out to be Strombolicchio Rock (to the north-east of Stromboli) though. (4) 8 Nov 1942 At 1735 A/8, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) fixed her position as being 38°45'N, 15°19'E. At 1846 A/8, HMS P 35 surfaced and proceeded south at slow speed to exchange positions with HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN). At 1900 A/8, in position 38°41'N, 15°36'E, a brightly lighted southbound ship was sighted off Cape Vaticano. This was thought to be a hospital ship. This was probably the Italian hospital ship Gradisca (13868 GRT, built 1913) which sailed from Naples at 0230 A/8 and arrived at Tobruk at 0800 A/11. At 2300 A/8, HE (150 revolutions) was picked up. The light of a small craft was seen proceeding westwards. (4) 9 Nov 1942 At 0505 A/9, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) heard HE (210 revolutions) of a fast reciprocating engine bearing 230° and crossing to port on an easterly course. At 0613 A/9, HMS P 35 dived. No more HE was heard at this time. At 0629 A/9, HMS P 35 fixed her position as being 38°28'N, 15°32'E. At 0713 A/9, in position 38°20’N, 15°25’E, a small eastbound tanker was seen bearing 195°. The enemy vessel was of about 1000 tons and was escorted by two flying boats. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.] At 1638 A/9, HMS P 35 fixed her position as being 38°25'N, 15°35'E. At 2046 A/9, HMS P 35 surfaced in position 38°33'N, 15°35'E and patrolled in the vicinity. (4) 10 Nov 1942 At 0500 A/10, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) dived. At 0623 A/10, HMS P 35 fixed her position as being 38°35'N, 15°35'E. At 1051 A/10, when in position 38°25'N, 15°38'E, a small tanker was seen bearing 164°. The enemy was proceeding northbound coming from the Straits of Messina. It was probably the same ship that had been seen at 0713 A/9. She was escorted by flying boats. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.] At 1518 A/10, in position 38°25'N, 15°30'E a small westbound coaster was seen bearing 140°. The enemy was seen leaving the Straits of Messina. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.] At 1717 A/10, in position 38°28'N, 15°38'E, a small southbound coaster was seen off Cape Vaticano. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.] At 1840 A/10, HMS P 35 surfaced in position 38°32'N, 15°32'E and proceeded north at slow speed to exchange positions with HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN). (4) 11 Nov 1942 At 0141 A/11, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) fixed her position as being 38°46'N, 15°20'E. At 0502 A/11, HMS P 35 dived. At 0604 A/11, HMS P 35 fixed her position as being 38°37'N, 15°11'E. At 0900 A/11, in position 38°43'N, 15°12'E, a ship similar to the Italian transport Lorenzo Marcello (1413 GRT, built 1928) was sighted bearing 250° proceeding on course 310°. The enemy vessel was passing through the Panarea / Salina Channel. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.] At 1200 A/11, HMS P 35 fixed her position as being 38°42'N, 15°21'E. At 1224 A/11, HMS P 35 set course to proceed to the eastward in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1022 A/11 which ordered HMS P 35 and HMS P 43 (Lt. A.C. Halliday, RN) to move 5 miles 090° from their present positions. Three enemy cruisers were expected to move northwards through the Straits of Messina. At 1842 A/11, surfaced in position 38°42'N, 15°26'E. At 1912 A/11, a brightly lighted northbound vessel was seen to the eastward. This was probably a hospital ship but it was not possible to close sufficiently to identify the vessel. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.] Between 2100 A/11 and 2115 A/11, a large number of red aircraft recognition flares were fired to the south-west. Also the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1754 A/11 was received which instructed HMS P 43 to move out of the area and HMS P 35 to carry out a railway demolition on the coast. At 2255 A/11, three dark objects were seen bearing 030°. These appeared to proceed in approximate direction 320°. No HE was heard. HMS P 35 chased at full speed but was unable to close. At 2323 A/11, the chase was abandoned. P 35 then set course for the Gulf of St. Euphemia to reconnoitre for landing the special boat party. As no reconnaissance had so far been carried out it was praticable to make a landing during the night of 11/12 November as had been suggested by the Capt. S.10. (4) 12 Nov 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) attacked an Italian battleship of the Littorio-class to the west of Cape Vaticano in position 38°39'5"N, 15°44'5"E with four torpedoes. No hits were obtained. Battleships Littorio, Roma and Vittorio Veneto escorted by the destroyers Aviere, Corsaro, Legionario, Bombardiere, Mitragliere, Ascari, Velite and Corazziere had sailed from Taranto at 0000 A/12-0156 A/12. At 2030 A/11-2135 A/11, the destroyers Alpino, Camicia Nera, Bersagliere and Granatiere had sailed from Messina to reinforce the escort. At 1423 A/12, they were in position 38°08'N, 15°30'E, when an Italian fighter aircraft crashed in the sea, and Camicia Nera was detached at 1430 A/12 to pick up the pilot which she did before rejoining the squadron. At about 1500 A/12, they crossed the Straits of Messina and about an hour later, Corazziere, which had defects, was detached to Messina, arriving at 1723 A/12. At the time of the attack, the squadron was made up of the Littorio, Roma and Vittorio Veneto, screened by Aviere, Corsaro, Legionario, Bombardiere, Mitragliere, Ascari, Velite, Alpino, Granatiere, Camicia Nera, Bersagliere and perhaps Corazziere (but this is not certain). Granatiere and Alpino returned to Messina at 2135 A/12-2207 A/12 and Bersagliere at 2400 A/12. The squadron was making 20 knots at the time of the attack which was not observed but they were informed at 1824 A/12 that they had been sighted by an enemy submarine (this was when P 35 had radioed an enemy report). They arrived at Naples at 0400 A-0820 A/13. 0137 A/12 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2359 A/11 which instructed HMS P 35 to remained east of 15°10'E and make two railway raids and also attack southbound shipping. 0456 A/12 - HMS P 35 dived and proceeded into the Gulf of St. Euphemia. 0648 A/120 - HMS P 35 sighted three motor schooners bearing 160°. They were steering 330° and probably had left Vibo Valentino Marina. These vessels were proceeding in starboard quarter line at about 8 knots and had single sweep wires over the stern. Two were seen to be armed with light automatic AA guns. 0705 A/12 - HMS P 35 fixed her position as being 38°46'N, 16°07'E. Proceeded close inshore to examine the beach and locality of the railway between Pizzo and the bridge over the Angitola River. This place was chosen as a likely spot for railway demolition. A careful periscope examination of the shore was made from within a mile of the shore. 0824 A/12 - When in position 38°46'N, 16°10'30"E and sounding 28 fathoms, HMS P 35 turned away and proceeded out of the bay. 0914 A/12 - HMS P 35 sighted the three schooners seen earlier returning on course 110°. 1236 A/12 - HMS P 35 fixed her position as being 38°50'N, 16°03'E. The Capt. S.10's signal timed 1109 A/12 was received which ordered HMS P 35 to return to her original patrol position, which was 38°27'N, 15°31'E. if within 20 miles of it. Italian battleships had left Taranto. HMS P 34 was actually 34 miles away but Lt. Maydon decided to increase speed to 5 knots and endeavour to get close north of his ordered position, at any rate being off Cape Vaticano by 1600 A/12. 1453 A/12 - Fixed position as being 38°43'B, 15°52'E. 1556 A/12 - Sighted a vessel bearing 190°. Started attack. Altered course to 260° and increased to full speed to get clear to westward from Cape Vaticano. 1600 A/12 - Enemy bearing by Asdic 180°. IT was surprising that the enemy HE could be heard on Asdic when the submarine was doing full speed submerged (500 revolutions). 1603 A/12 - Identified the enemy as two battleships of the Littorio-class screened by at least twelve destroyers. Three aircraft were seen overhead. Enemy course was estimated as 335°. 1606 A/12 - Asdic gave speed of heavy ships, bearing 175°, as 250 revolutions, 28 knots. 1608 A/12 - The battleships were now bearing 172° and turning to starboard. P 35 turned to port to keep the bow on. 1610 A/12 - Enemy course was estimated as being 007° and was now thought to have steadied. 1612 A/12 - Bearing of the leading battleship was now 160°, range 7300 yards, course 035°. 1615 A/12 - The leading battleship was now bearing 145°. P 35 turned to 090° and shifted target to the second battleship in line to give more time. 1619 A/12 - In position 38°39'30"N, 15°44'45"E fired four torpedoes at the second battleship in the line from a range of 4000 yards. Went to 120 feet after firing and altered course to get off the torpedo's tracks. No hits were obtained and no counter attack followed. 1625 A/12 - Picked up the HE of a destroyer passing close from 120° to across the stern (030°). 1629 A/12 - Heard an explosion after a little over 10 minutes after firing. This was probably a torpedo hitting the shore north of Cape Vaticano. 1652 A/12 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight. 1700 A/12 - Altered course to 270° to clear the coast before surfacing to make an enemy report. 1710 A/12 - In position 38°38'N, 15°46'E, sighted a single southbound destroyer bearing 135°. This was most likely the Corazziere after having been detached and proceeding to Messina with defects (see above). 1753 A/12 - Surfaced in position 38°40'N, 15°42'E to pass an enemy report which was timed 1619 A/12. 1804 A/12 - Message passed. Dived and commenced reloading the torpedo tubes. 1916 A/12 to 1935 A/12 - Sixteen distant explosions were heard. 2200 A/12 - Reloading was completed. There had been a delay due to one torpedo having a defective pistol which could be repaired. 2224 A/12 - Surfaced in position 38°45'N, 15°25'E. (4) 13 Nov 1942 At 0445 A/13, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) dived in position 38°49'N, 15°35'E. At 0811 A/13, HMS P 35 fixed her position as being 38°50'N, 15°39'E. At 0819 A/13, six distant explosions were heard. At 0827 A/13, smoke was sighted on bearing 150°. P 35 altered course to close. At 0917 A/13, seven distant explosions were heard. At 1044 A/13, the source of the smoke was identified as three armed trawlers patrolling on a northerly course. Position was 38°43'N, 15°40'E. [We have been unable to identify these vessels.] At 1100 A/13, the first two trawlers turned to approximate course 140° and the third one to 350°. This trawler was using Asdic. At 1215 A/13, HMS P 35 fixed her position as being 38°44'N, 15°38'E. At 1529 A/13, smoke was sighted bearing 153°. HMS P 35 closed to intercept. At 1540 A/13, In position 38°46'N, 15°36'E, a northbound merchant ship was sighted bearing 157°. Enemy course was estimated to be 326°. This ship was subsequently identified as the Italian hospital ship Virgilio (11718 GRT, 1928). Enemy speed was 9.5 knots. A practice attack was made on this hospital ship. This was indeed Virgilio. She sailed from Derna at 1500 A/11 and arrived at Naples at 0700 A/14. At 1656 A/13, HMS P 35 fixed her position as being 38°46'N, 15°38'E. At 1839 A/13, HMS P 35 surfaced and patrolled to the south-west. (4) 14 Nov 1942 At 0458 A/14, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) dived. At 0621 A/14, HMS P 35 fixed her position as being 38°29'N, 15°27'E. At 0752 A/14, in position 38°28'N, 15°28'E, the Asdic fitted trawler, seen the previous day, was seen again. She passed to the westward along the Sicilian coast then turned east at about 0900 A/14 and returned steering 090° and passing about a mile distant down the starboard side on opposite course to P 35. When on the submarines starboard quarter at 0946 A/14 she turned again and closed on a course of 230° but HMS P 35 evaded her to the north and she was finally shaken off to the south-east. No depth charges were dropped. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.] At 1042 A/14, P 35 fixed her position as being 38°30'N, 15°35'E. At 1300 A/14, course was set for a reconnaissance in the Gulf of St. Euphemia. The weather was now suitable to do so. At 1332 A/14, HMS P 35 fixed her position as being 38°32'N, 15°32'E. At 1420 A/14, in position 38°32'N, 15°40'E, a hospital ship was sighted bearing 062° which was thought to be the Italian Gradisca (13868 GRT, built 1913). This hospital ship was northbound off Cape Vaticano. This was indeed Gradisca, which had sailed from Derna at 1400 A/12 and arrived at Naples at 0740 A/15. At 2043 A/14, HMS P 35 surfaced in position 38°47'N, 15°49'E and proceeded further into the Gulf of St. Euphemia. At 2308 A/14, HE was heard bearing 210°. Stopped the generators to listen. This was probably a patrol trawler on a northerly course to the west of the Gulf, doing 140 revolutions. The HE crossed astern and was last heard bearing 285°. (4) 15 Nov 1942 In the early hours of 15 November 1942, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) landed a party of two (Corporal J. Booth of the Seaforth Highlanders and Marine R.R. Didcott. Both were members of the SBS = Special Boat Service) in a folbot to destroy a railway to the North of Pizzo, Italy. They successfully landed but the presence of a sentry and extensive barbed wire prevented them from reaching the railway line and they abandoned the attempt. 0003 A/15 - Stopped both main engines when it was estimated P 35 was in position 270° - Mouth of the Angitola River - 3.5 miles. No.2 and 3 Main ballast tanks were blown to get the for hatch well clear of the water. 0016 A/15 - P 35 stopped and the fore hatch was opened. The Folbot and necessary gear was lifted on the fore casing. 0025 A/15 - The fore hatch was shut and P 35 was trimmed down again and proceeded on course 090° at 6 knots. 0042 A/15 - Sighted the shoulder of the hill marking the chosen landing beach. Altered course to 105°. 0056 A/15 - Sounding showed a depth of 76 fathoms. 0059 A/15 - A southbound train was seen crossing the bridge. The night was ideal, with a flat sea with a very slight swell and a moderate off-shore breeze which would effectively dispell ant inadvertent noise from the submarine. 0103 A/15 - A northbound train passed. 0109 A/15 - A southbound train passed. Soundings now showed 31 fathoms. 0114 A/15 - Soundings now gave 20 fathoms. Speed was reduced to dead slow. 0118 A/15 - Soudings now gave 14 fathoms. Stopped both motors. 0124 A/15 - P 35 stopped in a water depth of 6 fathoms. The Folbot was launched and left the submarine four minutes later when P 35 also retired into 15 fathoms of water. 0138 A/15 - A northbound train passed. 0145 A/15 - The Folbot returned. The party had landed successfully but there was a sentry box on the crest of the beach about 20 yards from where they had landed. The sentry box was found to be empty but there was a figure, probably the sentry, proceeding away, some 50 yards off. Wire was encountered between the beach and the railway line. Corporal Booth did not consider the obstacles encountered justified an attempt to proceed with the enterprise so he decided rightly to withdraw and return to the submarine. 0152 A/15 - A northbound train passed. P 35 retired into deeper water before striking down the Folbot. 0205 A/15 - A northbound train passed. 0212 A/15 - All gear was stowed and the hatch was shut. P 35 set course to leave the Gulf of St. Euphemia on course 280° at 6 knots. 0519 A/15 - P 35 dived in position 38°48'N, 15°50'E. 0630 A/15 - P 35 fixed her position as being 38°46'N, 15°41'E. During the night the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2032 A/14 was received with directed P 35 to patrol, if two or more torpedoes remained, to patrol within 20 miles from the original patrol position (38°27'N, 15°31'E). Course was therefore set for this position. 1200 A/15 - P 35 fixed her position as being 38°33'N, 15°36'E. The Capt. S.10's signal timed 0936 A/15 was received which contained the following orders; Establish patrol forthwith in vicinity position 170° - 38°27'N, 15°31'E - 4 miles, if you consider this position tenable. You are only to attack north-westbound large merchant ships or cruisers. 1237 A/15 - Sighted smoke. This appeared to be originating from small coasters proceeding close inshore. 1600 A/15 - P 35 fixed her position as being 38°23'N, 15°35'E. 2040 A/15 - Surfaced in position 38°27'N, 15°28'E. Withdrew to the northwest while charging the batteries. (4) 16 Nov 1942 At 0451 A/16, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) dived and closed the ordered patrol position. At 0638 A/16, HMS P 35 fixed her position as being 38°22'N, 15°38'E. At 0640 A/16, in position 38°32'N, 15°39'E, a southbound motor torpedo boat was seen to enter the Straits of Messina. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.] At 1156 A/16, HMS P 35 fixed her position as being 38°25'N, 15°28'E. At 1700 A/16, HMS P 35 fixed her position as being 38°25'N, 15°36'E. At 2042 A/16, HMS P 35 surfaced in position 38°33'N, 15°33'E. She then withdrew to the north to charge the batteries. At 2120 A/16, a ship, which was burning lights was seen bearing 003°. Closed to investigate. At 2135 A/16, in position 38°36'N, 15°37'E, this ship was identified as the Italian hospital ship Virgilio (11718 GRT, 1928). P 35 withdrew to the west. This was indeed Virgilio which sailed from Naples at 1030 A/16 and arrived at Tripoli at 0840 A/18. (4) 17 Nov 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) torpedoed and damaged the Italian troop transport Piemonte (15209 GRT, built 1918) 4 nautical miles bearing 310 of Capo Rasocolmo, Sicily, Italy in position 38°21'N, 15°28'E. Piemonte, escorted by the torpedo boat Nicola Fabrizi, sailed from Palermo at 0338 A/17. At 1317 A/17, Piemonte was hit by a torpedo at the extreme stern. Nicola Fabrizi hunted the submarine until 1557 A/17, when three MAS boats arrived to relieve her. It was believed that she had inflicted slight damage to the submarine and she arrived at Messina at 1940 A/17. Piemonte was also escorted by a seaplane (Cant Z.501 no.1 of 189^Squadriglia) at the time of the attack. As said Piemonte was hit aft and was subsequenlty beached near Cape Rasocolmo. The crew took to three lifeboats, they had three wounded. The seaplane dropped two bombs on the submarine. Fabrizi, after dropping intimidation depth charges (30kg), dropped the more potent 100kg depth charges set to a depth of 50-75 metres) then combed the area with a towing mine but result was uncertain. 0440 A/17 - Dived and set course to return to the ordered patrol position. 0704 A/17 - P 35 fixed her position as being 38°23'N, 15°40'E. 1100 A/17 - In position 38°20'N, 15°28'E, two eastbound merchant ships of about 2000 ton were sighted escorted by a General-class torpedo boat were seen passing to the southwards. These were the transports Honestas (Italian, 4959 GRT, built 1920) and Vertunno (3159 GRT, built 1905) escorted by the torpedo boat Giuseppe Dezza which sailed from Palermo at 2145 A/16 and arrived at Messina at 1340 A/17. 1207 A/17 - While in position 38°23'N, 15°28'E sighted a large column of black smoke bearing 250°. Closed to investigate. 1240 A/17 - In position 38°22'N, 15°27'E, the smoke was now seen to come from a two-funnelled passenger ship thought to be of 7000 tons. She was escorted by a Generale-class torpedo boat and a flying boat. Enemy course was 078°. Started attack. 1259 A/17 - The enemy was now bearing 230°. Enemy course was 090° at 11 knots. Range was 4850 yards, distance of track 2500 yards. P 35 was closing on course 200°. 1313 A/17 - P 35 altered course to 150°. 1316 A/17 - In position 38°21'N, 15°28'E fired two torpedoes from 3000 yards. Went to 120 feet after firing. After 2 minutes and 35 seconds a torpedo explosion was heard and HE of the target ceased. A counter attack with 2 bombs and 20 depth charges followed. The bombs were rather close but caused no damage. The depth charges were further away. 1320 A/17 - HE of the target bearing 150° ceased. 1334 A/17 - Heard faint HE, possibly the target under way again. 1345 A/17 - The last depth charge was dropped. The torpedo boat was heard to be searching astern. 1428 A/17 - Returned to periscope depth. Sighted the torpedo boat bearing 189° about 4 nautical miles off. Two aircraft were also patrolling the area. P 35 went deep again and cleared the area to the north. 1510 A/17 - P 35 fixed her position as being 38°25'N, 15°27'E. 1847 A/17 - P 35 surfaced in position 38°36'N, 15°27'E. P 35 turned to course 300° with her head into the sea. Weather conditions had deteriorated. Efficient lookout from the bridge was impossible. 2035 A/17 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1819 A/17 which stated that P 35 had been sighted at 1330 A/17. Also orders were received to patrol close east of Ustica Island. Lt. Maydon decided to pass south of Stromboli and thence direct to this new patrol area. 2300 A/17 - The sea moderated slightly and speed could be slightly increased. (4) 18 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1331 A/18, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); 1) Leave patrol after dark tonight through position 38°23'N, 12°28'E and position 'B' (37°48'N, 11°44'E) to arrive in position 'F''at dawn/20th. Thence on the surface to rendezvous with sweepers at 1335 A/20. 2) My 1620 A/17, convoy may leave Palermo tonight and offer you an attack. 3) HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) passes Q.B.B. 255 westbound during daylight 19th at 80 ft. The signal 1620 A/17 from the Capt. S.10 had contained the following information amongst orders for other submarines [which are not included here]; Reconnaissance shows three large merchant ships entered Palermo 1000 today and four destroyers arriving now. Consider ships will sail for Bizerta after dark, either two convoys of about 14 and 10 knots or all at 10 knots. Convoy vitally important. (34) 18 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1912 A/18, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) informed the Capt. S.10 that her arrival at Malta would be delayed by 24 hours. (4) 20 Nov 1942 At 2330 A/20, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) sighted a submarine 5 miles on the port beam which was thought to be either HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) or HMS Turbulent (Cdr. J.W. Linton, DSO, DSC, RN). Avoiding action was taken. At 2334 A/20, south-south-west of Licata, Sicily, in position 36°37'N, 13°36'E, HMS P 35 sighted HMS Una on an opposite course. Avoiding action was taken to the westward. (35) 21 Nov 1942 At 1545 A/21, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) ended her 14th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. She, and HMS Turbulent (Cdr. J.W. Linton, DSO, DSC, RN), were swept in by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC and Bar, RNR) which had joined around 1130 A/21. (4) 30 Nov 1942 Around 1315 A/30, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Malta for her 15th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the Tunisian coast between Hammamet and Kerkennah in the vicinity of Kerkennah No.4 buoy (34°53'N, 11°47'E) until 0900 A/2 when she was to proceed north to patrol in an area bounded by the Tunisian coast and 11°20'E, between latitudes 35°30'N and 36°10'N. The port of Sousse was to be given particular attention. She was escorted out by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR) until 1530 A/30. (4) 30 Nov 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 35 during her 15th war patrol see the map below. 1 Dec 1942 Around 1900 A/1, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) arrived near Kerkennah No.4 buoy and patrolled in that vicinity during the night. (4) 2 Dec 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) sank the Italian transport Sacro Cuore (1097 GRT, built 1899) with gunfire about 15 nautical miles east of Mahdia, Tunisia in position 35°27'N, 11°22'E. She was on passage from Tripoli (departed at 0000 A/1) to Trapani. Twenty-one survivors reached Sousse in a lifeboat. 0904 A/2 - As no shipping had been encountered off Kerkennah, course was altered to 350° to proceed to the northwards to seaward of Mahedia as it was anticipated that enemy southbound shipping would probably keep well out of sight of the coast proceeding by direct route from Pantelleria to Kerkennah No.4 buoy. 1201 A/2 - Sighted smoke bearing 205°. This appeared to be a small coal-burning northbound merchant ship, too distant to intercept submerged. 1325 A/2 - Sighted smoke bearing 224°. 1425 A/2 - Smoke now bearing 240°. P 35 altered course to 360° to close. 1621 A/2 - Sighted funnel and masts with smoke bearing 250°. Increased speed to close. 1727 A/2 - Surfaced in position 35°23'N, 11°32'E to close the small merchant vessel. Enemy course was estimated as 330°, speed 7 knots. 1826 A/2 - Closed the enemy's starboard quarter and turned to a parallel course while overtaking. Position was now 35°27'N, 11°22'E. 1830 A/2 - Opened fire from 300 yards with the 12 pdr. gun. The target was soon stopped and on fire aft. When a boat was seen abandoning ship fire was checked. Eleven rounds had now been fired. 1838 A/2 - Started picking up survivors. They turned out to be Italians and German Luftwaffe men. The Luftwaffe men were retained on board as well as one other German. Ten Germans in all were taken prisoner. 1919 A/2 - Fired ten rounds into the waterline of the ship to finish her off. She was left on fire and settling in the water. 1923 A/2 - Proceeded to get clear of the area. 2100 A/2 - Set course to Malta to land the German survivors shortly afterwards. 2335 A/2 - Passed P 35's signal timed 2150 A/2 to the Capt. S.10 informing him of the intention to return to Malta with the prisoners. (4) 2 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 1603 A/2, the Capt. S.10 sent the following to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Aircraft report at 1400 A/2 two merchant ships of 5000 tons, one 6-inch cruiser, one destroyer and one sloop in position 35°21'N, 11°32'E, course 180°, speed 10 knots. This signal however was only received at 1759 A/2 when plotting showed that the reported enemy convoy had passed about 11 miles to the west of P 35 around 1430 A/2. This referred to the following convoy; Convoy 'C', made up of the transports Chisone (6168 GRT, built 1922), Veloce (5437 GRT, built 1911) and the Italian tanker Devoli (former Yugoslavian Perun, 3177 GRT, built 1939) escorted by Italian torpedo boats Lupo, Aretusa and Sagittario sailed from Naples at 2145 A/30-2320 A/30. At 0930 A/1, the Italian torpedo boat Ardente sailed from Palermo and joined the convoy off Trapani at 1630 A/1 and Devoli was detached to Trapani. At 1954 A/1, Sagittario was forced to leave the formation because of defects and arrived at Trapani at 0025 A/2. From 2130 A/1 to 0417 A/2, numerous flares were observed and several aircraft flew over the convoy without attacking it. At 1917 A/2, a single flare was seen near the convoy which began to make smoke. At about 2000 A/2, near Kerkennah, the convoy, which had split into two groups, Veloce and Lupo to the east of Chisone, Ardente and Aretusa was attacked by four Albacore torpedo bombers of 821 and 828 Squadron (FAA). Veloce was hit astern and set afire. At 2105 A/2, Lupo signalled that she would stand by her and ordered the other three to proceed. At 2346 A/2, Ardente observed gunfire from the direction of Lupo and assumed that she was being attacked by a naval force. Lupo and Veloce had been attacked by three or four destroyers and sunk.
