Because, the vessels were laying smoke, Ardente lost sight of Chisone and Aretusa she increased speed to 24 knots to interfere with the enemy force should it try to deal with Chisone. At 0635 A/3, two German Junker 88 were observed and one of them released its bombs before landing in the sea. The torpedo boat picked up its crew. From 1208 A/3, survivors of Veloce and Lupo were seen, and Ardente began to pick them up. At 1528 A/3, an enemy bomber appeared. Antiaircraft was opened fire from a distance of 3000 metres, and the torpedo boat avoided a torpedo. At 1635 A/3, the last survivor was picked up; in all 29 were from Lupo and 72 from Veloce, there were 135 victims from Lupo and 63 from Veloce. Supermarina ordered Ardente to return to Trapani. Chisone and Aretusa arrived at Tripoli at 1830 A/3-1845 A/3. Convoy 'C' had departed Naples on 30 November bound for Tripoli. It was made up of the Italian transports Chisone (6168 GRT, built 1922) and Veloce (5437 GRT, built 1911) and the Italian tanker Devoli (former Yugoslavian Perun, 3177 GRT, built 1939). The convoy was escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Lupo, Aretusa and Sagittario. The Italian torpedo boat Ardente joined on 1 December and Sagittario was detached also on 1 December. The convoy was attacked by torpedo bombers and the Veloce was sunk near Kerkennah on 2 December. Lupo which was picking up her survivors was sunk by a British destroyer force (see below for the composition of this destroyer force). P 35 was also informed that Allied surface forces would be operating to the southward (Kerkennah area) during the night of 2/3 December. This referred to the destroyers HMS Jervis (Capt. A.L. Poland, CB, DSO and Bar, DSC, RN), HMS Javelin (Lt.Cdr. W.F.N. Gregory-Smith, DSO and Bar, DSC, RN or Lt.Cdr. J.M. Alliston, DSC, RN), HMS Kelvin (Cdr. M.S. Townsend, OBE, DSC and Bar, RN) and HMS Nubian (Cdr. D.E. Holland-Martin, DSC, RN) which had departed Malta around 1400 A/2 and returned there around 0900 A/3. (36) 3 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 1305 A/3, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); HMS Welshman (Capt. W.H.D. Friedberger, RN) is approaching swept channel from south eastward A.M./4. She is informed of your movements. Four British MTB's will arrive at Malta from south-east around 0701 A/4 are ordered not to attack submarines. (37) 4 Dec 1942 Around 1245 A/4, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) ended her 15th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. She was swept in by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) which had joined around 1045 A/4. (4) 5 Dec 1942 Around 1300 A/5, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Malta for her 16th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the Tunisian coast. She was to patrol in an area bounded by the Tunisian coast and Longtitude 12°00'E between Latitudes 35°10'N and 36°10'N. She was escorted out by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN) until 1450 A/5. (4) 5 Dec 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 35 during her 16th war patrol see the map below. (4) 6 Dec 1942 At 1826 A/6, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) surfaced in position 35°28'N, 11°39'E and proceeded on course 270°. At 1930 A/6, course was altered to 330° and speed was increased to 7.5 knots on receipt of the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1309 A/6 which said that P 35 may find it profitable to be lose of Sousse daily at dawn. (4) 7 Dec 1942 At 0130 A/7, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) altered course to 270° and proceeded into the Gulf of Hammamet. At 0608 A/7, P 35 dived close to Sousse with the MDU (Mine Detector Unit) running until 1730 A/7 while in the vicinity of Sousse. At 0835 A/7, P 35 fixed her position as being 35°57'N, 10°41'E. She then proceeded to investigate Sousse harbour. The French national flas was seen to be flying from the Citadel. Possibly there was a small steamship in the harbour and a schooner of about 800 tons was moored off the mole. After reconnoitring the harbour HMS P 35 patrolled to the eastward of Sousse. At 1826 A/7, HMS P 35 surfaced in position 35°56'N, 10°55'E and during the night she patrolled to the north east of Sousse to close the port again at dawn the following morning. (4) 8 Dec 1942 At 0603 A/8, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) dived in position 35°59'N, 10°45'E. At 0820 A/8, P 35 fixed her position as being 35°56'N, 10°44'E. She then patrolled between Sousse and Kuriat. At 1400 A/8, P 35 went to 50 feet as periscope depth patrol became very difficult due to the worsening weather conditions. At 2046 A/8, P 35 surfaced in position 35°44'N, 11°25'E. Heavy rain squalls were experienced during the night. Lt. Maydon decided to proceeded to the vicinity of Mahedia to patrol there the following day. At 2130 A/8, P 35 closed the coast south of Hammamet to intercept an important southbound target which was expected around daylight on the 9th. (4) 9 Dec 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) grounded near Hammamet. With some effort she came off a little over an hour later. Later the same day, HMS P 35 torpedoed and sank the German transport Süllberg (1663 GRT, built 1912) south of Hammamet, Tunisia in position 36°14'N, 10°32'E. Süllberg was carrying 645 tons of ammunition, 62 tons of stores, 4 guns and 23 vehicles from Trapani (sailed at 1415 A/8) to Sousse. The tug Vega was sailed to her assistance but Süllberg had already sunk. 0405 A/9 - Started sounding which gave a depth of 36 fathoms. Lt. Maydon gave orders to reduce speed to 6 knots at 0500 A/9 when the estimated position would be 36°19'30"N, 10°38'E. 0445 A/9 - Sound gave a depth of 33 fathoms which agreed with the charted soundings for the estimated position P 35 was in. Visibility was very variable due to rain squalls and at its worst visibility was very bad. Also P 35 was rolling heavily due a fairly heavy sea and swell which effected the accuracy of the echo sounder. 0451 A/9 - Grounded. The motors were put to full astern but P 35 was unable to get off. Main ballast was also blown to obtain full buoyancy but this also did not help. 0505 A/9 - Lt. Maydon ordered all main ballast to be flooded again to ensure P 35 was not driven further ashore by the breaking sea and that the bow was kept onto the land. Instructions were now given to drain down the torpedo tubes and pump out all torpedo compensating tanks and auxiliary tanks with the exception of tank 'X' which is right aft and was to be flooded fully. At the same time demolition charges were placed and fuses prepared to destroy the submarine if necessary and a start was made on the burning of signal publications and confidential matter. 300 Gallons of water were pumped out from forward leaving sufficient to continue the patrol when the submarine was reloated. 0550 A/9 - All tanks empty with the exception of 'X' auxiliary, fresh water, fuel and lubricating oil. Blew No.1, 2, 3 and 4 main ballasts using high pressure air. Also went full astern. 0552 A/9 - Blew No.5 and 6 main ballast. 0554 A/9 - Dropped the drop keel. P 35 now broke loose. Proceeded astern and then turned the bow through south. 0615 A/9 - P 35 was now on course 140° at a speed of 9 knots. The trim of the submarine was re-adjusted. 0641 A/9 - Dived and proceeded to the southward. 0732 A/9 - P 35 fixed her position by land bearings as being 36°19'N, 10°41'E. The bow caps of the torpedo tubes were test and found correct. Other equipment was also tested and it seemed like no damage was sustained following the grounding. Lt. Maydon decided to continue the patrol close to the south of Hammamet. 0930 A/9 - Sighted a small merchant vessel, estimated to be 2000 to 3000 tons, bearing 342°. She was proceeding close inshore south of Hammamet. Estimated enemy course was 250°. Altered course and went to full submerged speed to intercept further South on the coast. 0945 A/9 - Enemy course was now seen to be 235°, speed was 9 knots. The target was keeping very close inshore. P 35 proceeded to the South-West at full speed. 1057 A/9 - Turned to a firing course. 1100 A/9 - In position 36°14'N, 10°32'E fired 3 torpedoes from 4500 yards. A very heavy explosion followed after about 4 minutes. Another very slight explosions followed about 15 seconds after the heavy explosion. 1109 A/9 - Returned to periscope depth. All that was left of the target was a magnificent column of orange and white smoke rising to about 1000 feet in the air. 1223 A/9 - P 35 fixed her position as being 36°29'N, 10°36'E. Patrolled to the eastward and completed reloading the torpedo tubes. 1835 A/9 - Surfaced in position 36°04'N, 10°57'E. 1950 A/9 - Passed P 35's signal timed 1747 A/9 to the Capt. S.10 reporting the grounding and destruction of the target. 2103 A/9 - P 35 had to dive for an aircraft. 2156 A/9 - Surfaced in position 36°05'N, 11°15'E. Visibility was bad due to very heavy thunder and rainsqualls. P 35 withdrew to the south-west to patrol off Mahedia the following day. (4) 10 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 1821 A/10, the Capt. S.10 sent the following to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Your 1747 A/9. Well done, you have freedom of action in your area but the northernmost boundary is now 35°55'N. In a signal timed 1915 A/10, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 35; Yellow dinghy with two airmen on board in position 305° - Lampion - 12 miles at 1100 A/10 and 270° - Lampion - 8 miles at 1600 A/10 thought to be Beaufighter crew but may be Germans. Provisions and pyrotechnics delivered by air this afternoon and latter may have sunk. Estimate their positions at 0200 A/11 to be 35°30'N, 12°00'E. Close them by this time when aircraft will fire two Green Very lights overhead and dinghy should reply. If unsuccessful search by daylight surfacing at your discretion. On recovery report, returning to Malta if British, continuing patrol if prisoners. Dinghy was using life jacket as sail. (37) 11 Dec 1942 At 0144 A/11, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0020 A/11 which stated that the airmen sighted were considered hostile and that own aircraft would not co-operate in the search. P 35 was instructed to make a quick search at dawn an then return to her patrol area. At 0200 A/11, HMS P 35 was estimated to be in position 35°30'N, 12°00'E. Course was altered to 180° and speed was reduced to 4 knots to search. At 0400 A/11, course was altered to 020°. At 0604 A/11, HMS P 35 dived in position 35°30'N, 12°00'E. Course was set to 360°. At 0630 A/11, course was altered to 180°. At 0715 A/11, course was altered to 090°. At 0822 A/11, in estimated position 35°29'N, 12°03'E the dinghy with two men was sighted. At 0835 A/11, HMS P 35 picked up two German airmen from the dinghy. At 0909 A/11, course was set to 270° to return to the patrol area. At 0911 A/11, HMS P 35 dived. At 1034 A/11, a column of smoke was sighted bearing 113°. P 35 altered course and proceeded at full submerged speed to investigate. At 1122 A/11, nothing was sighted and nothing was heard on the Asdic. The chase was now abandoned and course 270° was resumed to return to the patrol area. At 2038 A/11, HMS P 35 surfaced in position 35°31'N, 11°33'E. The signal timed 0955 A/11 was then passed to the Capt. S.10 which reported the rescue of the two German airmen. At 2349 A/11, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2314 A/11 was received which ordered P 35 to be close off Sousse at dawn/12 to intercept southbound coastal traffic, but no to approach within 3 miles of the entrance to the harbour as own aircraft had laid mines there. Course was thus set for the Gulf of Hammamet and speed was increased to 8.5 knots. (4) 12 Dec 1942 At 0558 A/12, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) dived in estimated position 35°58'N, 10°51'E. At 0745 A/12, HMS P 35 fixed her position as being 35°56'N, 10°45'E which was about 7.5 miles north-east of Sousse. She then commenced to patrol in the vicinity. At 0800 A/12, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0710 A/12 was received which stated the following; Aircraft reports at 0600 A/12, two merchant ships of 2000 and 500 tons in position 36°05'N, 10°35'E, course 180° speed 7 knots. Targets justify heavy torpedo expenditure. At 1122 A/12, in approximate position 35°56'N, 10°46'E, a landing craft was sighted bearing 220° proceeding on an estimated course of 140°. The speed of this craft was not at first appreciated and estimation of the range was very difficult owing to its low silhouette. P 35 turned to attack setting the one remaining MK.VIII torpedo to 4 feet depth. The enemy was pursued almost continuously for 40 minutes but this failed to close the range at all and the attack had to be abandoned at 1215 A/12. It was estimated this landing craft was doing at least 10 knots. The enemy was last seen steering 120° for the boat channel to the south of Kuriat. Probably this was either the German F 477 or F 479 which were both on their way from Bizerta to Sfax. At 2031 A/12, HMS P 35 surfaced in position 35°51'N, 11°13'E. She then received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1620 A/12 which cancelled his signal timed 0710 A/12. HMS P 35 was ordered to be within 12 miles from Sousse at 0600 A/13. P 35 patrolled north of Kuriat during the night until it was time to set course to return to Sousse. At 2238 A/12, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1840 A/12 was received with amended to patrol to the Gulf of Hammamet west of 11°08'E. She was also ordered to leave patrol to return to Malta after dark on 14 December. (4) 13 Dec 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) attacked an old-fashioned merchant ship of 1500 tons but missed. This was the Italian transport Docilitas (1246 GRT, built 1900) who was on a trip from Trapani to Sousse. Docilitas had sailed from Trapani at 0735 A/12, via Sousse (1600 A/13-1900 A/13) and Sfax (2000 A/14-1800 A/18) and arrived at Tripoli at 1815 A/19. Shortly afterwards HMS P 35 torpedoed and damaged the German troop transport Macedonia (2875 GRT, built 1922) about 5 nautical miles north of Sousse, Tunisia in position 35°55'N, 10°38'E. The damaged German ship was beached and abandoned. She was on trip from Palermo to Sousse carrying 1000 tons of fuel for tanks. She was in company with the small German transport Jaedjoer (308 GRT, built 1937) who had joined from Trapani and they were escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Cigno. On the morning of the 13th, Jaedjoer had been lost from sight, Cigno had turned back and found her adrift as her engine had broken down. The torpedo boat took her in tow and brought her to Ras Mahmur while Macedonia had continued independently. Cigno rejoined her only after she was hit by P 35. Macedonia had been hit and taken a list, the torpedo tracks had not been seen and it was believed that she had been mined. Cigno arrived on the scene to find her abandoned except for her captain and three crew members. The torpedo-boat took her in tow and asked Sousse for assistance. The tug Optimiste sailed but arrived too late, Macedonia had sunk in shallow waters (12 metres). At this time Supermarina informed Cigno that Macedonia had been the victim of a submarine and she was ordered to hunt it. It is interesting to note that Naval Headquarters in Rome were more informed on the local situation than the participants themselves. They were often accused of interfering too much with operations but this time they were justified. At 1930 hours Cigno obtained an echo and at 1945 hours dropped a pattern of depth charges but then lost contact. It was assumed that it was a false echo and the chase abandoned. At 0520 A/12, the German transport Macedonia (2875 GRT, built 1922) escorted by Cigno sailed from Palermo at 8 knots. The small German transport Jaedjoer (309 GRT, built 1938) sailed from Trapani at 1400 A/12 and joined the convoy but she could only make 6 knots. During the night of 12/13, in heavy fog, Cigno lost track of Jaedjoer. At 0705 A/13, aerial escort having arrived, Cigno signalled Macedonia to proceed independently and turned back to look for Jaedjoer. At 0822 A/13, the torpedo boat sighted her and the German vessel reported that she had engine defects which could not be repaired. The torpedo boat passed her a tow, and they proceeded at 10 knots. At 1200 A/13, Cigno received a signal from Supermarina ordering her to leave Jaedjoer at Ras Mahmur and rejoin Macedonia. This was done at 1445 A/13, and the torpedo boat proceeded at 24 knots to catch up with the other vessel. At 1630 A/13, she sighted Macedonia which had been torpedoed and had a slight list. The vessel had been abandoned, and her Captain informed Cigno that he believed she had been mined. At 1702 A/13, the torpedo boat took her in tow. At 1900 A/13, Macedonia sank in shallow water. The French tug Optimiste arrived and was told to recover the barrels of petrol. Fifteen minutes later, Supermarina ordered Cigno to hunt the submarine and, at 1945 A/13, she dropped a pattern of depth charges. At 2020 A/13, she sighted the German transport Lisboa (1799 GRT, built 1919) which had sailed from Sousse and escorted her to Trapani. However, at 0254 A/14, Cigno was ordered by Supermarina to abandon the escort of Lisboa and escort Jaedjoer which was now again under way. She sighted her at 0758 A/14 and escorted her to Sousse. They entered the port at 1213 A/14, Cigno just dropped anchor and landed in a boat with the military passengers she had picked up. They had barely arrived when six Boston bombers of the USAAF attacked and dropped small bombs, none hit. The torpedo boat was instructed to escort the motor refrigeration ship Assab (625 GRT, built 1919) to Trapani, and they sailed at 1347 A/14 and arrived at destination at 1153 A/15. 0557 A/13 - Dived in estimated position 36°02'N, 10°46'E. 0858 A/13 - Fixed position as being 35°59'N, 10°39'E. 0910 A/13 - Sighted a three-masted schooner approach the area mined with British mines off Sousse. The schooner was approaching from the eastward. This may have been the Giuseppina Chiesa (213 GRT, built 1912) which had sailed from Tripoli (she had suffered some damage during an air attack on Tripoli on 29 November 1942). During the night of 12/13 December, she was machine-gunned by an enemy aircraft without result. She arrived at Sousse on the 13th and was sunk there during an air attack at 1900 A/14. 1300 A/13 - Fixed the position as being 35°54'N, 10°42'E and also received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1155 A/12 which ordered HMS P 35 to keep within striking distance of the coastal route north of Sousse except when charging. 1403 A/13 – Sighted masts and funnel of a small steamship, very close to shore bearing 285°, estimated course 160°. 1442 A/13 – In position 35°54'N, 10°39'E, fired two torpedoes aimed at the foremast and mainmast then altered course to starboard and dived to 45 feet increasing speed to clear the torpedo tracks. 1445 A/13 – Explosion (muffled) after 3m. 9s. Torpedoes missed probably due to an overestimation of speed. [Docilitas was missed and luckily for her, the appearance of another ship drew the attention away from her and she reached Sousse safely.] 1449 A/13 - Sighted a vessel bearing 327°. The enemy was seen to be a merchant vessel of about 3000 tons on a course of 152°. Started attack. 1522 A/13 - In position 35°55'N, 10°38'E fired one torpedo aimed at the funnel. 47 Seconds after firing the torpedo hit the target. The target was run aground to prevent her from sinking. She was down by the bow and listing to port. P 35 then withdrew to the northward but remained in the area. 1615 A/13 - An Italian torpedo boat was sighted closing the target. 1651 A/13 - The torpedo boat went alongside the target as had been hoped. Unfortunately it was on the far side. It was decided to fire the last torpedo in the tubes at the merchant vessel hoping to finish this ship off and damaging the torpedo boat as well. 1653 A/13 - Fired on the torpedo at the merchant vessel from 4100 yards. Unfortunately it missed. Withdrew to reload a torpedo as fast as possible. 1746 A/13 - Completed the reload. Stalked the torpedo boat that was now stopped a little distance from the grounded merchant vessel. 1812 A/13 - When about to open fire on the torpedo boat it shifted position. As the torpedo boat shifted position several times it was not possible to attack. 1947 A/13 - By now the torpedo boat had obtained contact on P 35 which had gone to 90 feet. First 6 and then 3 depth charges were dropped in rapid succession. Minor damage was caused by these depth charges. No further depth charges were dropped. 2135 A/13 - Nothing was heard on Asdic and P 35 returned to periscope depth. 2156 A/13 - Surfaced in position 35°58'N, 10°46'E and proceeded on course 060° to get clear of the Gulf of Hammamet at 8 knots. Lt. Maydon decided to return to Malta as only one torpedo was remaining. (4) 15 Dec 1942 Around 1020 A/15, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) ended her 16th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. She was escorted in by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) which had joined around 0720 A/15. (38) 19 Dec 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) is docked in No.5 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. (39) 21 Dec 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) is undocked. (39) 27 Dec 1942 Around 1230 A/27, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) departed Malta for patrol off Naples. In a signal timed 2141 A/27, she informed the Capt. S.10 that she would return to Malta due to a suspected case of diphtheria. Her E.T.A. at Malta was estimated to be around 0800 A/28. (40) 28 Dec 1942 Around 0945 A/28, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) returned to Malta. (39) 6 Jan 1943 Around 1300 A/6, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) departed Malta for her 17th war patrol (16th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the south of Naples. For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 35 during this patrol see the map below. (4) 7 Jan 1943 At 1652 A/7, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) altered course to 176° for position 36°00'N, 11°40'E in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1257 A/7 which contained the following orders; Cancel previous patrol orders. Proceed through position 36°00'N, 11°40'E to establish patrol in area to westward of line 340° from Kerkenah No.4 buoy. If you attack or are sighted off Mahedia move to Gulf of Hammamet and vice versa. South and northbound coastal traffic is your objective. (41) 8 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1105 A/8, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN); Move north of latitude 36°00'N by 2200 A/8 owing to own surface forces operating south of Kuriat. This referred to an anti-shipping sweep during then night of 8/9 January by the destroyers HMS Kelvin (Cdr. M.S. Townsend, OBE, DSC, RN) and HMS Nubian (Cdr. D.E. Holland-Martin, DSC, RN) (42) 9 Jan 1943 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian merchant Emilio Morandi (1523 GRT, built 1904) about 35 nautical miles east-north-east of Sousse, Tunisia in position 35°58'N, 11°20'E. She was in company with the Italian motorized lighters MZ 727, MZ 736, MZ 744 and MZ 755. They were on passage from Tripoli to Trapani. Morandi sank in 12 minutes, the lighters were hampered by the bad weather but picked up 23 survivors and landed them at Pantelleria. The Italian torpedo boat Partenope later arrived on the scene of the sinking and searched for survivors but found none. They had sailed from Tripoli at 2200 A/6 for Trapani. After the sinking of Emilio Morandi, the four F-lighters arrived at Pantelleria at 1030 A/10 and sailed from there at 1810 A/10, arriving at Trapani at 1230 A/11. 0600 A/9 - Dived in position 35°52'N, 11°20'E. 1033 A/9 - Fixed position off Kuriat as being 35°49'N, 11°09'E. 1416 A/9 - Sighted a merchant ship bearing 190°. Enemy course was 345°. Altered course to intercept. 1440 A/9 - The target was a 2000 tons old-fashioned merchant vessel. She was Northbound and in ballast. She was escorted by 4 'F-boats' (Tank landing craft). 1514 A/9 - In position 35°37'N, 11°09'E fired a torpedo at the target from 1200 yards. Due to an error in drill the torpedo was fired to late resulting in a miss. Another torpedo was then fired but this one also missed. 1820 A/9 - Surfaced in position 35°49'N, 11°12'E and set off in pursuit. 2003 A/9 - In position 35°58'N, 11°20'E fired another torpedo from 600 yards. After 27 seconds it hit the target. P 35 dived after firing and retired from the area. No counter attack followed. 2145 A/9 - Surfaced in position 35°54'N, 11°21'E and proceeded southwards. (4) 10 Jan 1943 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) attacked a small tanker with torpedo and gunfire off Kuriat. The tanker was able to escape into shallow water. This was most probably the Italian water carrier Teodolinda (361 GRT, built 1925) in company with the Italian tug R 97 / Genova (91 GRT, built 1918) escorted by the Italian auxiliary schooner R 112 / Maria di Piedigrotta (62 GRT, built 1932). They were on passage from Tripoli (departed at 1500 A/6). None of the ships sustained damage. After the attack took refuge in Monastir. 0600 A/10 - Dived in position 35°28'N, 11°14'E. 0729 A/10 - Fixed position as being 35°27'N, 11°11'E. 1300 A/10 - Sighted smoke bearing 207°. Altered course to close. 1343 A/10 - The smoke was seen to come from a small tanker of about 1000 tons. She was northbound and close inshore. Altered course to intercept her in deeper water between Ras Dimass and Kuriat. 1440 A/10 - The tanker was seen to be escorted by a single masted auxiliary schooner armed with a small automatic gun forward and a small tug that was thought to be unarmed. 1502 A/10 - In position 35°41'N, 11°06'E fired one torpedo at the tanker. It missed. 1637 A/10 - Surfaced in position 35°49'N, 11°06'E and proceeded at full speed in pursuit of the target. 1641 A/10 - Opened fire with the gun from a range of 5000 to 6000 tons. The target fled into the Gulf of Monastir. P 35 was unable to follow in the shallow water. After about 20 minutes 43 rounds had been fired and at least one hit was obtained. 1710 A/10 - Dived and retired to seaward on course 070°. 1715 A/10 - Fixed position as being 35°51'N, 11°01'E. 1846 A/10 - Surfaced in position 35°52'N, 11°05'E. (4) 11 Jan 1943 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) attacked a 2000 ton merchant vessel with one torpedo off the Gulf of Hammamet in position 36°26'N, 11°01'E. The torpedo missed its target. This was most likely the Italian transport Siculo (1481 GRT, built 1906) which was in company with the Italian schooner Luciano (264 GRT, built 1902) and Italian submarine chaser AS 95 / Garibaldi (81 GRT, built 1938) which sailed from Tripoli at 2100 A/7 via Sfax (0800 A/10) and arrived at Trapani at 1150 A/12. Later the same day she attacked the Italian sailing vessels Nuovo Domenico I (25 GRT, built 1938) and Concetta Falco (152 GRT, built 1920) with gunfire in the Gulf of Hammamet in position 36°24'N, 10°43'E. The Nuovo Domenico I is damaged during the attack. Italian sources just indicate that Nuovo Domenico I beached herself two miles north-east of Hammamet on 10 January and was declared a total loss on 14 January. Concetta Falco reached Bizerta and had to undergo repairs. In May 1943 she was scuttled there. 0609 A/11 - Dived in position 36°27'N, 11°07'E and proceeded on course 300°. 0704 A/11 - Sighted an object bearing 305°, altered course and increased speed. 0712 A/11 - Identified the target as a 2000 tons, old fashioned merchant vessel, with a tall thin funnel and two masts. The vessel was eastbound and in ballast and was escorted by two auxiliary schooners. 0738 A/11 - In position 36°26'N, 11°01'E fired one torpedo from 3800 yards. As the ship changed course and manned her gun the torpedo was most likely spotted and evaded. The target made off to the north. P 35 now withdrew to the south before closing the coast. 1220 A/11 - Fixed position as being 36°23'N, 10°54'E. 1617 A/11 - Sighted two sailing vessels leaving Hammamet eastbound. Closed to intercept. 1702 A/11 - Surfaced in position 36°24'N, 10°43'E and opened fire on both sailing vessels with Lewis guns and one the larger one also with the 12-pdr. deck gun. Range was 2000 yards. Both sailing vessels turned to the North-West and ran for the shore. The smaller beached herself. The crew of the larger one attempted to board two small boats but as the ship was still under way both capsized and the crew was seen to swim to the shore. 1712 A/11 - Range to the larger sailing vessel was now 1000 yards. She was abandoned and drifting ashore. Ceased fire. 24 Rounds had been fired for 5 hits. The larger sailing vessel was also on fire. P 35 then retired to the south to make for deeper water before diving. 1900 A/11 - Surfaced in position 36°23'N, 10°49'E and proceeded on course 130° to get clear of the area. 2243 A/11 - Sighted light bearing 132°. 2250 A/11 - Increased speed to close and went to action stations. 2314 A/11 - Sighted more lights and the contact appeared to be a well illuminated ship which was now bearing 110°. 2359 A/11 - Position was now 36°06'N, 11°24'E. The target was most probably a northbound hospital ship. P 35 was unable to close sufficiently to identify it. The chase was broken off and course was set to the southward. The Italian hospital ship Gradisca (13868 GRT, built 1913) sailed from Tripoli at 1740 A/11 and arrived at Naples at 1340 A/13 and could not have reached this position at this time. Outside possibility: the small Italian hospital ship Capri (630 GRT, built 1930) sailed from Palermo at 0020 A/11 and arrived at Pantelleria at 0915 A/12. Normally, she was not northbound unless she sailed southward toward Tripoli and then turned back to Pantelleria. (4) 12 Jan 1943 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) attacked a large German landing craft with a torpedo off Mahedia, Tunisia. The torpedo ran under. This vessel has not been yet identified but the two small landing craft were probably SF 211 and SF 258 which were operating in the area. 0552 A/12 - Dived in estimated position 35°38'N, 11°18'E. 0831 A/12 - Sighted a southbound two-masted schooner of about 250 ton to the north of Mahedia. 0835 A/12 - Increased speed and set course to intercept this schooner about 5 miles south of Mahedia with the intention of surfacing and attack with the gun. 0943 A/12 - Surfaced in position 35°27'N, 11°08'E but sighted two German landing craft with another peculiar craft, with a structure not unlike a U-boat conning tower in company. All were close inshore to the southward. It was decided to engage this vessel with a torpedo so dived again and closed. 0948 A/12 - Identified the target as a large 'Siebel ferry' (tank landing craft). Set the torpedo in No.4 tube to 4 feet. Continued to close to attack from close range. 1018 A/12 - In position 35°27'N, 11°06'E fired one torpedo from 500 yards. The torpedo ran under. All three landing craft went into Mahedia harbour. 1117 A/12 - Sighted a northbound two-masted schooner, closed preparatory to surfacing and gunning. 1127 A/12 - Sighted the masts and bridge of an Italian torpedo boat bearing 050° on a southerly course at a range of about 5 miles. This was Perseo which had sailed from Palermo at 0045 A/11, escorting the Italian tanker Gimma (1542 GRT, built 1897) to Sousse. The tanker entered Sousse at 0815 A/12 and the torpedo boat continued to Tripoli. At 0840 A/12, she was circled by four Spitfires and three Beaufighters which remained out of range of her antiaircraft guns and finally arrived at Tripoli at 0710 A/13. 1134 A/12 - Altered course to 020° to reach deeper water. Very distant Asdic transmissions were heard from the torpedo boat which course was estimated to be 155°. 1150 A/12 - P 35 fixed her position as being 35°24'N, 11°07'E. 1200 A/12 - The torpedo boat disappeared on approximate bearing 130°. 1630 A/12 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1346 A/12 which informed HMS P 35 that Allied destroyers would patrol between Kerkennah and Kuriat between 2200 A/12 and 0200 A/13. P 35 was ordered to be north of 36°00'N during this anti-shipping sweep. This referred to an anti-shipping sweep by HMS Nubian (Cdr. D.E. Holland-Martin, DSC, RN) and HMS Paladin (Lt.Cdr. L.St.G. Rich, RN). They departed Malta around 1500 A/12 and returned around 1030 A/13. They had not encountered enemy shipping. 1845 A/12 - Surfaced in position 35°43'N, 11°14'E and proceeded northwards to patrol in the vicinity of Ras Mahmur. (4) 14 Jan 1943 At 0016 A/14, north-east of Kuriat, in position 35°58'N, 11°20'E, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) sighted a flashing blue light which looked like morse code and dark shape bearing approximately 070°. P 35 went to action stations and closed at full speed to investigate. P 35 was in an unfortunate position being up moon from the target so she was well silhouetted when closing on a course of 100°. At 0028 A/14, the target appeared to be a small ship whose course was between 190° and 210° with at least two small craft in company. At 0032 A/13, more morse code flashing with the blue light was seen. The message appeared to be in coded groups of letters, quite unintelligible. At 0034 A/13, the target appeared to turn towards. Range was estimated as being 3000 yards but soon it became apparent that the target had turned away and was further off. P 35 dived hoping to carry out a moonlight submerged attack, course 110°. Fast reciprocating engine HE was heard which soon faded on bearing 080°. There was insufficient light to see anything unless very close with the periscope. At 0100 A/14, HMS P 35 surfaced and proceeded on course 160°, endeavouring to intercept the enemy to the southward. At 0102 A/14, The target was sighted bearing 040°, flashing with blue light again and once more on a southerly course. HMS P 35 turned to 055° to attack. At 0112 A/14, the target turned towards, range was about 1500 yards. At 0113 A/14, HMS P 35 dived and altered course to 160°. HE passed down the port side and faded astern. At 0131 A/14, HMS P 35 altered course to 180°. No more HE was heard. At 0138 A/14, HMS P 35 surfaced and continued on course 180° at 10 knots. >p>At 0140 A/14, a red Very's light was fired on bearing 015°. Course was altered to 010°. The moon was setting. At 0159 A/14, two small craft were sighted bearing 005° fine on the port bow at a range of about 500 yards. P 35 dived. At 0202 A/14, P 35 altered course to 180°. No HE was heard. At 0220 A/14, P 35 surfaced in position 35°56'N, 11°25'E and continued on course 180° at 10 knots endeavouring to intercept the enemy to the southward at dawn. Estimated the enemy was making for the coast of Mehedia and would then proceeded south-east to Kerkennah. At 0400 A/14, P 35 altered course to 220° to cross the enemy's expected track to intercept at dawn. At 0558 A/14, P 35 altered course to 180°. Sighted a white Very's light fired on bearing 070°, very distant. Probably the enemy had crossed astern of P 35 to the eastward. [We have no clue on which vessel(s) were encountered during the night.] At 0607 A/14, P 35 dived in position 35°27'N, 11°12'E. At 0733 A/14, P 35 fixed her position as being 35°24'N, 11°09'E. Course was set to investigate the area between Mahedia and Khadija. At 0910 A/14, a small northbound sailing vessel was sighted close inshore. At 1000 A/14, in position 35°19'N, 11°07'E, another small northbound sailing vessels was sighted. At 1600 A/14, P 35 was in position 35°25'N, 11°10'E. Nothing further had been sighted. At 1842 A/14, P 35 surfaced in position 35°30'N, 11°11'E. The Capt. S.10's signal timed 1726 A/14 was received which ordered HMS P 35 to keep south of 35°50'N after 1200 A/15 as HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) would be operating to the north of this area. Course was set to patrol off Kuriat during the night. (4) 15 Jan 1943 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) encountered an enemy merchant vessel off the Gulf of Hammamet. P 35 and the enemy vessel then opened fire on each other. As the merchant's gunfire was rather accurate P 35 dived to avoid being hit. P 35 later surfaced and set off to chase the enemy but she was unable to obtain a favourable attack position so the chase had to be abandoned. The enemy vessel encountered was the old Italian steamer Gimma (1542 GRT, built 1897) which had sailed from Sousse at 1530 A/15 via Pantelleria (1115 A/16-2100 A/17) and arrived at Palermo at 1420 A/18. She claimed to have hit the submarine with the 8th round from her 76mm gun, forcing the submarine to break off the action and submerge. 15 January 1943 0156 A/15 - Sighted a flickering white light bearing 270°. Closed at full speed. 0229 A/15 - Sighted two flickering lights bearing 285°. Went to action stations. 0234 A/15 - Sighted a light on the water very close ahead. Went full astern. This turned out to be a small calcium flare floating on the water and giving off a bright flame intermittently, a very white light, in estimated position 36°05'N, 11°23'E. 0245 A/15 - Set course to 090°. 0600 A/15 - Dived in position 35°51'N, 11°20'E. 0915 A/15 - Fixed position as being 35°52'N, 11°11'E. Patrolled of Kuriat. 1332 A/15 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1156 A/15 ordering HMS P 35 to steer to the north and endeavour to surface north of Kuriat after dark. Set course to comply with this order. 1715 A/15 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1221 A/15 in rather corrupt form ordering HMS P 35 to position 36°29'N, 10°55'E by 2301 A/15 and HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) to patrol in a position due east of P 35 by 2359 A/15. In another signal, timed 1241 A/15, the Capt. S.10 ordered P 35 to withdraw after attack or at 0401 A/16 to latitude 36°07'N and thence return to Malta. 1828 A/15 - Surfaced in position 35°57'N, 11°09'E and continued on to the position ordered. 2155 A/15 - In position 36°20'N, 10°59'E sighted a ship bearing 320°. Went to night action stations. 2203 A/15 - Identified the vessel as a northbound merchant vessel. Altered course to the north and went to full speed to intercept. 2208 A/15 - The targets course was seen to be 055°. The target was thought to be of 2000 tons with 2 masts and a thin tall funnel. Commenced chase on her starboard quarter. 2230 A/15 - As we were gaining very slowly and our position was unsuitable for a torpedo attack, manned the 12pdr gun and a Lewis gun. Altered course to close. 2233 A/15 - The target must have sighted P 35 as she opened fire on the submarine. P 35 now also opened fire. Range was about 3000 yards. The enemy's shooting was very accurate and the action was broken off and P 35 dived at 2236 hours. 2311 A/15 - Surfaced and commenced chase. 2325 A/15 - The target appeared to have turned and to come towards. Dived with the intention to attack with a torpedo. 2340 A/15 - Lost sight of the target through the periscope. Heard no HE. 2341 A/15 - Surfaced in position 36°23'N, 11°09'E and again set off in pursuit. 16 January 1943 0040 A/16 - Unable to close the target which was making much smoke and obviously doing her best speed. Abandoned the chase and set course for Malta. (4) 17 Jan 1943 Around 1115 A/17, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) ended her 17th war patrol (16th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (4) 17 Jan 1943 At 0107 A/17, in position 36°02'N, 13°21'E, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) dived for a submarine zig-zagging on an opposite mean course. This was presumed to be HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN). HMS P 35 reported nothing at this time though. She did had altered course before to get of <>P 35's route but apparently this was thus not successful. (43) 24 Jan 1943 Around 1600 A/24, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) departed Malta for the U.K. where she was to refit. The first leg of the trip was to Algiers. For the daily positions of HMS P 35 during this passage see the map below. (4) 26 Jan 1943 At 1845 A/26, about 20 miles west of Marettimo Island, in approximate position 37°57'N, 11°19'E, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) heard fast HE (300 revolutions) crossing the bow from starboard to port. The HE was first heard on bearing 355° and finally faded out on bearing 340°. It was suspected that this was a patrolling motor torpedo boat. Lt. Maydon decided to surfaced later and only did so at 2010 A/26. (4) 30 Jan 1943 At 0936 A/30, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) surfaced and passed her signal timed 0915 A/30 informing the Capt. S.8 that she was three hours late. At 1122 A/30, P 35 sighted her escort, HMML 273 (?). Rendezvous was effected at 1138 A/30. Around 1400 A/30, HMS P 35 arrived at Algiers. (4) 31 Jan 1943 Around 1730 A/31, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) departed Algiers for Gibraltar. (4) 4 Feb 1943 Around 1300 A/4, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) arrived at Gibraltar from Algiers. (4) 7 Feb 1943 Around 1400 A/7, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) departed Gibraltar for the U.K. P 35 was to pass through positions 35°55'N, 06°05'W, 35°53'N, 06°15'W at 2100 A/7, 36°37'N, 07°24'W at 0630 A/8 and 43°01'N, 13°50'W at 2300 A/12. Further routing would be signalled by the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.). For the daily positions of HMS P 35 during this passage see the map below. (4) 8 Feb 1943 In a signal timed 2347/8 the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) ordered HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) to pass through position 43°01'N, 12°26'E at 1900 A/12 [instead of 43°01'N, 13°50'W at 2300 A/12], thence by 'Red' route. (44) 9 Feb 1943 At 2030 A/9, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) received the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic's (F.O.C.N.A.) signal timed 1528 A/9 which ordered her to pass through positions 41°00'N, 10°01'W and 43°01'N, 11°10'W thence as routed by the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.). In a signal timed 2127 A/9, F.O.S. signalled the following orders to P 35; 1) Estimate you will pass position 43°01'N, 11°10'W at 1800 A/12. Proceed dived by day through position 46°02'N, 11°09'E, 49°00'N, 11°10'E, 49°10'N, 09°00'E, 180° - Bishop Rock - 10 miles at 0800 A/17, thence on the surface at the Commanding Officers discretion to make rendezvous in position 180° - Lizard Head - 10 miles at 1500 A/17 for onward escort to Plymouth. 2) Estimate your speed during the night of 12/13 7 knots, and daily advance 115 miles. (44) 17 Feb 1943 At 1820 A/17, HMS Umbra (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) arrived at Plymouth. At 1237 A/17, she had made rendezvous with HMS Fir (T/Skr. W.H. Buley, RNR) at the end of the swept channel to Plymouth. HMS Umbra had arrived early at the rendezvous and had signalled to the Lizard signalled station that she would proceeded towards the end of the swept channel and meet her escort there. HMS Umbra started her refit at the Devonport Dockyard on 24 February 1943. Refit was completed in late July 1943. [As no log is available for July 1943 some details for this month may be missing.] (4) 1 Aug 1943 HMS Umbra (Lt. P.H. May, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Plymouth with HMCS Athabaskan (Cdr. G.R. Miles, DSO, OBE, RCN), HMS Cattistock (Lt. R.G.D. Keddie, DSC, RN), HMS Cambridgeshire (T/Lt. H.F.B. Ashby, RNVR), HMS Ambrose Pare (Skr. J.W. Morris, RNR), HMS Victrix (T/Lt. J.D. The torpedo boats Calliope and Perseo sailed from Tripoli to reinforce the convoy and joined it at 1150 B/23. The convoy arrived at Tripoli at 1525 B/23-1735 B/23. (16)

22 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1018 B/22, the Capt. S.10 informed all submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla which were at sea that at 0925 B/22 three merchant ships, two cruisers and ten destroyers had been sighted in position 35°18'N, 18°40'E proceeding on course 190° at speeds of 8 to 12 knots

In a signal timed 1111 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that eight merchant merchant ships, two battleships, five cruisers and fifteen destroyers had been sighted in position 35°12'N, 18°22'E on course 190° at a speed of 8 knots.

In a signal timed 1336 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that an enemy battleship of the Littorio-class, two cruisers of the Trento / Condottieri-class, four destroyers, three merchant ships and three enemy aircraft had been reported in position 34°48'N, 18°30'E proceeding on course 190° at a speed of 10 knots.

The following submarines were the ones that were on patrol at that time and thus must have received these signals; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN), HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN).

Presumably HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) also received these signals as she was also on patrol but she was to the north of Sicily at that time so given the position of the convoy sighted the signals were of no use to her. (14)
23 Feb 1942 At 0815 B/23, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) was in position 32°57'N, 14°45'E and course was altered to 180° for the ordered patrol position which was position 'T' (32°48'N, 14°45'E).

Between 1000 B/23 and 1450 B/23, numerous and heavy explosions were heard fairly close. Nothing could be seen through the periscope though or detected on Asdic. P 35 therefore continued on proceeding southwards towards position 'T'.
At 1430 B/23, HMS P 35 was in estimated position 32°38'N, 14°43'E. She continued on to the southwards to fix her position by land bearings.
At 1803 B/23, P 35 was in estimated position 32°32'N, 14.45'E. Soundings gave a depth of 44 fathoms. Course was altered to 080°.

At 2205 B/23, course was altered to 010° and HMS P 35 proceeded in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1203 B/23 in which she was ordered to patrol east of Sicily / South of the Straits of Messina. (12)

23 Feb 1942 In signals timed 1152 B/23 and 1203 B/23, Capt. S.10 sent the following orders; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) leave their patrol positions at 2000 B/23 through positions 35°00'N, 14°12'E, and 35°00'N, 14°18'E respectively to arrive off Marsamxett harbour at 0715 B/25.
HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'K', 34°20'N, 11°10'E - 000° - 40 - 60 through position 33°25'N, 13°45'E.
HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'G', 35°05'N, 12°05'E - 000° - 50 - 40 through positions 33°05'N, 14°11'E and 35°00'N, 13°05'E.

HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'L', south of 34°15'N and west of 11°40'E. She was to proceed through positions 33°00'N, 14°11'E and 33°50'N, 13°20'E.

HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'H', 35°15'N, 11°00'E - 000° - 40 - 50 proceeding through positions 33°00'N, 14°32'E and 35°00'N, 12°30'E.

HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'N', south of 33°45'N and between 11° 50'E and 12°55'E proceeding through positions 32°50'N, 14°45'E and 33.20'N, 13.45'E.

HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'J', 36°54'N, 15°24'E - 330° - 40 - 70 proceeding through positions 32°38'N, 15°00'E and 35°00'N, 15°30'E.
They were not to attack submarines encountered during the night of 23/24 February to prevent friendly fire incidents from happening. (14)

24 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 2314 B/24, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Suspected for 20 miles morth and 10 miles west of position 36°00'N, 15°40'E. (14)

27 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1707 B/27, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Air reconnaissance Messina on the 27th showed, two 8-inch, one 6-inch cruiser, eleven destroyers and six submarines. (14)

28 Feb 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) attacked a medium seized merchant vessel with two torpedoes about 10 nautical miles South-East of Catania, Sicily in position 37°22'N, 15°16'E. No hits were obtained.
[The target has not yet been identified.]
0917 B/28 - Sighted smoke bearing 190°. Set course to intercept.

1012 B/28 - In position 37°22'N, 15°16'E fired two torpedoes at a 2000 - 3000 tons merchant vessel. Range was 3800 yards. A loud explosion was heard and the torpedoes were not heard to run anymore. The target made a dense cloud of black funnel smoke and made for Catania at increased speed. Proceeded to the northward to patrol along the Calabrian shore. (12)

1 Mar 1942 At 0849 B/1, was in position Capo Del Armi - 325° - 5.2 miles, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN). She then patrolled eastwards along the Calabrian coast. Large railway and road bridges at the Amendolea river estuary appeared inviting gun targets. (12)

1 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 1203 B/1, the Capt. S.10 sent the following instructions on leaving patrol to the submarines mentioned; HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) was to arrive at Malta on 4 March and was to proceed through positions 35°40'N, 12°00'E and 35°40'N, 14°20'E on the way back. HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) was to arrive at Malta on 5 March and was to proceed through positions 34°00'N, 12°50'E and 35°32'N, 14°14'E on the way back. HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) was to arrive at Malta on 5 March and was to proceed through positions 37°02'N, 15°40'E and 35°51'N, 15°15'E on the way back. (17)

2 Mar 1942 Lt. Maydon decided to bombard the bridges seen the day before so HMS P 35 returned during the forenoon to within 3/4 of a miles of the river mouth for final reconnaissance and then withdrew to seaward to attack late in the afternoon.

At 1040 B/2, two small vessels, prossible MA/SB's (A/S launches) close inshore proceeding slowly eastwards. In view of this, Lt. Maydon abandoned the projected attack on the bridges and set course for the area to the south-east of Catania. (12)
3 Mar 1942 At 1122 B/3, near Catania, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) sighted two small vessels which could not be identified before they disappeared in the mist. (12)

4 Mar 1942 At 0755 B/4, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) was to the east of Syracuse, in position 37°03'N, 15°27'E. She then left patrol to return to Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1203 B/1. (12)
5 Mar 1942 At 0635 B/5, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) ended her 4th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (12)

8 Mar 1942 While dived during daylight at Malta with half the crew on board, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) had her pressure hull pierced forward by accident. The tube space flooded. At 0940 B/8 she tried to surface but failed to do so. She was then raised at 1220 B/8 and was docked for repairs. During repairs the fore-end compartment, which had been flooded was re-wired. Repairs were completed by March 27th. (18)

27 Mar 1942 At 2000 B/27, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Malta for her 5th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol between Kerkennah and Lampedusa, between 35°00'N / 35°50'N and 11°10'E and 12°96'E.
[As no log is available for this period it is not possible to display a map of this patrol.] (12)

28 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 1630 B/28, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) of the following; Escorted southbound medium motor vessel of 11.5 knots may be encountered in the vicinity of position 35°20'N, 11°50'E around 1700 B/29. Aircraft may attack after dark.

This was most probably the Italian steamer Argentea (3302 GRT, built 1908) escorted by the Italian destroyer Antoniotto Usodimare which known from ULTRA intercepts (DEFE3/751/frames 226, 229, 265, 272, 350) to leave Naples at 0400 B/28, at a speed of 12 knots for Tripoli. Argentea escorted by the Antoniotto Usodimare and the Italian torpedo boat Giuseppe Sirtori actually sailed from Naples at 0500 B/28-0550 B/28 in heavy fog and rain. At 2302 B/28, when off Cape San Vito, they turned back and stopped at Palermo (0410 B/29-1630 B/29). Shortly after leaving Palermo, defects forced Giuseppe Sirtori to leave them and return to Palermo, only Antoniotto Usodimare escorted the merchant ship. At 0448 B/31, they met the torpedo boat Castore which had sailed from Tripoli at 0445 B/31 to pilot them in, and they reached Tripoli at 0943 B/31-0950 B/31.

Note: it was also known that the Italian tanker Saturno (former French Massis, 5022 GRT, built 1914) (DEFE3/751, frames 217, 219, 228, 256, 326) was to sail from Naples at 1700 B/27, at a speed of 9 knots for Tripoli. She actually sailed at 1950 B/27 escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Lince. The Italian torpedo boat Generale Carlo Montanari had sailed from Trapani at 1835 B/27 to reinforce the escort, but bad weather forced them to anchor at Favignana which they reached at 0030 B/28. Generale Carlo Montanari briefly returned to Trapani to refuel before returning to Favignana. The three ships sailed from there at 1730 B/28-1900 B/28. The torpedo boat Calliope sailed from Tripoli at 0430 B/30 and met the convoy at 0800 B/30 to pilot them in. They reached Tripoli at 1120 B/30-1150 B/30. (17)

30 Mar 1942 In the afternoon, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) investigated the wreck of the Italian transport Napoli (6142 GRT, built 1941) she had torpedoed on 3 February. The vessel beached near Mahedia appeared abandoned. Also her guns had been removed and no further action seemed necessary. (4)

30 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 2207 B/30, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Patrol in the vicinity of position 35°23'N, 12°21'E from 1400 B/31 where reconnaissance suggests that a large southbound convoy may be encountered before dark.

On 26 March, ULTRA had intercepted a signal that six merchant ships were expected to arrive at Tripoli on 1 April (see DEFE3/091/1/frames 202, 214, 236). No details of this convoy were known but it was suspected that the battleship Littorio and the light cruiser Eugenio di Savoia might be involved in covering it.

This must have referred to the convoys of operation LUPO (three convoys which merged into one) which were delayed when it was learned that an enemy squadron of two cruisers and four destroyers screening a convoy of four merchant ships had been sighted at 1315 B/1 in position 040° - Ras Haleima – 35 miles, course 285°. This probably referred to convoy AT 36 which had sailed from Alexandria on 31 March for Tobruk, escorted by the corvettes HMS Delphinium (Cdr.(Retd.) R.L. Spalding, RN), HMS Primula (T/Lt. J. Paterson, RNR), HMS Snapdragon (T/Lt. P.H. Potter, RNR), the escort destroyers HMS Eridge (Lt.Cdr. W.F.N. Gregory-Smith, DSC, RN), HMS Beaufort (Lt.Cdr. S.O’G Roche, RN), minesweepers HMS Fareham (Lt.Cdr. N.H.B. Bloye, RNR) and the auxiliary M/S whaler HMS Firmament (?). The three Italian convoys finally sailed on 2-3 April:

Convoy 1: Transports Unione (Italian 6071 GRT, built 1942) and Lerici (Italian 6070 GRT, built 1941), escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta (leader), Antonio da Noli, Euro and the torpedo boat Cigno, sailed from Taranto at 1200 B/2-1250 B/2. During the evening, Euro was detached and reached Bari at 0625 B/3.

Convoy 2: Transports Nino Bixio (Italian, 7137 GRT, built 1940) and Monviso (Italian 5322 GRT, built 1941), escorted by the destroyers Emanuele Pessagno (leader), Folgore and the torpedo boat Centauro, sailed from Messina at 2350 B/2-0030 B/3.

Convoy 3: Transports Gino Allegri (Italian, 6836 GRT, built 1941) and Monreale (6989 GRT, built 1941), escorted by the destroyers Nicolò Zeno (leader), Freccia and the torpedo boat Pallade, sailed from Augusta at 0145 B/3-0200 B/3.

Covering force: The light cruisers Eugenio di Savoia and Raimondo Monteccucoli (7th Cruiser Division) screened by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello and Mitragliere sailed from Taranto at 1759 B/2-1900 B/2. Mitragliere left the escort at about 0800 B/3 and returned to Taranto at 2230 B/3. The battleship Littorio did not take part in the operation as she was docked for repairs at Taranto on 2 April.

The convoys were united at 0747 B/3 in position 36°59'N, 16°47'E. At this point, Cigno received the order to return to Messina where she arrived at 1412 B/3. The covering force joined at 0830 B/3 and proceeded together until 2020 B/3 when the covering force left and at 2110 B/3, when, in position 34°03'N, 16°04'E, the three convoys separated again.

Monreale and Gino Allegri escorted by Nicolò Zeno (leader), Folgore and Centauro. Monviso and Lerici escorted by Emanuele Pessagno (leader), Freccia and Pallade. Unione and Nino Bixio escorted by Antonio Pigafetta (leader), Antonio da Noli.

At 0035 B/4, Antonio da Noli was attacked by an enemy bomber but was missed. The torpedo boat Castore sailed from Buerat El Hsun at 1630 B/3 to carry out an A/S sweep off Tripoli ahead of the arrival of the convoys. At 0540 B/4, she sighted the Monviso/Lerici convoy which was following the coastal route to Tripoli and at 0606 B/4, she sighted the Unione/Nino Bixio convoy arriving from the north and took position ahead to pilot it to Tripoli where they arrived at 0928 B/4. In the meantime, Emanuele Pessagno escorting the Monviso/Lerici convoy, ordered Pallade to pilot it to Tripoli where the trio arrived at 0858 B/4. The torpedo Calliope sailed from Tripoli at 0445 B/4 and at 0627 B/4, joined the Gino Allegri/Monreale convoy to pilot it in.

The destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Antonio da Noli, Nicolò Zeno, Emanuele Pessagno and Freccia stopped short of entering harbour and returned to Italy. Antonio da Noli and Emanuele Pessagno arrived at Messina at 0730 B/5-0740 B/5, Antonio Pigafetta, Nicolò Zeno and Freccia arrived at Taranto at 1510 B/5-1600 B/5.
The covering force returned to Taranto at 1842 B/4-1930 B/4. (17)

31 Mar 1942 In a singal timed 1415 B/31, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) of the following; My 2207 B/30. Convoy now unlikely. Patrol eastern route near position 35°40'N, 12°15'E for a northbound merchant vessel expected in the forenoon of April 1st. speed about 13.5 knots. (17)
1 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1019 B/1 the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Remain in the vicinity as ordered in my signal timed 1415 B/31. (19)

3 Apr 1942 At 0600 B/3, south-west of Lampedusa, in position 35°23'N, 12°15'E, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) sighted three vessels bearing 045° at a range of 10000 yards. This were thought to be probably two merchant vessels with one small escort. They were zigzagging on a northerly course to pass between Lampion and Lampedusa. Estimated speed of the enemy convoy was 10 about 10 knots. HMS P 35 proceeded at full speed but was unable to close.
At 0700 B/3, the convoy was lost from sight. HMS P 35 set course to patrol to the north of Lampion Island.
At 1900 B/3, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1215 B/3 was received which contained the following orders; Cancel my signal timed 1019 B/1. Resume normal patrol.
Lt. Maydon decided to remain in the vicinity of Lampion though. (12)

4 Apr 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) attacked an escorted merchant vessel with one torpedo about 10 nautical miles north-west of Lampion Island in position 35°37'N, 12°09'E. The torpedo missed.

This must have been the Italian transport Una (1397 GRT, built 1904) escorted by the German motor minesweepers R 6, R 10, R 11 and R 12. They had sailed from Trapani around 1730 B/3 bound for Tripoli but stopped on the way at Lampedusa at 1715 B/4. Una continued her passage to Tripoli at 2000 B/5 but now escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Calliope and the German motor minesweepers R 10, R 11, R 15 and R 16. The convoy arrived at Tripoli around 0500 B/7.
1138 B/4 - Sighted smoke bearing 299°. Altered course to intercept, went deep and closed at full speed.
1155 B/4 - The target appeared to be a 3500 tons merchant vessel with an escort and accompanying aircraft. Altered course to close more quickly.
1228 B/4 - The target was now seen to be painted black and white. She was escorted by 4 E-boats or R-boats and aircraft. Enemy course was estimated as 160°, speed 8,5 knots, range 7500 yards.
1238 B/4 - The target was seen to have altered course to 130°. P 35 was now almost right ahead of her at a range of 3500 yards. Maneuvered to obtain a favourable attack position.
1250 B/4 - It appeared P 35's periscope had been spotted so Lt. Maydon went to 180 feet and altered course. No counter attack followed however.

1317 B/4 - Returned to periscope depth. Saw the target about 4500 yards away steering a course of 140°. The escorts were in station on each side of the target. P 35 was nearly right astern of the target, not a favourable attack position. Given the strong escort of the target it was decided to attack with 1 torpedo as it must have been a valuable ship and cargo for the enemy.
1319 B/4 - In position 35°37'N, 12°09'E fired no.1 torpedo tube. Range was 5000 yards. The torpedo missed. P 35 then retired from the area to the west. (20)

5 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1808 B/5, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) were informed that HMS Havock (Lt.Cdr. G.R.G. Watkins, DSC, RN) would be sailing at 2000 B/5 by the south east swept channel thence direct to position 36°42.5'N, 11°04'E. Speed of advance would be 28 knots. The submarines were instructed to keep clear. (19)

6 Apr 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) attacked an escorted merchant vessel with two torpedoes about 20 nautical miles west-north-west of Lampedusa in position 35°37'N, 12°12'E. No hits were obtained.
This must have been the German transport Atlas (2297 GRT, built 1927) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Perseo and the German motor minesweepers R 9, R 12 and R 14 bound from Trapani to Tripoli.

At 1205 B/6, the submarine had observed three E-boats or R-boats moving to the northwest, this were R 9, R 12 and R 14 who had sailed from Lampedusa at 1000 B/6 to reinforce Atlas her escort. Curiously enough Lt. Maydon did not mention the presence of the R-boats during the attack. Perhaps they were not seen due to their low silhouettes and the probability that the target was only very briefly observed with the periscope because of the presence of aircraft. The R-boats had joined the convoy at 1255 B/6. The attack appeared not to have been observed.
The convoy arrived at Tripoli around 1130 B/7.

1205 B/6 - In position 35°43'N, 12°08'E, sighted three E-boats or R-boats bearing 100° at a range of 4 miles. No other ships were in company. Enemy course was approximately 300° at about 8 knots. Due to the presence of a number of aircraft HMS P 35 altered course to the westward and went to 80 feet.
1340 B/6 - Heard HE bearing 250°. Went to periscope depth and sighted masts on that bearing. Altered course to 200° to intercept and closed at full speed.
1414 B/6 - The target was seen to be a merchant ship escorted by a Spica-class torpedo boat which was ahead of her. Turned to a 120 degree track and closed at full speed.
1430 B/6 - In position 35°37'N, 12°12'E fired two torpedoes from 3200 yards. Both missed. Went to 80 feet as aircraft were sighted and retired to the west. (20)

6 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1335 B/6, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Leave patrol through position 35°20'N, 12°10'E and the south-east swept channel to arrive at Marsamxett P.M. 9/4. (17)

9 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1215 B/9, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Adjust speed to enter Marsamxett at 2100 B and berth alongside HMS Talbot. HMS P 31 and HMS Urge are submerged within two miles of 033° - 8 miles and 079° - 9 miles respectively from St. Elmo Light. Former to enter harbour at 1945 B/9, ,latter at 2030 B/9 wile passing through position 067° - St. Elmo - 4.7. miles.
Around 2100 B/9, HMS P 35 ended her 5th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (12)

14 Apr 1942 At 0510 B/14, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Malta for her 6th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol between Kerkenah and Lampedusa, between 35°00'N / 35°30'N and 11°10'E / 13°01'E.
15 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1347 B/15, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) to keep west of 12°05'E in area 'K' for 24 hours from 2359 B/16. (19)
17 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 2217 B/17, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) to patrol north of Kerkennah No.4 buoy until 0800 B/18 to intercept a 15 knot northbound ship. (19)

18 Apr 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) attacked the Italian transport Nino Bixio (7137 GRT, built 1941) about 40 nautical miles south-west of Lampedusa in position 35°05'N, 11°49'E. All torpedoes fired missed their target.

Nino Bixio sailed from Tripoli at 1915 B/17-1920 B/17 making 15 knots. She was escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta and Nicolò Zeno. At 0658 B/18, Nicolò Zeno first sighted a bubble discharge from a distance of 1500 metres then a torpedo wake which passed astern Nino Bixio and apparently exploded on Kerkenah Bank. Nicolò Zeno dropped five 50-kg and two 100-kg depth charges. They arrived at Naples at 1017 B/19-1035 B/19.

0015 B/18 - In position 35°14'N, 11°32'E, sighted a steady white light bearing 185°. Gave chase at full speed. After thirty minutes speed was reduced to listen for HE but nothing was heard. Soundings gave a depth of 8.5 fathoms. The light was considered to be most likely a fishing boat among the banks. The chase was abandoned.
0315 B/18 - Arrived in position 15 miles bearing 020° from Kerkennah No.4 buoy and commenced to patrol on a 6-mile line on courses 020° / 200°.
0453 B/18 - Dived in position 35°03'N, 11°50'E. Soundings gave a depth of 15.5 fathoms.
0630 B/18 - Sighted a 6000-tons merchant vessel escorted by two destroyers. Started attack.
0655 B/18 - In position 35°05'N, 11°49'E fired three torpedoes from 5800 yards. It was thought one probable hit was obtained but this was not the case.
0704 B/18 - The first pattern of four depth charges was dropped. After this attack only two more depth charges were dropped. None caused any damage.
0734 B/18 - Returned to periscope depth. Sighted the target bearing 338°.

2333 B/18 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2205 B/18 which contained the following orders; Patrol Kuriat - Kerkennah No.4 buoy route until 2030 B/19 for a southbound 10 knots tanker and escort. Aircraft are searching and will attack tonight in the Marettimo - Cape Bon area. Course was set to comply with these orders.

This last signal referred to the tanker Panuco (Italian 7751 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the destroyer Folgore and the torpedo Centauro sailed from Naples at 2220 B/18-2256 B/18 (convoy ALFA). The torpedo boat Pallade sailed from Tripoli at 0125 B/20 to reinforce the escort. At 2312 B/20, the convoy was attacked by a Wellington bomber, bombs were dropped between Pallade and Centauro in position 176° Lampedusa – 40 miles. They arrived at Tripoli at 1432 B/21-1520 B/21. ULTRA intercepts had revealed their imminent departure since 14 April, but it was delayed (see DEFE3/840/1/pages 359, 367, 368, 312, 498, 583-584). (12)

19 Apr 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian transport Assunta de Gregori (4219 GRT, built 1914) about 20 nautical miles east-south-east of Mahdia, Tunisia in position 35°23'N, 11°23'E.
The Italian transport was on passage from Sfax to Naples escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Castore.

Despite being equipped with sonar, the Castore, who was leading ahead, heard nothing and did not observe any torpedo track. Only the sound of a double explosion followed by the sight of Assunta di Gregori broken in two with her stern sinking vertically, triggered a counterattack. The torpedo boat turned back and picked up 35 survivors. She also dropped two depth charges for intimidation purposes.

At 1632 B/18, the torpedo boat Castore had sailed from Tripoli and at 0446 B/19 was in view of the Sfax lighthouse. At 0545 B/19, she sighted Assunta di Gregori coming out of Sfax and at 0605 B/19, she signalled the merchant ship to stop and sent a boat with an officer to bring the operation orders from Marina Tripoli. At 0636 B/19, they proceeded in company. At 0735 B/19, aircraft appeared to provide air cover. At 1645 B/19, a double explosion occurred in position 35°22'N, 11°32'E and the merchant ship sank vertically. Castore did not sight any torpedo wake and did not know from where the attack had come. At 0702 B/19, she could only drop two depth charges for intimidation purposes. She recovered 35 survivors and proceeded to Palermo. She was then instructed by Supermarina to remain in the area and hunt the submarine but her portside engine broke down. She returned to the area of the attack, but the search was fruitless and, at 0800 B/20, she resumed her passage to Palermo where she arrived at 0757 B/21.
1550 B/19 - Sighted a merchant ship approaching from the southward. Altered course to intercept.
1557 B/19 - Sighted a Spica-class torpedo boat escorting the merchant vessel of about 4000 to 5000 tons. Also two aircraft were patrolling the area. Started attack.

1643 B/19 - In position 35°23'N, 11°23'E fired two torpedoes at the merchant vessel from 1100 yards. Both torpedoes hit the target. The torpedo boat dropped only 2 depth charges which caused no damage to P 35.
1730 B/19 - Returned to periscope depth. Saw the torpedo boat laying stopped to pick up the survivors of the merchant ship.
P 35 continued to patrol to the south-east of Mahedia in the hope of intercepting the reported southbound tanker. (12)
20 Apr 1942 At 0638 B/20, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) fixed her position off Mahedia as being 35°29'N, 11°11'E, Course was set to 090° to return to the vicinity of Lampion Island.

At 1156 B/20, in approximate position 35°29'N, 11°28'E a Spica-class torpedo boat was seen proceeding on a zigzag course of approximately 250°/310° at 15 knots. The enemy vessel was transmitting on Asdic every six seconds. She passed at a range of 4000 yards. No attack was made due to the shallow draft of the enemy vessel and the fact that P 35 had only three torpedoes remaining. It was appreciated that this torpedo boat might be going to reinforce the escort of some southbound traffic and accordingly P 35 set course to return to the coastal route.
This was most probably Castore on her way to Palermo after searching for the submarine which had sunk the Assunta di Gregori (see 19 April). (12)

20 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1654 B/20, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) were informed by Capt. S.10 that their position was probably compromised by sighting. (19)

21 Apr 1942 At 0031 B/21, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1047 B/20, which ordered her to concentate on the central routes through her patrol area. Course was set to patrol near Lampion Island to comply with these orders. (21)

22 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1031 B/22, referring to his signal timed 1025 B/21, the Capt. S. 10 informed HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) that two 14-knot escorted merchant ships may be encountered proceeding north on meridian 11°49'E crossing 34°30'N about 1400 B/23. At 2030 B/23, HMS Una was extend her patrol area east to 12°50'E and north to 36°10'N.

This must have referred to the convoy made up of the transports Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941) and Vettor Pisani (Italian, 6339 GRT, built 1939) en-route from Tripoli (departed 1935 B/22-2040 B/22) at 14.5 knots for Naples escorted by the Italian destroyer Folgore and the Italian torpedo boats Centauro and Pallade.

At about 1630 B/23-1747 B/23, about 30 miles north of Pantelleria, there was a change of escort with another convoy proceeding to Tripoli: This convoy was made up of the transports Gino Allegri (Italian, 6836 GRT, built 1941) and Agostino Bertani (Italian, 8329 GRT, built 1941) and had sailed from Naples at 2245 B/22-2330 B/22, proceeding at 15.5 knots, escorted by the Italian destroyers Antonio Pigafetta Pigafetta, Nicolò Zeno and the Italian torpedo boat Cantore.
The information was once again based on an ULTRA source (see DEFE3/753/486, frame 490 and DEFE3/754, frames 8, 78). (19)

23 Apr 1942 At 2056 B/23, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) surfaced in approximate position 35°15'N, 11°47'E and proceeded on passage back to Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.10's orders contained in his signal timed 1033 B/22.

This signal contained the following orders; Leave patrol P.M./23 through positions 35°24'N, 12°48'E and 36°11'N, 14°11'E. Keep more than 7 miles from the coast until 070° from Stelmo on which bearing motor launches and drifters may be on patrol after dark. Enter Marsamxett (Malta) at 2101 B/25. (21)
25 Apr 1942 At 2100 B/25, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) ended her 6th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (12)

3 May 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Malta for her 7th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to perform a special operation on the north-west coast of Sicily. We do not know the exact details of her mission.
She was to proceeded through position 'A' (38°00'N, 11° 44'E) about 1300 B/5.
In a signal timed 2214 B/3, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS P 35 that in case the object is not achieved she should spend 24 hours on the Palermo - St. Vito convoy route before returning to Malta.
[As no log is available for this period it is not possible to display a map of this patrol.] (4)
6 May 1942 In a signal timed 0050 B/6, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) informed the Capt. S.10 of the following; Starboard engine broken down, returning to Malta, E.T.A. 2030 B/6.
Later that day, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) ended her 7th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (4)
10 May 1942 Around 0400 B/10, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Malta for passage to Alexandria. She was to proceed through position 35°51'N, 15°15'E and 34°50'N, 20°00'E.

At 0417 hours, shortly after leaving harbour, five darkened vessels were observed and did not answer the challenge. P 35 opened fire with its Lewis gun. The Port Wireless Station ordered the submarine to cease firing. These were HMS Beryl, HMS Trusty Star and three others who were waiting for the arrival of HMS Welshman. HMS P 35 had not been informed of their presence.
[As no log is available for this period it is not possible to display a map of this patrol.] (22)

11 May 1942 In a signal timed 1118 C/11, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Proceed direct to position 34°22'N, 26°35'E thence to arrive at the end of the Alexandria searched channel at 0530 C/19 reporting when safe to do so if you cannot comply within three hours. U-boats are operating on the Cyrenaican coast and may be encountered proceeding to and from Aegean. Submarines are not to be attacked unless definitely identified as hostile. (23)
19 May 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) arrived at Alexandria from Malta. (22)
6 Jun 1942 Around 1850 C/6, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Alexandria for her 8th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean).

She was ordered to give cover during convoy operations to Malta and took position 'D' (35°57'N, 19°13'E) in a line with three other submarines in the Ionian Sea. (HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) in position 'A' (35°37'N, 18°44'E), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) in position 'B' (35°43'N, 18°55'E) and HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) in position 'C' (35°50'N, 19°02'E). This was to provide cover for operation VIGOROUS.
For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 35 during this patrol see the map below.

13 Jun 1942 At 0145 B/13, in position (D.R.) 35°32'N, 20°09'E, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) sighted a submarine bearing 275° at a range of only 1000 yards. Course of the other submarine was approximately 020°. Avoiding action was taken as this submarine was most likely friendly. It was thought to be most probably HMS Proteus (Lt.Cdr. P.S. Francis, RN). This submarine however reported noting at this time nor did any other British submarine in the area.
At 0217 C/13, HMS P 35 resumed it's original course of 285°.
At 0512 C/13, HMS P 35 dived in position 35°37'N, 19°42'E and proceeded on course 310°.
At 1413 C/13, HMS P 35 surfaced for a sunsight whih gave position 35°51'N, 19°16'E. She immediately dived again and proceeded to patrol in the vicinity of position 'D' (35°57'N, 19°13'E).
At 2140 C/13, HMS P 35 fixed her position by starsights as being 35°55'N, 19°05'E. (22)

15 Jun 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) made a attack on the Italian battleship Vittorio Veneto. The torpedoes however missed their target. Position of the attack was 36°00'N, 19°06'E. The Italian squadron was led by the battleship Littorio with the Vittorio Veneto next in line. They were in company with the heavy cruiser Gorizia, the light cruisers Giuseppe Garibaldi and Emanuele Filiberto Duca D’Aosta and three squadrons of destroyers. The heavy cruiser Trento had just been disabled by an aircraft torpedo and lagged behind with two destroyers as escort.

Later the same morning, HMS P 35 torpedoed and sank the already damaged Italian heavy cruiser Trento in position 35°55'N, 18°59'E. Trento was already damaged by a torpedo from a British Beaufort aircraft (217. Sqn. RAF based at Malta). Trento was screened by the destroyers Saetta and Antonio Pigafetta which subsequently started picking up survivors. The hospital ships Aquileia (9448 GRT, built 1914) and Città di Trapani (2467 GRT, built 1929) were directed to the scene to help with the search. In all 602 survivors were picked up from a crew of 1152 but many were wounded and some died later.
0001 C/15 - Altered course to 234° to proceed to position 'D 1' (35°30'N, 18°18'E) this in accordance with the operation orders.

0115 C/15 - Course was altered to 072° to regain position 'D' (35°57'N, 19°13'E) in accordance with the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2356 C/14 in which the submarines were ordered to re-occupy their original patrol positions.
0239 C/15 - Sighted a white light bearing 282°. Altered course towards to close at full speed.
0256 C/15 - The light appeared to be a calcium flare buring in the water with nothing attached to it. Hailed twice in case there was an aircraft dinghy in the neighbourhood but there came no reply.
0400 C/15 - Estimated to be back in position 'D'. Course was altered to 360° and speed was reduced.
0402 C/15 - Two very distant aircraft flares were seen on bearing 355°.
0413 C/15 - Further aircraft flares were seen. Course was altered to 340° and speed was increased.

0420 C/15 - A large number of aircraft flares were being dropped to illuminate the enemy that had been reported in the Vice-Admiral Malta's signal timed 0235 C/15. Course was altered to 300° and speed was further increased to try to intercept the enemy at dawn.
0427 C/15 - Twelve aircraft flares were seen being dropped in line on bearing 345°.
0524 C/15 - Took starsights and established position to be 36°02'N, 19°07'E.
0529 C/15 - Dived. Set course to 300°.
0540 C/15 - Heard HE bearing 320°.
0545 C/15 - Sighted two large vessels bearing 310°. Altered course to intercept and closed at full speed.
0555 C/15 - Started attack on two Littorio class battleships and two cruisers in line ahead. Enemy course was 175°, speed 26 knots.
0611 C/15 - Aircraft started a bombing attack. P 35 was about to fire torpedoes at a battleship. The Italians went all over the place and it was not possible to attack now.
0618 C/15 - The pandemonium had died down and two cruisers were seen approaching. Started attack on these.
0622 C/15 - The cruisers turned away.
0628 C/15 - The battleships were sighted again escorted by four destroyers.
