It was believed that she had inflicted slight damage to the submarine and she arrived at Messina at 1940 A/17. Piemonte was also escorted by a seaplane (Cant Z.501 no.1 of 189^Squadriglia) at the time of the attack.

As said Piemonte was hit aft and was subsequenlty beached near Cape Rasocolmo. The crew took to three lifeboats, they had three wounded. The seaplane dropped two bombs on the submarine. Fabrizi, after dropping intimidation depth charges (30kg), dropped the more potent 100kg depth charges set to a depth of 50-75 metres) then combed the area with a towing mine but result was uncertain.
0440 A/17 - Dived and set course to return to the ordered patrol position.
0704 A/17 - P 35 fixed her position as being 38°23'N, 15°40'E.
1100 A/17 - In position 38°20'N, 15°28'E, two eastbound merchant ships of about 2000 ton were sighted escorted by a General-class torpedo boat were seen passing to the southwards.

These were the transports Honestas (Italian, 4959 GRT, built 1920) and Vertunno (3159 GRT, built 1905) escorted by the torpedo boat Giuseppe Dezza which sailed from Palermo at 2145 A/16 and arrived at Messina at 1340 A/17.
1207 A/17 - While in position 38°23'N, 15°28'E sighted a large column of black smoke bearing 250°. Closed to investigate.

1240 A/17 - In position 38°22'N, 15°27'E, the smoke was now seen to come from a two-funnelled passenger ship thought to be of 7000 tons. She was escorted by a Generale-class torpedo boat and a flying boat. Enemy course was 078°. Started attack.
1259 A/17 - The enemy was now bearing 230°. Enemy course was 090° at 11 knots. Range was 4850 yards, distance of track 2500 yards. P 35 was closing on course 200°.
1313 A/17 - P 35 altered course to 150°.

1316 A/17 - In position 38°21'N, 15°28'E fired two torpedoes from 3000 yards. Went to 120 feet after firing. After 2 minutes and 35 seconds a torpedo explosion was heard and HE of the target ceased. A counter attack with 2 bombs and 20 depth charges followed. The bombs were rather close but caused no damage. The depth charges were further away.
1320 A/17 - HE of the target bearing 150° ceased.
1334 A/17 - Heard faint HE, possibly the target under way again.
1345 A/17 - The last depth charge was dropped. The torpedo boat was heard to be searching astern.

1428 A/17 - Returned to periscope depth. Sighted the torpedo boat bearing 189° about 4 nautical miles off. Two aircraft were also patrolling the area. P 35 went deep again and cleared the area to the north.
1510 A/17 - P 35 fixed her position as being 38°25'N, 15°27'E.
1847 A/17 - P 35 surfaced in position 38°36'N, 15°27'E. P 35 turned to course 300° with her head into the sea. Weather conditions had deteriorated. Efficient lookout from the bridge was impossible.

2035 A/17 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1819 A/17 which stated that P 35 had been sighted at 1330 A/17. Also orders were received to patrol close east of Ustica Island. Lt. Maydon decided to pass south of Stromboli and thence direct to this new patrol area.
2300 A/17 - The sea moderated slightly and speed could be slightly increased. (4)

18 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1331 A/18, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); 1) Leave patrol after dark tonight through position 38°23'N, 12°28'E and position 'B' (37°48'N, 11°44'E) to arrive in position 'F''at dawn/20th. Thence on the surface to rendezvous with sweepers at 1335 A/20. 2) My 1620 A/17, convoy may leave Palermo tonight and offer you an attack. 3) HMS Utmost (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) passes Q.B.B. 255 westbound during daylight 19th at 80 ft.

The signal 1620 A/17 from the Capt. S.10 had contained the following information amongst orders for other submarines [which are not included here]; Reconnaissance shows three large merchant ships entered Palermo 1000 today and four destroyers arriving now. Consider ships will sail for Bizerta after dark, either two convoys of about 14 and 10 knots or all at 10 knots. Convoy vitally important. (34)
18 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1912 A/18, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) informed the Capt. S.10 that her arrival at Malta would be delayed by 24 hours. (4)

20 Nov 1942 At 2330 A/20, HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) sighted a submarine 5 miles on the port beam which was thought to be either HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) or HMS Turbulent (Cdr. J.W. Linton, DSO, DSC, RN). Avoiding action was taken.
At 2334 A/20, south-south-west of Licata, Sicily, in position 36°37'N, 13°36'E, HMS P 35 sighted HMS Una on an opposite course. Avoiding action was taken to the westward. (35)
21 Nov 1942 At 1545 A/21, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) ended her 14th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She, and HMS Turbulent (Cdr. J.W. Linton, DSO, DSC, RN), were swept in by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC and Bar, RNR) which had joined around 1130 A/21. (4)

30 Nov 1942 Around 1315 A/30, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Malta for her 15th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the Tunisian coast between Hammamet and Kerkennah in the vicinity of Kerkennah No.4 buoy (34°53'N, 11°47'E) until 0900 A/2 when she was to proceed north to patrol in an area bounded by the Tunisian coast and 11°20'E, between latitudes 35°30'N and 36°10'N. The port of Sousse was to be given particular attention.
She was escorted out by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR) until 1530 A/30. (4)
30 Nov 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 35 during her 15th war patrol see the map below.
1 Dec 1942 Around 1900 A/1, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) arrived near Kerkennah No.4 buoy and patrolled in that vicinity during the night. (4)

2 Dec 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) sank the Italian transport Sacro Cuore (1097 GRT, built 1899) with gunfire about 15 nautical miles east of Mahdia, Tunisia in position 35°27'N, 11°22'E. She was on passage from Tripoli (departed at 0000 A/1) to Trapani. Twenty-one survivors reached Sousse in a lifeboat.

0904 A/2 - As no shipping had been encountered off Kerkennah, course was altered to 350° to proceed to the northwards to seaward of Mahedia as it was anticipated that enemy southbound shipping would probably keep well out of sight of the coast proceeding by direct route from Pantelleria to Kerkennah No.4 buoy.
1201 A/2 - Sighted smoke bearing 205°. This appeared to be a small coal-burning northbound merchant ship, too distant to intercept submerged.
1325 A/2 - Sighted smoke bearing 224°.
1425 A/2 - Smoke now bearing 240°. P 35 altered course to 360° to close.
1621 A/2 - Sighted funnel and masts with smoke bearing 250°. Increased speed to close.
1727 A/2 - Surfaced in position 35°23'N, 11°32'E to close the small merchant vessel. Enemy course was estimated as 330°, speed 7 knots.
1826 A/2 - Closed the enemy's starboard quarter and turned to a parallel course while overtaking. Position was now 35°27'N, 11°22'E.
1830 A/2 - Opened fire from 300 yards with the 12 pdr. gun. The target was soon stopped and on fire aft. When a boat was seen abandoning ship fire was checked. Eleven rounds had now been fired.

1838 A/2 - Started picking up survivors. They turned out to be Italians and German Luftwaffe men. The Luftwaffe men were retained on board as well as one other German. Ten Germans in all were taken prisoner.
1919 A/2 - Fired ten rounds into the waterline of the ship to finish her off. She was left on fire and settling in the water.
1923 A/2 - Proceeded to get clear of the area.
2100 A/2 - Set course to Malta to land the German survivors shortly afterwards.
2335 A/2 - Passed P 35's signal timed 2150 A/2 to the Capt. S.10 informing him of the intention to return to Malta with the prisoners. (4)

2 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 1603 A/2, the Capt. S.10 sent the following to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Aircraft report at 1400 A/2 two merchant ships of 5000 tons, one 6-inch cruiser, one destroyer and one sloop in position 35°21'N, 11°32'E, course 180°, speed 10 knots.
This signal however was only received at 1759 A/2 when plotting showed that the reported enemy convoy had passed about 11 miles to the west of P 35 around 1430 A/2.

This referred to the following convoy; Convoy 'C', made up of the transports Chisone (6168 GRT, built 1922), Veloce (5437 GRT, built 1911) and the Italian tanker Devoli (former Yugoslavian Perun, 3177 GRT, built 1939) escorted by Italian torpedo boats Lupo, Aretusa and Sagittario sailed from Naples at 2145 A/30-2320 A/30. At 0930 A/1, the Italian torpedo boat Ardente sailed from Palermo and joined the convoy off Trapani at 1630 A/1 and Devoli was detached to Trapani. At 1954 A/1, Sagittario was forced to leave the formation because of defects and arrived at Trapani at 0025 A/2. From 2130 A/1 to 0417 A/2, numerous flares were observed and several aircraft flew over the convoy without attacking it. At 1917 A/2, a single flare was seen near the convoy which began to make smoke. At about 2000 A/2, near Kerkennah, the convoy, which had split into two groups, Veloce and Lupo to the east of Chisone, Ardente and Aretusa was attacked by four Albacore torpedo bombers of 821 and 828 Squadron (FAA). Veloce was hit astern and set afire. At 2105 A/2, Lupo signalled that she would stand by her and ordered the other three to proceed. At 2346 A/2, Ardente observed gunfire from the direction of Lupo and assumed that she was being attacked by a naval force. Lupo and Veloce had been attacked by three or four destroyers and sunk. Because, the vessels were laying smoke, Ardente lost sight of Chisone and Aretusa she increased speed to 24 knots to interfere with the enemy force should it try to deal with Chisone. At 0635 A/3, two German Junker 88 were observed and one of them released its bombs before landing in the sea. The torpedo boat picked up its crew. From 1208 A/3, survivors of Veloce and Lupo were seen, and Ardente began to pick them up. At 1528 A/3, an enemy bomber appeared. Antiaircraft was opened fire from a distance of 3000 metres, and the torpedo boat avoided a torpedo. At 1635 A/3, the last survivor was picked up; in all 29 were from Lupo and 72 from Veloce, there were 135 victims from Lupo and 63 from Veloce. Supermarina ordered Ardente to return to Trapani. Chisone and Aretusa arrived at Tripoli at 1830 A/3-1845 A/3. Convoy 'C' had departed Naples on 30 November bound for Tripoli. It was made up of the Italian transports Chisone (6168 GRT, built 1922) and Veloce (5437 GRT, built 1911) and the Italian tanker Devoli (former Yugoslavian Perun, 3177 GRT, built 1939). The convoy was escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Lupo, Aretusa and Sagittario. The Italian torpedo boat Ardente joined on 1 December and Sagittario was detached also on 1 December. The convoy was attacked by torpedo bombers and the Veloce was sunk near Kerkennah on 2 December. Lupo which was picking up her survivors was sunk by a British destroyer force (see below for the composition of this destroyer force).
P 35 was also informed that Allied surface forces would be operating to the southward (Kerkennah area) during the night of 2/3 December.

This referred to the destroyers HMS Jervis (Capt. A.L. Poland, CB, DSO and Bar, DSC, RN), HMS Javelin (Lt.Cdr. W.F.N. Gregory-Smith, DSO and Bar, DSC, RN or Lt.Cdr. J.M. Alliston, DSC, RN), HMS Kelvin (Cdr. M.S. Townsend, OBE, DSC and Bar, RN) and HMS Nubian (Cdr. D.E. Holland-Martin, DSC, RN) which had departed Malta around 1400 A/2 and returned there around 0900 A/3. (36)

3 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 1305 A/3, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); HMS Welshman (Capt. W.H.D. Friedberger, RN) is approaching swept channel from south eastward A.M./4. She is informed of your movements. Four British MTB's will arrive at Malta from south-east around 0701 A/4 are ordered not to attack submarines. (37)
4 Dec 1942 Around 1245 A/4, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) ended her 15th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was swept in by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) which had joined around 1045 A/4. (4)
5 Dec 1942 Around 1300 A/5, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) departed Malta for her 16th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the Tunisian coast.
She was to patrol in an area bounded by the Tunisian coast and Longtitude 12°00'E between Latitudes 35°10'N and 36°10'N.
She was escorted out by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN) until 1450 A/5. (4)
5 Dec 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 35 during her 16th war patrol see the map below.
6 Dec 1942 At 1826 A/6, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) surfaced in position 35°28'N, 11°39'E and proceeded on course 270°.

At 1930 A/6, course was altered to 330° and speed was increased to 7.5 knots on receipt of the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1309 A/6 which said that P 35 may find it profitable to be lose of Sousse daily at dawn. (4)
7 Dec 1942 At 0130 A/7, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) altered course to 270° and proceeded into the Gulf of Hammamet.
At 0608 A/7, P 35 dived close to Sousse with the MDU (Mine Detector Unit) running until 1730 A/7 while in the vicinity of Sousse.

At 0835 A/7, P 35 fixed her position as being 35°57'N, 10°41'E. She then proceeded to investigate Sousse harbour. The French national flas was seen to be flying from the Citadel. Possibly there was a small steamship in the harbour and a schooner of about 800 tons was moored off the mole. After reconnoitring the harbour HMS P 35 patrolled to the eastward of Sousse.
At 1826 A/7, HMS P 35 surfaced in position 35°56'N, 10°55'E and during the night she patrolled to the north east of Sousse to close the port again at dawn the following morning. (4)
8 Dec 1942 At 0603 A/8, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) dived in position 35°59'N, 10°45'E.
At 0820 A/8, P 35 fixed her position as being 35°56'N, 10°44'E. She then patrolled between Sousse and Kuriat.
At 1400 A/8, P 35 went to 50 feet as periscope depth patrol became very difficult due to the worsening weather conditions.

At 2046 A/8, P 35 surfaced in position 35°44'N, 11°25'E. Heavy rain squalls were experienced during the night. Lt. Maydon decided to proceeded to the vicinity of Mahedia to patrol there the following day.
At 2130 A/8, P 35 closed the coast south of Hammamet to intercept an important southbound target which was expected around daylight on the 9th. (4)
9 Dec 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) grounded near Hammamet. With some effort she came off a little over an hour later.
Later the same day, HMS P 35 torpedoed and sank the German transport Süllberg (1663 GRT, built 1912) south of Hammamet, Tunisia in position 36°14'N, 10°32'E.

Süllberg was carrying 645 tons of ammunition, 62 tons of stores, 4 guns and 23 vehicles from Trapani (sailed at 1415 A/8) to Sousse. The tug Vega was sailed to her assistance but Süllberg had already sunk.
0405 A/9 - Started sounding which gave a depth of 36 fathoms. Lt. Maydon gave orders to reduce speed to 6 knots at 0500 A/9 when the estimated position would be 36°19'30"N, 10°38'E.

0445 A/9 - Sound gave a depth of 33 fathoms which agreed with the charted soundings for the estimated position P 35 was in. Visibility was very variable due to rain squalls and at its worst visibility was very bad. Also P 35 was rolling heavily due a fairly heavy sea and swell which effected the accuracy of the echo sounder.
0451 A/9 - Grounded. The motors were put to full astern but P 35 was unable to get off. Main ballast was also blown to obtain full buoyancy but this also did not help.

0505 A/9 - Lt. Maydon ordered all main ballast to be flooded again to ensure P 35 was not driven further ashore by the breaking sea and that the bow was kept onto the land. Instructions were now given to drain down the torpedo tubes and pump out all torpedo compensating tanks and auxiliary tanks with the exception of tank 'X' which is right aft and was to be flooded fully. At the same time demolition charges were placed and fuses prepared to destroy the submarine if necessary and a start was made on the burning of signal publications and confidential matter. 300 Gallons of water were pumped out from forward leaving sufficient to continue the patrol when the submarine was reloated.
0550 A/9 - All tanks empty with the exception of 'X' auxiliary, fresh water, fuel and lubricating oil. Blew No.1, 2, 3 and 4 main ballasts using high pressure air. Also went full astern.
0552 A/9 - Blew No.5 and 6 main ballast.
0554 A/9 - Dropped the drop keel. P 35 now broke loose. Proceeded astern and then turned the bow through south.
0615 A/9 - P 35 was now on course 140° at a speed of 9 knots. The trim of the submarine was re-adjusted.
0641 A/9 - Dived and proceeded to the southward.

0732 A/9 - P 35 fixed her position by land bearings as being 36°19'N, 10°41'E. The bow caps of the torpedo tubes were test and found correct. Other equipment was also tested and it seemed like no damage was sustained following the grounding. Lt. Maydon decided to continue the patrol close to the south of Hammamet.

0930 A/9 - Sighted a small merchant vessel, estimated to be 2000 to 3000 tons, bearing 342°. She was proceeding close inshore south of Hammamet. Estimated enemy course was 250°. Altered course and went to full submerged speed to intercept further South on the coast.
0945 A/9 - Enemy course was now seen to be 235°, speed was 9 knots. The target was keeping very close inshore. P 35 proceeded to the South-West at full speed.
1057 A/9 - Turned to a firing course.

1100 A/9 - In position 36°14'N, 10°32'E fired 3 torpedoes from 4500 yards. A very heavy explosion followed after about 4 minutes. Another very slight explosions followed about 15 seconds after the heavy explosion.
1109 A/9 - Returned to periscope depth. All that was left of the target was a magnificent column of orange and white smoke rising to about 1000 feet in the air.
1223 A/9 - P 35 fixed her position as being 36°29'N, 10°36'E. Patrolled to the eastward and completed reloading the torpedo tubes.
1835 A/9 - Surfaced in position 36°04'N, 10°57'E.
1950 A/9 - Passed P 35's signal timed 1747 A/9 to the Capt. S.10 reporting the grounding and destruction of the target.
2103 A/9 - P 35 had to dive for an aircraft.
2156 A/9 - Surfaced in position 36°05'N, 11°15'E. Visibility was bad due to very heavy thunder and rainsqualls. P 35 withdrew to the south-west to patrol off Mahedia the following day. (4)

10 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 1821 A/10, the Capt. S.10 sent the following to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); Your 1747 A/9. Well done, you have freedom of action in your area but the northernmost boundary is now 35°55'N.

In a signal timed 1915 A/10, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 35; Yellow dinghy with two airmen on board in position 305° - Lampion - 12 miles at 1100 A/10 and 270° - Lampion - 8 miles at 1600 A/10 thought to be Beaufighter crew but may be Germans. Provisions and pyrotechnics delivered by air this afternoon and latter may have sunk. Estimate their positions at 0200 A/11 to be 35°30'N, 12°00'E. Close them by this time when aircraft will fire two Green Very lights overhead and dinghy should reply. If unsuccessful search by daylight surfacing at your discretion. On recovery report, returning to Malta if British, continuing patrol if prisoners. Dinghy was using life jacket as sail. (37)

11 Dec 1942 At 0144 A/11, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0020 A/11 which stated that the airmen sighted were considered hostile and that own aircraft would not co-operate in the search. P 35 was instructed to make a quick search at dawn an then return to her patrol area.
At 0200 A/11, HMS P 35 was estimated to be in position 35°30'N, 12°00'E. Course was altered to 180° and speed was reduced to 4 knots to search.
At 0400 A/11, course was altered to 020°.
At 0604 A/11, HMS P 35 dived in position 35°30'N, 12°00'E. Course was set to 360°.
At 0630 A/11, course was altered to 180°.
At 0715 A/11, course was altered to 090°.
At 0822 A/11, in estimated position 35°29'N, 12°03'E the dinghy with two men was sighted.
At 0835 A/11, HMS P 35 picked up two German airmen from the dinghy. At 0909 A/11, course was set to 270° to return to the patrol area.
At 0911 A/11, HMS P 35 dived.
At 1034 A/11, a column of smoke was sighted bearing 113°. P 35 altered course and proceeded at full submerged speed to investigate.
At 1122 A/11, nothing was sighted and nothing was heard on the Asdic. The chase was now abandoned and course 270° was resumed to return to the patrol area.
At 2038 A/11, HMS P 35 surfaced in position 35°31'N, 11°33'E. The signal timed 0955 A/11 was then passed to the Capt. S.10 which reported the rescue of the two German airmen.

At 2349 A/11, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2314 A/11 was received which ordered P 35 to be close off Sousse at dawn/12 to intercept southbound coastal traffic, but no to approach within 3 miles of the entrance to the harbour as own aircraft had laid mines there. Course was thus set for the Gulf of Hammamet and speed was increased to 8.5 knots. (4)
12 Dec 1942 At 0558 A/12, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) dived in estimated position 35°58'N, 10°51'E.
At 0745 A/12, HMS P 35 fixed her position as being 35°56'N, 10°45'E which was about 7.5 miles north-east of Sousse. She then commenced to patrol in the vicinity.

At 0800 A/12, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0710 A/12 was received which stated the following; Aircraft reports at 0600 A/12, two merchant ships of 2000 and 500 tons in position 36°05'N, 10°35'E, course 180° speed 7 knots. Targets justify heavy torpedo expenditure.

At 1122 A/12, in approximate position 35°56'N, 10°46'E, a landing craft was sighted bearing 220° proceeding on an estimated course of 140°. The speed of this craft was not at first appreciated and estimation of the range was very difficult owing to its low silhouette. P 35 turned to attack setting the one remaining MK.VIII torpedo to 4 feet depth. The enemy was pursued almost continuously for 40 minutes but this failed to close the range at all and the attack had to be abandoned at 1215 A/12. It was estimated this landing craft was doing at least 10 knots. The enemy was last seen steering 120° for the boat channel to the south of Kuriat.
Probably this was either the German F 477 or F 479 which were both on their way from Bizerta to Sfax.

At 2031 A/12, HMS P 35 surfaced in position 35°51'N, 11°13'E. She then received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1620 A/12 which cancelled his signal timed 0710 A/12. HMS P 35 was ordered to be within 12 miles from Sousse at 0600 A/13.
P 35 patrolled north of Kuriat during the night until it was time to set course to return to Sousse.

At 2238 A/12, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1840 A/12 was received with amended to patrol to the Gulf of Hammamet west of 11°08'E. She was also ordered to leave patrol to return to Malta after dark on 14 December. (4)

13 Dec 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) attacked an old-fashioned merchant ship of 1500 tons but missed. This was the Italian transport Docilitas (1246 GRT, built 1900) who was on a trip from Trapani to Sousse.
Docilitas had sailed from Trapani at 0735 A/12, via Sousse (1600 A/13-1900 A/13) and Sfax (2000 A/14-1800 A/18) and arrived at Tripoli at 1815 A/19.

Shortly afterwards HMS P 35 torpedoed and damaged the German troop transport Macedonia (2875 GRT, built 1922) about 5 nautical miles north of Sousse, Tunisia in position 35°55'N, 10°38'E. The damaged German ship was beached and abandoned. She was on trip from Palermo to Sousse carrying 1000 tons of fuel for tanks. She was in company with the small German transport Jaedjoer (308 GRT, built 1937) who had joined from Trapani and they were escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Cigno. On the morning of the 13th, Jaedjoer had been lost from sight, Cigno had turned back and found her adrift as her engine had broken down. The torpedo boat took her in tow and brought her to Ras Mahmur while Macedonia had continued independently. Cigno rejoined her only after she was hit by P 35.

Macedonia had been hit and taken a list, the torpedo tracks had not been seen and it was believed that she had been mined. Cigno arrived on the scene to find her abandoned except for her captain and three crew members. The torpedo-boat took her in tow and asked Sousse for assistance. The tug Optimiste sailed but arrived too late, Macedonia had sunk in shallow waters (12 metres). At this time Supermarina informed Cigno that Macedonia had been the victim of a submarine and she was ordered to hunt it. It is interesting to note that Naval Headquarters in Rome were more informed on the local situation than the participants themselves. They were often accused of interfering too much with operations but this time they were justified. At 1930 hours Cigno obtained an echo and at 1945 hours dropped a pattern of depth charges but then lost contact. It was assumed that it was a false echo and the chase abandoned.

At 0520 A/12, the German transport Macedonia (2875 GRT, built 1922) escorted by Cigno sailed from Palermo at 8 knots. The small German transport Jaedjoer (309 GRT, built 1938) sailed from Trapani at 1400 A/12 and joined the convoy but she could only make 6 knots. During the night of 12/13, in heavy fog, Cigno lost track of Jaedjoer. At 0705 A/13, aerial escort having arrived, Cigno signalled Macedonia to proceed independently and turned back to look for Jaedjoer. At 0822 A/13, the torpedo boat sighted her and the German vessel reported that she had engine defects which could not be repaired. The torpedo boat passed her a tow, and they proceeded at 10 knots. At 1200 A/13, Cigno received a signal from Supermarina ordering her to leave Jaedjoer at Ras Mahmur and rejoin Macedonia. This was done at 1445 A/13, and the torpedo boat proceeded at 24 knots to catch up with the other vessel. At 1630 A/13, she sighted Macedonia which had been torpedoed and had a slight list. The vessel had been abandoned, and her Captain informed Cigno that he believed she had been mined. At 1702 A/13, the torpedo boat took her in tow. At 1900 A/13, Macedonia sank in shallow water. The French tug Optimiste arrived and was told to recover the barrels of petrol. Fifteen minutes later, Supermarina ordered Cigno to hunt the submarine and, at 1945 A/13, she dropped a pattern of depth charges. At 2020 A/13, she sighted the German transport Lisboa (1799 GRT, built 1919) which had sailed from Sousse and escorted her to Trapani. However, at 0254 A/14, Cigno was ordered by Supermarina to abandon the escort of Lisboa and escort Jaedjoer which was now again under way. She sighted her at 0758 A/14 and escorted her to Sousse. They entered the port at 1213 A/14, Cigno just dropped anchor and landed in a boat with the military passengers she had picked up. They had barely arrived when six Boston bombers of the USAAF attacked and dropped small bombs, none hit. The torpedo boat was instructed to escort the motor refrigeration ship Assab (625 GRT, built 1919) to Trapani, and they sailed at 1347 A/14 and arrived at destination at 1153 A/15.
0557 A/13 - Dived in estimated position 36°02'N, 10°46'E.
0858 A/13 - Fixed position as being 35°59'N, 10°39'E.
0910 A/13 - Sighted a three-masted schooner approach the area mined with British mines off Sousse. The schooner was approaching from the eastward.

This may have been the Giuseppina Chiesa (213 GRT, built 1912) which had sailed from Tripoli (she had suffered some damage during an air attack on Tripoli on 29 November 1942). During the night of 12/13 December, she was machine-gunned by an enemy aircraft without result. She arrived at Sousse on the 13th and was sunk there during an air attack at 1900 A/14.

1300 A/13 - Fixed the position as being 35°54'N, 10°42'E and also received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1155 A/12 which ordered HMS P 35 to keep within striking distance of the coastal route north of Sousse except when charging.
1403 A/13 – Sighted masts and funnel of a small steamship, very close to shore bearing 285°, estimated course 160°.
1442 A/13 – In position 35°54'N, 10°39'E, fired two torpedoes aimed at the foremast and mainmast then altered course to starboard and dived to 45 feet increasing speed to clear the torpedo tracks.

1445 A/13 – Explosion (muffled) after 3m. 9s. Torpedoes missed probably due to an overestimation of speed. [Docilitas was missed and luckily for her, the appearance of another ship drew the attention away from her and she reached Sousse safely.]
1449 A/13 - Sighted a vessel bearing 327°. The enemy was seen to be a merchant vessel of about 3000 tons on a course of 152°. Started attack.

1522 A/13 - In position 35°55'N, 10°38'E fired one torpedo aimed at the funnel. 47 Seconds after firing the torpedo hit the target. The target was run aground to prevent her from sinking. She was down by the bow and listing to port. P 35 then withdrew to the northward but remained in the area.
1615 A/13 - An Italian torpedo boat was sighted closing the target.

1651 A/13 - The torpedo boat went alongside the target as had been hoped. Unfortunately it was on the far side. It was decided to fire the last torpedo in the tubes at the merchant vessel hoping to finish this ship off and damaging the torpedo boat as well.
1653 A/13 - Fired on the torpedo at the merchant vessel from 4100 yards. Unfortunately it missed. Withdrew to reload a torpedo as fast as possible.
1746 A/13 - Completed the reload. Stalked the torpedo boat that was now stopped a little distance from the grounded merchant vessel.
1812 A/13 - When about to open fire on the torpedo boat it shifted position. As the torpedo boat shifted position several times it was not possible to attack.

1947 A/13 - By now the torpedo boat had obtained contact on P 35 which had gone to 90 feet. First 6 and then 3 depth charges were dropped in rapid succession. Minor damage was caused by these depth charges. No further depth charges were dropped.
2135 A/13 - Nothing was heard on Asdic and P 35 returned to periscope depth.

2156 A/13 - Surfaced in position 35°58'N, 10°46'E and proceeded on course 060° to get clear of the Gulf of Hammamet at 8 knots. Lt. Maydon decided to return to Malta as only one torpedo was remaining. (4)
15 Dec 1942 Around 1020 A/15, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) ended her 16th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) which had joined around 0720 A/15. (38)
19 Dec 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) is docked in No.5 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. (39)
21 Dec 1942 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) is undocked. (39)
27 Dec 1942 Around 1230 A/27, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) departed Malta for patrol off Naples.
In a signal timed 2141 A/27, she informed the Capt. S.10 that she would return to Malta due to a suspected case of diphtheria. Her E.T.A. at Malta was estimated to be around 0800 A/28. (40)
28 Dec 1942 Around 0945 A/28, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) returned to Malta. (39)
6 Jan 1943 Around 1300 A/6, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) departed Malta for her 17th war patrol (16th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the south of Naples.

7 Jan 1943 At 1652 A/7, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) altered course to 176° for position 36°00'N, 11°40'E in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1257 A/7 which contained the following orders; Cancel previous patrol orders. Proceed through position 36°00'N, 11°40'E to establish patrol in area to westward of line 340° from Kerkenah No.4 buoy. If you attack or are sighted off Mahedia move to Gulf of Hammamet and vice versa. South and northbound coastal traffic is your objective. (41)

8 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1105 A/8, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN); Move north of latitude 36°00'N by 2200 A/8 owing to own surface forces operating south of Kuriat.
This referred to an anti-shipping sweep during then night of 8/9 January by the destroyers HMS Kelvin (Cdr. M.S. Townsend, OBE, DSC, RN) and HMS Nubian (Cdr. D.E. Holland-Martin, DSC, RN) (42)

9 Jan 1943 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian merchant Emilio Morandi (1523 GRT, built 1904) about 35 nautical miles east-north-east of Sousse, Tunisia in position 35°58'N, 11°20'E. She was in company with the Italian motorized lighters MZ 727, MZ 736, MZ 744 and MZ 755. They were on passage from Tripoli to Trapani.

Morandi sank in 12 minutes, the lighters were hampered by the bad weather but picked up 23 survivors and landed them at Pantelleria. The Italian torpedo boat Partenope later arrived on the scene of the sinking and searched for survivors but found none.

They had sailed from Tripoli at 2200 A/6 for Trapani. After the sinking of Emilio Morandi, the four F-lighters arrived at Pantelleria at 1030 A/10 and sailed from there at 1810 A/10, arriving at Trapani at 1230 A/11.
0600 A/9 - Dived in position 35°52'N, 11°20'E.
1033 A/9 - Fixed position off Kuriat as being 35°49'N, 11°09'E.
1416 A/9 - Sighted a merchant ship bearing 190°. Enemy course was 345°. Altered course to intercept.
1440 A/9 - The target was a 2000 tons old-fashioned merchant vessel. She was Northbound and in ballast. She was escorted by 4 'F-boats' (Tank landing craft).

1514 A/9 - In position 35°37'N, 11°09'E fired a torpedo at the target from 1200 yards. Due to an error in drill the torpedo was fired to late resulting in a miss. Another torpedo was then fired but this one also missed.
1820 A/9 - Surfaced in position 35°49'N, 11°12'E and set off in pursuit.
2003 A/9 - In position 35°58'N, 11°20'E fired another torpedo from 600 yards. After 27 seconds it hit the target. P 35 dived after firing and retired from the area. No counter attack followed.
2145 A/9 - Surfaced in position 35°54'N, 11°21'E and proceeded southwards. (4)

10 Jan 1943 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) attacked a small tanker with torpedo and gunfire off Kuriat. The tanker was able to escape into shallow water. This was most probably the Italian water carrier Teodolinda (361 GRT, built 1925) in company with the Italian tug R 97 / Genova (91 GRT, built 1918) escorted by the Italian auxiliary schooner R 112 / Maria di Piedigrotta (62 GRT, built 1932). They were on passage from Tripoli (departed at 1500 A/6). None of the ships sustained damage. After the attack took refuge in Monastir.
0600 A/10 - Dived in position 35°28'N, 11°14'E.
0729 A/10 - Fixed position as being 35°27'N, 11°11'E.
1300 A/10 - Sighted smoke bearing 207°. Altered course to close.
1343 A/10 - The smoke was seen to come from a small tanker of about 1000 tons. She was northbound and close inshore. Altered course to intercept her in deeper water between Ras Dimass and Kuriat.
1440 A/10 - The tanker was seen to be escorted by a single masted auxiliary schooner armed with a small automatic gun forward and a small tug that was thought to be unarmed.
1502 A/10 - In position 35°41'N, 11°06'E fired one torpedo at the tanker. It missed.
1637 A/10 - Surfaced in position 35°49'N, 11°06'E and proceeded at full speed in pursuit of the target.

1641 A/10 - Opened fire with the gun from a range of 5000 to 6000 tons. The target fled into the Gulf of Monastir. P 35 was unable to follow in the shallow water. After about 20 minutes 43 rounds had been fired and at least one hit was obtained.
1710 A/10 - Dived and retired to seaward on course 070°.
1715 A/10 - Fixed position as being 35°51'N, 11°01'E.
1846 A/10 - Surfaced in position 35°52'N, 11°05'E. (4)
11 Jan 1943 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) attacked a 2000 ton merchant vessel with one torpedo off the Gulf of Hammamet in position 36°26'N, 11°01'E. The torpedo missed its target.

This was most likely the Italian transport Siculo (1481 GRT, built 1906) which was in company with the Italian schooner Luciano (264 GRT, built 1902) and Italian submarine chaser AS 95 / Garibaldi (81 GRT, built 1938) which sailed from Tripoli at 2100 A/7 via Sfax (0800 A/10) and arrived at Trapani at 1150 A/12.

Later the same day she attacked the Italian sailing vessels Nuovo Domenico I (25 GRT, built 1938) and Concetta Falco (152 GRT, built 1920) with gunfire in the Gulf of Hammamet in position 36°24'N, 10°43'E. The Nuovo Domenico I is damaged during the attack.

Italian sources just indicate that Nuovo Domenico I beached herself two miles north-east of Hammamet on 10 January and was declared a total loss on 14 January. Concetta Falco reached Bizerta and had to undergo repairs. In May 1943 she was scuttled there.
0609 A/11 - Dived in position 36°27'N, 11°07'E and proceeded on course 300°.
0704 A/11 - Sighted an object bearing 305°, altered course and increased speed.

0712 A/11 - Identified the target as a 2000 tons, old fashioned merchant vessel, with a tall thin funnel and two masts. The vessel was eastbound and in ballast and was escorted by two auxiliary schooners.

0738 A/11 - In position 36°26'N, 11°01'E fired one torpedo from 3800 yards. As the ship changed course and manned her gun the torpedo was most likely spotted and evaded. The target made off to the north. P 35 now withdrew to the south before closing the coast.
1220 A/11 - Fixed position as being 36°23'N, 10°54'E.
1617 A/11 - Sighted two sailing vessels leaving Hammamet eastbound. Closed to intercept.

1702 A/11 - Surfaced in position 36°24'N, 10°43'E and opened fire on both sailing vessels with Lewis guns and one the larger one also with the 12-pdr. deck gun. Range was 2000 yards. Both sailing vessels turned to the North-West and ran for the shore. The smaller beached herself. The crew of the larger one attempted to board two small boats but as the ship was still under way both capsized and the crew was seen to swim to the shore.

1712 A/11 - Range to the larger sailing vessel was now 1000 yards. She was abandoned and drifting ashore. Ceased fire. 24 Rounds had been fired for 5 hits. The larger sailing vessel was also on fire. P 35 then retired to the south to make for deeper water before diving.
1900 A/11 - Surfaced in position 36°23'N, 10°49'E and proceeded on course 130° to get clear of the area.
2243 A/11 - Sighted light bearing 132°.
2250 A/11 - Increased speed to close and went to action stations.
2314 A/11 - Sighted more lights and the contact appeared to be a well illuminated ship which was now bearing 110°.

2359 A/11 - Position was now 36°06'N, 11°24'E. The target was most probably a northbound hospital ship. P 35 was unable to close sufficiently to identify it. The chase was broken off and course was set to the southward.
The Italian hospital ship Gradisca (13868 GRT, built 1913) sailed from Tripoli at 1740 A/11 and arrived at Naples at 1340 A/13 and could not have reached this position at this time.

Outside possibility: the small Italian hospital ship Capri (630 GRT, built 1930) sailed from Palermo at 0020 A/11 and arrived at Pantelleria at 0915 A/12. Normally, she was not northbound unless she sailed southward toward Tripoli and then turned back to Pantelleria. (4)
12 Jan 1943 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) attacked a large German landing craft with a torpedo off Mahedia, Tunisia. The torpedo ran under.
This vessel has not been yet identified but the two small landing craft were probably SF 211 and SF 258 which were operating in the area.
0552 A/12 - Dived in estimated position 35°38'N, 11°18'E.
0831 A/12 - Sighted a southbound two-masted schooner of about 250 ton to the north of Mahedia.
0835 A/12 - Increased speed and set course to intercept this schooner about 5 miles south of Mahedia with the intention of surfacing and attack with the gun.

0943 A/12 - Surfaced in position 35°27'N, 11°08'E but sighted two German landing craft with another peculiar craft, with a structure not unlike a U-boat conning tower in company. All were close inshore to the southward. It was decided to engage this vessel with a torpedo so dived again and closed.
0948 A/12 - Identified the target as a large 'Siebel ferry' (tank landing craft). Set the torpedo in No.4 tube to 4 feet. Continued to close to attack from close range.
1018 A/12 - In position 35°27'N, 11°06'E fired one torpedo from 500 yards. The torpedo ran under. All three landing craft went into Mahedia harbour.
1117 A/12 - Sighted a northbound two-masted schooner, closed preparatory to surfacing and gunning.
1127 A/12 - Sighted the masts and bridge of an Italian torpedo boat bearing 050° on a southerly course at a range of about 5 miles.

This was Perseo which had sailed from Palermo at 0045 A/11, escorting the Italian tanker Gimma (1542 GRT, built 1897) to Sousse. The tanker entered Sousse at 0815 A/12 and the torpedo boat continued to Tripoli. At 0840 A/12, she was circled by four Spitfires and three Beaufighters which remained out of range of her antiaircraft guns and finally arrived at Tripoli at 0710 A/13.
1134 A/12 - Altered course to 020° to reach deeper water. Very distant Asdic transmissions were heard from the torpedo boat which course was estimated to be 155°.
1150 A/12 - P 35 fixed her position as being 35°24'N, 11°07'E.
1200 A/12 - The torpedo boat disappeared on approximate bearing 130°.

1630 A/12 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1346 A/12 which informed HMS P 35 that Allied destroyers would patrol between Kerkennah and Kuriat between 2200 A/12 and 0200 A/13. P 35 was ordered to be north of 36°00'N during this anti-shipping sweep.

This referred to an anti-shipping sweep by HMS Nubian (Cdr. D.E. Holland-Martin, DSC, RN) and HMS Paladin (Lt.Cdr. L.St.G. Rich, RN). They departed Malta around 1500 A/12 and returned around 1030 A/13. They had not encountered enemy shipping.
1845 A/12 - Surfaced in position 35°43'N, 11°14'E and proceeded northwards to patrol in the vicinity of Ras Mahmur. (4)

14 Jan 1943 At 0016 A/14, north-east of Kuriat, in position 35°58'N, 11°20'E, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) sighted a flashing blue light which looked like morse code and dark shape bearing approximately 070°. P 35 went to action stations and closed at full speed to investigate. P 35 was in an unfortunate position being up moon from the target so she was well silhouetted when closing on a course of 100°.
At 0028 A/14, the target appeared to be a small ship whose course was between 190° and 210° with at least two small craft in company.
At 0032 A/13, more morse code flashing with the blue light was seen. The message appeared to be in coded groups of letters, quite unintelligible.

At 0034 A/13, the target appeared to turn towards. Range was estimated as being 3000 yards but soon it became apparent that the target had turned away and was further off. P 35 dived hoping to carry out a moonlight submerged attack, course 110°. Fast reciprocating engine HE was heard which soon faded on bearing 080°. There was insufficient light to see anything unless very close with the periscope.
At 0100 A/14, HMS P 35 surfaced and proceeded on course 160°, endeavouring to intercept the enemy to the southward.
At 0102 A/14, The target was sighted bearing 040°, flashing with blue light again and once more on a southerly course. HMS P 35 turned to 055° to attack.
At 0112 A/14, the target turned towards, range was about 1500 yards.
At 0113 A/14, HMS P 35 dived and altered course to 160°. HE passed down the port side and faded astern.
At 0131 A/14, HMS P 35 altered course to 180°. No more HE was heard.
At 0138 A/14, HMS P 35 surfaced and continued on course 180° at 10 knots. >p>At 0140 A/14, a red Very's light was fired on bearing 015°. Course was altered to 010°. The moon was setting.
At 0159 A/14, two small craft were sighted bearing 005° fine on the port bow at a range of about 500 yards. P 35 dived.
At 0202 A/14, P 35 altered course to 180°. No HE was heard.

At 0220 A/14, P 35 surfaced in position 35°56'N, 11°25'E and continued on course 180° at 10 knots endeavouring to intercept the enemy to the southward at dawn. Estimated the enemy was making for the coast of Mehedia and would then proceeded south-east to Kerkennah.
At 0400 A/14, P 35 altered course to 220° to cross the enemy's expected track to intercept at dawn.
At 0558 A/14, P 35 altered course to 180°. Sighted a white Very's light fired on bearing 070°, very distant. Probably the enemy had crossed astern of P 35 to the eastward.
[We have no clue on which vessel(s) were encountered during the night.]
At 0607 A/14, P 35 dived in position 35°27'N, 11°12'E.
At 0733 A/14, P 35 fixed her position as being 35°24'N, 11°09'E. Course was set to investigate the area between Mahedia and Khadija.
At 0910 A/14, a small northbound sailing vessel was sighted close inshore.
At 1000 A/14, in position 35°19'N, 11°07'E, another small northbound sailing vessels was sighted.
At 1600 A/14, P 35 was in position 35°25'N, 11°10'E. Nothing further had been sighted.

At 1842 A/14, P 35 surfaced in position 35°30'N, 11°11'E. The Capt. S.10's signal timed 1726 A/14 was received which ordered HMS P 35 to keep south of 35°50'N after 1200 A/15 as HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) would be operating to the north of this area. Course was set to patrol off Kuriat during the night. (4)

15 Jan 1943 HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) encountered an enemy merchant vessel off the Gulf of Hammamet. P 35 and the enemy vessel then opened fire on each other. As the merchant's gunfire was rather accurate P 35 dived to avoid being hit. P 35 later surfaced and set off to chase the enemy but she was unable to obtain a favourable attack position so the chase had to be abandoned.

The enemy vessel encountered was the old Italian steamer Gimma (1542 GRT, built 1897) which had sailed from Sousse at 1530 A/15 via Pantelleria (1115 A/16-2100 A/17) and arrived at Palermo at 1420 A/18. She claimed to have hit the submarine with the 8th round from her 76mm gun, forcing the submarine to break off the action and submerge.
15 January 1943
0156 A/15 - Sighted a flickering white light bearing 270°. Closed at full speed.
0229 A/15 - Sighted two flickering lights bearing 285°. Went to action stations.

0234 A/15 - Sighted a light on the water very close ahead. Went full astern. This turned out to be a small calcium flare floating on the water and giving off a bright flame intermittently, a very white light, in estimated position 36°05'N, 11°23'E.
0245 A/15 - Set course to 090°.
0600 A/15 - Dived in position 35°51'N, 11°20'E.
0915 A/15 - Fixed position as being 35°52'N, 11°11'E. Patrolled of Kuriat.
1332 A/15 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1156 A/15 ordering HMS P 35 to steer to the north and endeavour to surface north of Kuriat after dark. Set course to comply with this order.

1715 A/15 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1221 A/15 in rather corrupt form ordering HMS P 35 to position 36°29'N, 10°55'E by 2301 A/15 and HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) to patrol in a position due east of P 35 by 2359 A/15.
In another signal, timed 1241 A/15, the Capt. S.10 ordered P 35 to withdraw after attack or at 0401 A/16 to latitude 36°07'N and thence return to Malta.
1828 A/15 - Surfaced in position 35°57'N, 11°09'E and continued on to the position ordered.
2155 A/15 - In position 36°20'N, 10°59'E sighted a ship bearing 320°. Went to night action stations.
2203 A/15 - Identified the vessel as a northbound merchant vessel. Altered course to the north and went to full speed to intercept.
2208 A/15 - The targets course was seen to be 055°. The target was thought to be of 2000 tons with 2 masts and a thin tall funnel. Commenced chase on her starboard quarter.
2230 A/15 - As we were gaining very slowly and our position was unsuitable for a torpedo attack, manned the 12pdr gun and a Lewis gun. Altered course to close.

2233 A/15 - The target must have sighted P 35 as she opened fire on the submarine. P 35 now also opened fire. Range was about 3000 yards. The enemy's shooting was very accurate and the action was broken off and P 35 dived at 2236 hours.
2311 A/15 - Surfaced and commenced chase.
2325 A/15 - The target appeared to have turned and to come towards. Dived with the intention to attack with a torpedo.
2340 A/15 - Lost sight of the target through the periscope. Heard no HE.
2341 A/15 - Surfaced in position 36°23'N, 11°09'E and again set off in pursuit.
16 January 1943 0040 A/16 - Unable to close the target which was making much smoke and obviously doing her best speed. Abandoned the chase and set course for Malta. (4)
17 Jan 1943 Around 1115 A/17, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) ended her 17th war patrol (16th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (4)

17 Jan 1943 At 0107 A/17, in position 36°02'N, 13°21'E, HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN) dived for a submarine zig-zagging on an opposite mean course. This was presumed to be HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN).
HMS P 35 reported nothing at this time though. She did had altered course before to get of <>P 35's route but apparently this was thus not successful. (43)
24 Jan 1943 Around 1600 A/24, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) departed Malta for the U.K. where she was to refit. The first leg of the trip was to Algiers.
For the daily positions of HMS P 35 during this passage see the map below.

26 Jan 1943 At 1845 A/26, about 20 miles west of Marettimo Island, in approximate position 37°57'N, 11°19'E, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) heard fast HE (300 revolutions) crossing the bow from starboard to port. The HE was first heard on bearing 355° and finally faded out on bearing 340°. It was suspected that this was a patrolling motor torpedo boat. Lt. Maydon decided to surfaced later and only did so at 2010 A/26. (4)
30 Jan 1943 At 0936 A/30, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) surfaced and passed her signal timed 0915 A/30 informing the Capt. S.8 that she was three hours late.
At 1122 A/30, P 35 sighted her escort, HMML 273 (?).
Rendezvous was effected at 1138 A/30.
Around 1400 A/30, HMS P 35 arrived at Algiers. (4)
31 Jan 1943 Around 1730 A/31, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) departed Algiers for Gibraltar. (4)
4 Feb 1943 Around 1300 A/4, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) arrived at Gibraltar from Algiers. (4)
7 Feb 1943 Around 1400 A/7, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) departed Gibraltar for the U.K.

P 35 was to pass through positions 35°55'N, 06°05'W, 35°53'N, 06°15'W at 2100 A/7, 36°37'N, 07°24'W at 0630 A/8 and 43°01'N, 13°50'W at 2300 A/12. Further routing would be signalled by the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.).

8 Feb 1943 In a signal timed 2347/8 the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) ordered HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) to pass through position 43°01'N, 12°26'E at 1900 A/12 [instead of 43°01'N, 13°50'W at 2300 A/12], thence by 'Red' route. (44)

9 Feb 1943 At 2030 A/9, HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) received the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic's (F.O.C.N.A.) signal timed 1528 A/9 which ordered her to pass through positions 41°00'N, 10°01'W and 43°01'N, 11°10'W thence as routed by the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.).

In a signal timed 2127 A/9, F.O.S. signalled the following orders to P 35; 1) Estimate you will pass position 43°01'N, 11°10'W at 1800 A/12. Proceed dived by day through position 46°02'N, 11°09'E, 49°00'N, 11°10'E, 49°10'N, 09°00'E, 180° - Bishop Rock - 10 miles at 0800 A/17, thence on the surface at the Commanding Officers discretion to make rendezvous in position 180° - Lizard Head - 10 miles at 1500 A/17 for onward escort to Plymouth. 2) Estimate your speed during the night of 12/13 7 knots, and daily advance 115 miles. (44)
17 Feb 1943 At 1820 A/17, HMS Umbra (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, DSO, RN) arrived at Plymouth.

At 1237 A/17, she had made rendezvous with HMS Fir (T/Skr. W.H. Buley, RNR) at the end of the swept channel to Plymouth. HMS Umbra had arrived early at the rendezvous and had signalled to the Lizard signalled station that she would proceeded towards the end of the swept channel and meet her escort there.

HMS Umbra started her refit at the Devonport Dockyard on 24 February 1943. Refit was completed in late July 1943. [As no log is available for July 1943 some details for this month may be missing.] (4)

1 Aug 1943 HMS Umbra (Lt. P.H. May, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Plymouth with HMCS Athabaskan (Cdr. G.R. Miles, DSO, OBE, RCN), HMS Cattistock (Lt. R.G.D. Keddie, DSC, RN), HMS Cambridgeshire (T/Lt. H.F.B. Ashby, RNVR), HMS Ambrose Pare (Skr. J.W. Morris, RNR), HMS Victrix (T/Lt. J.D. Craighead, RNR) and HMS Kingston Andalusite (T/Lt. W.S. Lowe, RNVR). (45)

4 Oct 1943 Around 1345 A/4, HMS Umbra (Lt. P.H. May, RN) departed Rothesay for Fishguard. She was escorted by HMS Kihna (A/Cdr.(Retd.) A.R.W. Sayle, RD, RNR). (47)
5 Oct 1943 Around 1820 A/5, HMS Umbra (Lt. P.H. May, RN) and HMS Kihna (A/Cdr.(Retd.) A.R.W. Sayle, RD, RNR) arrived at Fishguard from Rothesay.
Based at Fishguard HMS Umbra was to participate in A/S exercises with aircraft. (47)

19 Nov 1943 HMS Umbra (Lt. A.G. Chandler, RNR) was towed from Fishguard to Milford Haven by the tug HMS Abeille IV. Both her main engines were damaged and could not be used. They arrived at Milford Haven later the same day.
They were escorted by the M/S drifter HMS Glen Heather (T/Skr. F.A. Nunn, DSC, RNR). (48)
24 Nov 1943 HMS Umbra (Lt. A.G. Chandler, RNR) conducted stationary engine trials. These were however unsatisfactory. (48)
4 Dec 1943 At 0730 A/4, HMS Umbra (Lt. A.G. Chandler, RNR) departed Milford Haven for Plymouth. She was towed by HMRT Marauder (T/Lt. F. Jennings, RNR). (49)
5 Dec 1943 Around 1700 A/5, HMS Umbra (Lt. A.G. Chandler, RNR) arrived at Plymouth under tow from Milford Haven. (49)
7 Dec 1943 At 0945 A/7, HMS Umbra (Lt. A.G. Chandler, RNR) departed Plymouth for Portsmouth. Again she was towed by HMRT Marauder (T/Lt. F. Jennings, RNR). (49)

8 Dec 1943 Around 1500 A/8, HMS Umbra (Lt. A.G. Chandler, RNR) arrived at Portsmouth (Gosport / Fort Blockhouse) for full repairs to both main engines. For these repairs to be effected both engines were removed from the submarine. (49)
